entries.content: 3-5. When ... are turned back-- It is the result of God's power alone. He, as a righteous Judge (Psa 7:11), vindicates His people. He rebukes by acts as well as words (Psa 6:1; 18:15), and so effectually as to destroy the names of nations as well as persons.

entries.title: Psalms 18:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Literally, "As to the enemy finished are his ruins for ever. Thou [God] hast destroyed," &c. (1Sa 15:3, 7; 27:8, 9). The wicked are utterly undone. Their ruins shall never be repaired.

entries.title: Psalms 18:21–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. God's eternal possession of a throne of justice is contrasted with the ruin of the wicked.

entries.title: Psalms 18:23–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. The oppressed, and all who know Him (Psa 5:3; 7:1), find Him a sure refuge.

entries.title: Psalms 18:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. (Compare Psa 2:6; 3:4).

entries.title: Psalms 18:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. for blood-- that is, murders (Psa 5:6), including all the oppressions of His people. maketh inquisition-- (compare Ge 9:5). He will avenge their cause.

entries.title: Psalms 18:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. gates-- or, "regions." of death-- Gates being the entrance is put for the bounds.

entries.title: Psalms 18:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. gates ... Zion-- The enclosure of the city (compare Psa 48:12; Is 23:12), or, church, as denoted by this phrase contrasted with that of death, carries out the idea of exaltation as well as deliverance. Signal favors should lead us to render signal and public thanks.

entries.title: Psalms 18:29–30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-16. The undesigned results of the devices of the wicked prove them to be of God's overruling or ordering, especially when those results are destructive to the wicked themselves.

entries.title: Psalms 18:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. shall be turned-- or, "shall turn," retreating under God's vengeance, and driven by Him to the extreme of destruction, even hell itself. Those who forget God are classed with the depraved and openly profane.

entries.title: Psalms 18:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. (Compare Psa 13:1-6). the needy-- literally, "poor," as deprived of anything; hence miserable. expectation of the poor-- or, "meek," "humble," made so by affliction.

entries.title: Psalms 18:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. Arise-- (compare Psa 4:7). let not man-- (Psa 8:4). let ... be judged-- and of course condemned.

entries.title: Psalms 18:34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. By their effectual subjection, make them to realize their frail nature (Psa 8:4), and deter them from all conceit and future rebellion.

entries.title: Psalms 18:36
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 10 Psa 10:1-18. The Psalmist mourns God's apparent indifference to his troubles, which are aggravated by the successful malice, blasphemy, pride, deceit, and profanity of the wicked. On the just and discriminating providence of God he relies for the destruction of their false security, and the defense of the needy. 1. These are, of course, figurative terms (compare Psa 7:6; 13:1, &c.). hidest-- Supply "thine eyes" or "face."

entries.title: Psalms 18:37
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. Literally, "In pride of the wicked they (the poor or humble, Psa 10:17; 12:5) shall be taken in the devices they (the proud) have imagined."

entries.title: Psalms 18:38
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. heart's-- or, "soul's." desire-- that is, his success in evil. and blesseth, &c.-- he (the wicked) blesseth the covetous, he despiseth the Lord.

entries.title: Psalms 18:39
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. The face expresses the self-conceit, whose fruit is practical atheism (Psa 14:1).

entries.title: Psalms 18:40–41
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. Such is his confidence in the permanence of his way or course of life, that he disregards God's providential government (out of sight, because he will not look, Is 26:11), sneers at his enemies, and boasts perpetual freedom from evil.

entries.title: Psalms 18:42–45
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-10. The malignity and deceit (Psa 140:3) of such are followed by acts combining cunning, fraud, and violence (compare Pr 1:11, 18), aptly illustrated by the habits of the lion, and of hunters taking their prey. "Poor," in Psa 10:8, 10, 14, represents a word peculiar to this Psalm, meaning the sad or sorrowful; in Psa 10:9, as usual, it means the pious or meek sufferer.

entries.title: Psalms 18:46
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. As before, such conduct implies disbelief or disregard of God's government.

entries.title: Psalms 18:47
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. (Compare Psa 9:19; 3:7). the humble-- (Compare Psa 10:17, and Margin.) lift up thine hand-- exert thy power.

entries.title: Psalms 18:48–49
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-14. It is in vain to suppose God will overlook sin, however forbearing; for He carefully examines or beholds all wickedness, and will mark it by His providential (Thine hand) punishment.

entries.title: Psalms 18:50
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. arm-- power. till thou find none-- So far from not requiting (Psa 10:11, 13), God will utterly destroy the wicked and his deeds (Psa 9:5, 6; 34:16; 37:36).

entries.title: Psalms 19 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-18. God reigns. The wicked, if for a time successful, shall be cut off. He hears and confirms the hearts of His suffering people (Psa 112:7), executes justice for the feeble, and represses the pride and violence of conceited, though frail, men (compare Psa 9:16).

entries.title: Psalms 19:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 11 Psa 11:1-7. On title, see Introduction. Alluding to some event in his history, as in 1Sa 23:13, the Psalmist avows his confidence in God, when admonished to flee from his raging persecutors, whose destruction of the usual foundations of safety rendered all his efforts useless. The grounds of his confidence are God's supreme dominion, His watchful care of His people, His hatred to the wicked and judgments on them, and His love for righteousness and the righteous. 1. my soul-- me (Psa 3:2). Flee-- literally, "flee ye"; that is, he and his companion. as a bird to your mountain-- having as such no safety but in flight (compare 1Sa 26:20; La 3:52).

entries.title: Psalms 19:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. privily-- literally, "in darkness," treacherously.

entries.title: Psalms 19:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Literally, "The foundations (that is, of good order and law) will be destroyed, what has the righteous done (to sustain them)?" All his efforts have failed.

entries.title: Psalms 19:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. temple ... heaven-- The connection seems to denote God's heavenly residence; the term used is taken from the place of His visible earthly abode (Psa 2:6; 3:4; 5:7). Thence He inspects men with close scrutiny.

entries.title: Psalms 19:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. The trial of the righteous results in their approval, as it is contrasted with God's hatred to the wicked.

entries.title: Psalms 19:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Their punishment is described by vivid figures denoting abundant, sudden, furious, and utter destruction (compare Ge 19:24; Job 18:15; Psa 7:15; 9:15). cup-- is a frequent figure for God's favor or wrath (Psa 16:5; 23:5; Mt 20:22, 23).

entries.title: Psalms 19:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. his countenance-- literally, "their faces," a use of the plural applied to God, as in Ge 1:26; 3:22; 11:7; Is 6:8, &c., denoting the fulness of His perfections, or more probably originating in a reference to the trinity of persons. "Faces" is used as "eyes" (Psa 11:4), expressing here God's complacency towards the upright (compare Psa 34:15, 16).

entries.title: Psalms 19:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 12 Psa 12:1-8. On title, see Introduction and see on Psa 6:1. The Psalmist laments the decrease of good men. The pride and deceit of the wicked provokes God's wrath, whose promise to avenge the cause of pious sufferers will be verified even amidst prevailing iniquity. 1. the faithful-- or literally, "faithfulness" (Psa 31:23).

entries.title: Psalms 19:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The want of it is illustrated by the prevalence of deceit and instability.

entries.title: Psalms 19:14–20:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. Boasting (Da 7:25) is, like flattery, a species of lying. lips, and ... tongue-- for persons.

entries.title: Psalms 20:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. The writer intimates his confidence by depicting God's actions (compare Psa 9:19; 10:12) as coming to save the poor at whom the wicked sneer (Psa 10:5).

entries.title: Psalms 20:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. The words-- literally, "saying of" (Psa 12:5). seven times-- thoroughly (Da 3:19).

entries.title: Psalms 20:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. them-- (Margin.)

entries.title: Psalms 20:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. The wicked roam undisturbed doing evil, when vileness and vile men are exalted.

entries.title: Psalms 20:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 13 Psa 13:1-6. On title, see Introduction. The Psalmist, mourning God's absence and the triumph of his enemies, prays for relief before he is totally destroyed, and is encouraged to hope his trust will not be in vain. 1. The forms of expression and figure here used are frequent (compare Psa 9:12, 18; 10:11, 12). How long ... for ever-- Shall it be for ever?

entries.title: Psalms 20:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The counsels or devices of his heart afford no relief.

entries.title: Psalms 20:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. lighten mine eyes-- dim with weakness, denoting approaching death (compare 1Sa 14:27-29; Psa 6:7; 38:10).

entries.title: Psalms 20:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. rejoice-- literally, "shout as in triumph." I am moved-- cast down from a firm position (Psa 10:6).

entries.title: Psalms 21 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. Trust is followed by rejoicing in the deliverance which God effects, and, instead of his enemy, he can lift the song of triumph.

entries.title: Psalms 21:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 14 Psa 14:1-7. The practical atheism and total and universal depravity of the wicked, with their hatred to the good, are set forth. Yet, as they dread God's judgments when He vindicates His people, the Psalmist prays for His delivering power. 1. Sinners are termed "fools," because they think and act contrary to right reason (Ge 34:7; Jos 7:15; Psa 39:8; 74:18, 22). in his heart-- to himself (Ge 6:12).

entries.title: Psalms 21:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. looked-- in earnest enquiry. understand-- as opposed to "fool" [Psa 14:1].

entries.title: Psalms 21:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. filthy-- literally, "spoiled," or, "soured," "corrupted" (Job 15:16; Ro 3:12).

entries.title: Psalms 21:6–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-6. Their conduct evinces indifference rather than ignorance of God; for when He appears in judgment, they are stricken with great fear. who eat up my people-- to express their beastly fury (Pr 30:14; Ha 3:14). To "call on the Lord" is to worship Him.

entries.title: Psalms 21:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. captivity-- denotes any great evil. Zion-- God's abode, from which He revealed His purposes of mercy, as He now does by the Church (compare Psa 3:4; 20:2), and which He rules and in which He does all other things for the good of His people (Ep 1:22).

entries.title: Psalms 21:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 15 Psa 15:1-5. Those who are fit for communion with God may be known by a conformity to His law, which is illustrated in various important particulars. 1. abide-- or, "sojourn" (compare Psa 5:4), where it means under God's protection here, as (Psa 23:6, 27:4, 6) communion. tabernacle-- seat of the ark (2Sa 6:17), the symbol of God's presence. holy hill-- (Compare Psa 2:6).

entries.title: Psalms 21:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. walketh-- (Compare Psa 1:1). uprightly-- in a complete manner, as to all parts of conduct (Ge 17:1), not as to degree. worketh-- or, "does." righteousness-- what is right. in his heart-- sincerely (Pr 23:7).

entries.title: Psalms 21:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. He neither slanders nor spreads slander.

entries.title: Psalms 22 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Love and hate are regulated by a regard to God. sweareth ... hurt-- or what so results (compare Le 5:4).

entries.title: Psalms 22:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. (Compare Le 25:37; De 23:19, 20). usury is derived from a verb meaning "to bite." All gains made by the wrongful loss of others are forbidden. taketh reward, &c.-- The innocent would not otherwise be condemned (compare Ex 23:8; De 16:19). Bribery of all sorts is denounced. doeth these, &c.-- Such persons admitted to God's presence and favor shall never be moved (Psa 10:6; 13:5).

entries.title: Psalms 22:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 16 Psa 16:1-11. Michtam, or, by the change of one letter, Michtab--a "writing," such as a poem or song (compare Is 38:9). Such a change of the letter m for b was not unusual. The position of this word in connection with the author's name, being that usually occupied by some term, such as Psalm or song, denoting the style or matter of the composition, favors this view of its meaning, though we know not why this and Psalms 56-60 should be specially, called "a writing." "A golden (Psalm)," or "a memorial" are explanations proposed by some-- neither of which, however applicable here, appears adapted to the other Psalms where the term occurs. According to Peter (Ac 2:25) and Paul (Ac 13:35), this Psalm relates to Christ and expresses the feelings of His human nature, in view of His sufferings and victory over death and the grave, including His subsequent exaltation at the right hand of God. Such was the exposition of the best earlier Christian interpreters. Some moderns have held that the Psalm relates exclusively to David; but this view is expressly contradicted by the apostles; others hold that the language of the Psalm is applicable to David as a type of Christ, capable of the higher sense assigned it in the New Testament. But then the language of Psa 16:10 cannot be used of David in any sense, for "he saw corruption." Others again propose to refer the first part to David, and the last to Christ; but it is evident that no change in the subject of the Psalm is indicated. Indeed, the person who appeals to God for help is evidently the same who rejoices in having found it. In referring the whole Psalm to Christ, it is, however, by no means denied that much of its language is expressive of the feelings of His people, so far as in their humble measure they have the feelings of trust in God expressed by Him, their head and representative. Such use of His language, as recorded in His last prayer (Joh 17:1-26), and even that which He used in Gethsemane, under similar modifications, is equally proper. The propriety of this reference of the Psalm to Christ will appear in the scope and interpretation. In view of the sufferings before Him, the Saviour, with that instinctive dread of death manifested in Gethsemane, calls on God to "preserve" Him; He avows His delight in holiness and abhorrence of the wicked and their wickedness; and for "the joy that was set before Him, despising the shame" [He 12:2], encourages Himself; contemplating the glories of the heritage appointed Him. Thus even death and the grave lose their terrors in the assurance of the victory to be attained and "the glory that should follow" [1Pe 1:11]. 1. Preserve me, &c.-- keep or watch over my interests. in thee ... I ... trust-- as one seeking shelter from pressing danger.

entries.title: Psalms 22:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. my soul-- must be supplied; expressed in similar cases (Psa 42:5, 11). my goodness ... thee-- This obscure passage is variously expounded. Either one of two expositions falls in with the context. "My goodness" or merit is not on account of Thee--that is, is not for Thy benefit. Then follows the contrast of Psa 16:3 (but is), in respect, or for the saints, &c.--that is, it enures to them. Or, my goodness--or happiness is not besides Thee--that is, without Thee I have no other source of happiness. Then, "to the saints," &c., means that the same privilege of deriving happiness from God only is theirs. The first is the most consonant with the Messianic character of the Psalm, though the latter is not inconsistent with it.

entries.title: Psalms 22:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. saints-- or, persons consecrated to God, set apart from others to His service. in the earth-- that is, land of Palestine, the residence of God's chosen people--figuratively for the Church. excellent-- or, "nobles," distinguished for moral excellence.

entries.title: Psalms 22:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. He expresses his abhorrence of those who seek other sources of happiness or objects of worship, and, by characterizing their rites by drink offerings of blood, clearly denotes idolaters. The word for "sorrows" is by some rendered "idols"; but, though a similar word to that for idols, it is not the same. In selecting such a term, there may be an allusion, by the author, to the sorrows produced by idolatrous practices.

entries.title: Psalms 22:7–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-7. God is the chief good, and supplies all need (De 10:9). portion of mine inheritance and of my cup-- may contain an allusion to the daily supply of food, and also to the inheritance of Levi (De 18:1, 2). maintainest-- or, drawest out my lot--enlargest it. Psa 16:7 carries out this idea more fully.

entries.title: Psalms 22:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. With God's presence and aid he is sure of safety (Psa 10:6; 15:5; Joh 12:27, 28; He 5:7, 8).

entries.title: Psalms 22:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. glory-- as heart (Psa 7:5), for self. In Ac 2:26, after the Septuagint, "my tongue" as "the glory of the frame"--the instrument for praising God. flesh-- If taken as opposed to soul (Psa 16:10), it may mean the body; otherwise, the whole person (compare Psa 63:1; 84:2). rest in hope-- (compare Margin).

entries.title: Psalms 22:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. soul-- or, "self." This use of "soul" for the person is frequent (Ge 12:5; 46:26; Psa 3:2; 7:2; 11:1), even when the body may be the part chiefly affected, as in Psa 35:13; 105:18. Some cases are cited, as Le 22:4; Nu 6:6; 9:6, 10; 19:13; Hag 2:13, &c., which seem to justify assigning the meaning of body, or dead body; but it will be found that the latter sense is given by some adjunct expressed or implied. In those cases person is the proper sense. wilt not leave ... hell-- abandon to the power of (Job 39:14; Psa 49:10). Hell as (Ge 42:38; Psa 6:5; Jon 2:2) the state or region of death, and so frequently--or the grave itself (Job 14:13; 17:13; Ec 9:10, &c.). So the Greek Hades (compare Ac 2:27, 31). The context alone can settle whether the state mentioned is one of suffering and place of the damned (compare Psa 9:17; Pr 5:5; 7:27). wilt ... suffer-- literally, "give" or "appoint." Holy One-- (Psa 4:3), one who is the object of God's favor, and so a recipient of divine grace which he exhibits--pious. to see-- or, "experience"--undergo (Lu 2:26). corruption-- Some render the word, the pit, which is possible, but for the obvious sense which the apostle's exposition (Ac 2:27; 13:36, 37) gives. The sense of the whole passage is clearly this: by the use of flesh and soul, the disembodied state produced by death is indicated; but, on the other hand, no more than the state of death is intended; for the last clause of Psa 16:10 is strictly parallel with the first, and Holy One corresponds to soul, and corruption to hell. As Holy One, or David (Ac 13:36, 37), which denotes the person, including soul and body, is used for body, of which only corruption can be predicated (compare Ac 2:31); so, on the contrary, soul, which literally means the immaterial part, is used for the person. The language may be thus paraphrased, "In death I shall hope for resurrection; for I shall not be left under its dominion and within its bounds, or be subject to the corruption which ordinarily ensues."

entries.title: Psalms 22:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. Raised from the dead, he shall die no more; death hath no more dominion over him. Thou wilt show me-- guide me to attain. the path of life-- or, "lives"--the plural denoting variety and abundance--immortal blessedness of every sort--as "life" often denotes. in thy presence-- or, "before Thy faces." The frequent use of this plural form for "faces" may contain an allusion to the Trinity (Nu 6:25, 26; Psa 17:15; 31:16). at thy right hand-- to which Christ was exalted (Psa 110:1; Ac 2:33; Col 3:1; He 1:3). In the glories of this state, He shall see of the travail (Is 53:10, 11; Php 2:9) of His soul, and be satisfied.

entries.title: Psalms 22:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 17 Psa 17:1-15. This Psalm is termed a prayer because the language of petition is predominant. With a just cause, sincerely presented, the writer prays for a just decision and help and protection. Pleading former mercies as a ground of hope, he urges his prayer in view of the malice, pride, rapacity, and selfishness of his foes, whose character is contrasted with his pious devotion and delight in God's favor. 2. sentence-- acquitting judgment. from thy presence-- Thy tribunal. things that are equal-- just and right, do Thou regard.

entries.title: Psalms 22:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. proved ... visited ... tried-- His character was most rigidly tested, at all times, and by all methods, affliction and others (Psa 7:10). purposed that, &c.-- or, my mouth does not exceed my purpose; I am sincere.

entries.title: Psalms 22:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. works of men-- sinful practices. by the word of thy lips-- as a guide (Psa 119:9, 11, 95). destroyer-- violent man.

entries.title: Psalms 22:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. May be read as an assertion "my steps or goings have held on to Thy paths."

entries.title: Psalms 22:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. wilt hear me-- that is, graciously (Psa 3:4).

entries.title: Psalms 22:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. Show-- set apart as special and eminent (Ex 8:18; Psa 4:3). thy right hand-- for Thy power.

entries.title: Psalms 22:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Similar figures, denoting the preciousness of God's people in His sight, in De 32:10, 11; Mt 23:37.

entries.title: Psalms 22:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. compass me-- (compare Psa 118:10-12).

entries.title: Psalms 22:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. enclosed ... fat-- are become proud in prosperity, and insolent to God (De 32:15; Psa 73:7).

entries.title: Psalms 22:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. They pursue us as beasts tracking their prey.

entries.title: Psalms 22:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. The figure made more special by that of a lion lurking.

entries.title: Psalms 22:27–29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-15. disappoint-- literally, "come before," or, "encounter him." Supply "with" before "sword" (Psa 17:13), and "hand" (Psa 17:14). These denote God's power.

entries.title: Psalms 22:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 18 Psa 18:1-50. "The servant of the Lord," which in the Hebrew precedes "David," is a significant part of the title (and not a mere epithet of David), denoting the inspired character of the song, as the production of one entrusted with the execution of God's will. He was not favored by God because he served Him, but served Him because selected and appointed by God in His sovereign mercy. After a general expression of praise and confidence in God for the future, David gives a sublimely poetical description of God's deliverance, which he characterizes as an illustration of God's justice to the innocent and His righteous government. His own prowess and success are celebrated as the results of divine aid, and, confident of its continuance, he closes in terms of triumphant praise. 2Sa 22:1-51 is a copy of this Psalm, with a few unimportant variations recorded there as a part of the history, and repeated here as part of a collection designed for permanent use. 1. I will love thee-- with most tender affection.

entries.title: Psalms 23 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-3. The various terms used describe God as an object of the most implicit and reliable trust. rock-- literally, "a cleft rock," for concealment. strength-- a firm, immovable rock. horn of my salvation-- The horn, as the means of attack or defense of some of the strongest animals, is a frequent emblem of power or strength efficiently exercised (compare De 33:17; Lu 1:69). tower-- literally, "high place," beyond reach of danger.

entries.title: Psalms 23:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. sorrows-- literally, "bands as of a net" (Psa 116:3). floods-- denotes "multitude."

entries.title: Psalms 23:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. death-- and hell (compare Psa 16:10) are personified as man's great enemies (compare Re 20:13, 14). prevented-- encountered me, crossed my path, and endangered my safety. He does not mean he was in their power.

entries.title: Psalms 23:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. He relates his methods to procure relief when distressed, and his success. temple-- (Compare Psa 11:4).

entries.title: Psalms 23:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. God's coming described in figures drawn from His appearance on Sinai (compare De 32:22).

entries.title: Psalms 24 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. darkness-- or, a dense cloud (Ex 19:16; De 5:22).

entries.title: Psalms 24:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. cherub-- angelic agents (compare Ge 3:24), the figures of which were placed over the ark (1Sa 4:4), representing God's dwelling; used here to enhance the majesty of the divine advent. Angels and winds may represent all rational and irrational agencies of God's providence (compare Psa 104:3, 4). did fly-- Rapidity of motion adds to the grandeur of the scene.

entries.title: Psalms 24:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. dark waters-- or, clouds heavy with vapor.

entries.title: Psalms 24:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. Out of this obscurity, which impresses the beholder with awe and dread, He reveals Himself by sudden light and the means of His terrible wrath (Jos 10:11; Psa 78:47).

entries.title: Psalms 24:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. The storm breaks forth-- thunder follows lightning, and hail with repeated lightning, as often seen, like balls or coals of fire, succeed (Ex 9:23).

entries.title: Psalms 24:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. The fiery brightness of lightning, in shape like burning arrows rapidly shot through the air, well represents the most terrible part of an awful storm. Before the terrors of such a scene the enemies are confounded and overthrown in dismay.

entries.title: Psalms 24:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. The tempest of the air is attended by appropriate results on earth. The language, though not expressive of any special physical changes, represents the utter subversion of the order of nature. Before such a God none can stand.

entries.title: Psalms 24:7–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-19. from above-- As seated on a throne, directing these terrible scenes, God-- sent-- His hand (Psa 144:7), reached down to His humble worshipper, and delivered him. many waters-- calamities (Job 30:14; Psa 124:4, 5).

entries.title: Psalms 25 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20-24. The statements of innocence, righteousness, &c., refer, doubtless, to his personal and official conduct and his purposes, during all the trials to which he was subjected in Saul's persecutions and Absalom's rebellions, as well as the various wars in which he had been engaged as the head and defender of God's Church and people.

entries.title: Psalms 25:5–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25-27. God renders to men according to their deeds in a penal, not vindictive, sense (Le 26:23, 24). merciful-- or, "kind" (Psa 4:3).

entries.title: Psalms 25:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28. To give one light is to make prosperous (Job 18:5, 6; 21:17). thou-- is emphatic, as if to say, I can fully confide in Thee for help.

entries.title: Psalms 25:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 29. And this on past experience in his military life, set forth by these figures.

entries.title: Psalms 25:10–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 30-32. God's perfection is the source of his own, which has resulted from his trust on the one hand, and God's promised help on the other. tried-- "as metals are tried by fire and proved genuine" (Psa 12:6). Shield (Psa 3:3). Girding was essential to free motion on account of the looseness of Oriental dresses; hence it is an expressive figure for describing the gift of strength.

entries.title: Psalms 25:13–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 33-36. God's help farther described. He gives swiftness to pursue or elude his enemies (Ha 3:19), strength, protection, and a firm footing.

entries.title: Psalms 25:17–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 37-41. In actual conflict, with God's aid, the defeat of his enemies is certain. A present and continued success is expressed.

entries.title: Psalms 25:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 42. This conquest was complete.

entries.title: Psalms 26 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 43-45. Not only does He conquer civil foes, but foreigners, who are driven from their places of refuge.

entries.title: Psalms 26:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 46. The Lord liveth-- contrasts Him with idols (1Co 8:4).

entries.title: Psalms 26:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 47-48. avengeth me-- His cause is espoused by God as His own.

entries.title: Psalms 26:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 49-50. Paul (Ro 15:9) quotes from this doxology to show that under the Old Testament economy, others than the Jews were regarded as subjects of that spiritual government of which David was head, and in which character his deliverances and victories were typical of the more illustrious triumphs of David's greater Son. The language of Psa 18:50 justifies this view in its distinct allusion to the great promise (compare 2Sa 7:12). In all David's successes he saw the pledges of a fulfilment of that promise, and he mourned in all his adversities, not only in view of his personal suffering, but because he saw in them evidences of danger to the great interests which were committed to his keeping. It is in these aspects of his character that we are led properly to appreciate the importance attached to his sorrows and sufferings, his joys and successes.

entries.title: Psalms 26:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 19 Psa 19:1-14. After exhibiting the harmonious revelation of God's perfections made by His works and His word, the Psalmist prays for conformity to the Divine teaching. 1. the glory of God-- is the sum of His perfections (Psa 24:7-10; Ro 1:20). firmament-- another word for "heavens" (Ge 1:8). handywork-- old English for "work of His hands."

entries.title: Psalms 26:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. uttereth-- pours forth as a stream; a perpetual testimony.

entries.title: Psalms 26:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Though there is no articulate speech or words, yet without these their voice is heard (compare Margin).

entries.title: Psalms 26:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Their line-- or, "instruction"--the influence exerted by their tacit display of God's perfections. Paul (Ro 10:18), quoting from the Septuagint, uses "sound," which gives the same sense.

entries.title: Psalms 27 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. The sun, as the most glorious heavenly body, is specially used to illustrate the sentiment; and his vigorous, cheerful, daily, and extensive course, and his reviving heat (including light), well display the wondrous wisdom of his Maker.

entries.title: Psalms 27:2–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-9. The law is described by six names, epithets, and effects. It is a rule, God's testimony for the truth, His special and general prescription of duty, fear (as its cause) and judicial decision. It is distinct and certain, reliable, right, pure, holy, and true. Hence it revives those depressed by doubts, makes wise the unskilled (2Ti 3:15), rejoices the lover of truth, strengthens the desponding (Psa 13:4; 34:6), provides permanent principles of conduct, and by God's grace brings a rich reward.

entries.title: Psalms 27:7–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-14. The clearer our view of the law, the more manifest are our sins. Still for its full effect we need divine grace to show us our faults, acquit us, restrain us from the practice, and free us from the power, of sin. Thus only can our conduct be blameless, and our words and thoughts acceptable to God.

entries.title: Psalms 27:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 20 Psa 20:1-9. David probably composed this Psalm to express the prayers of the pious for his success as at once the head of the Church and nation. Like other compositions of which David in such relations is the subject, its sentiments have a permanent value--the prosperity of Christ's kingdom being involved, as well as typified, in that of Israel and its king. 1. hear thee-- graciously (Psa 4:1). name of-- or manifested perfections, as power, wisdom, &c. defend thee-- set thee on high from danger (Psa 9:9; 18:3).

entries.title: Psalms 27:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. strengthen thee-- sustain in conflict; even physical benefits may be included, as courage for war, &c., as such may proceed from a sense of divine favor, secured in the use of spiritual privileges.

entries.title: Psalms 27:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. all thy offerings-- or gifts, vegetable offerings. accept-- literally, "turn to ashes" (compare 1Ki 18:38). Selah-- (See on Psa 3:2).

entries.title: Psalms 27:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. thy counsel-- or plan.

entries.title: Psalms 28 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. salvation-- that wrought and experienced by him. set up our banners-- (Nu 2:3, 10). In usual sense, or, as some render, "may we be made great."

entries.title: Psalms 28:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. He speaks as if suddenly assured of a hearing. his anointed-- not only David personally, but as the specially appointed head of His Church. his holy heaven-- or, literally, "the heavens of His holiness," where He resides (Psa 2:6; 11:4). saving ... hand-- His power which brings salvation.

entries.title: Psalms 28:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. remember-- or cause to remember, mention thankfully (1Sa 17:45; Psa 33:16).

entries.title: Psalms 28:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. They-- that is, who trust in horses, &c. stand upright-- literally, "we have straightened ourselves up from our distress and fears."

entries.title: Psalms 28:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. let the king hear-- as God's representative, delivered to deliver. Perhaps a better sense is, "Lord, save the king; hear us when we call," or pray.

entries.title: Psalms 28:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 21 Psa 21:1-13. The pious are led by the Psalmist to celebrate God's favor to the king in the already conferred and in prospective victories. The doxology added may relate to both Psalms; the preceding of petition, chiefly this of thanksgiving, ascribing honor to God for His display of grace and power to His Church in all ages, not only under David, but also under his last greatest successor, "the King of the Jews." 1. thy strength ... thy salvation-- as supplied by Thee.

entries.title: Psalms 28:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The sentiment affirmed in the first clause is reaffirmed by the negation of its opposite in the second.

entries.title: Psalms 28:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. preventest-- literally, "to meet here in good sense," or "friendship" (Psa 59:10; compare opposite, Psa 17:13). blessings of goodness-- which confer happiness. crown of pure gold-- a figure for the highest royal prosperity.

entries.title: Psalms 28:9–29:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-6. (Compare 2Sa 7:13-16). The glory and blessedness of the king as head of his line, including Christ, as well as in being God's specially selected servant, exceeded that of all others.

entries.title: Psalms 29:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. The mediate cause is the king's faith, the efficient, God's mercy.

entries.title: Psalms 29:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. The address is now made to the king. hand-- denotes power, and right hand-- a more active and efficient degree of its exercise. find out-- reach, lay hold of, indicating success in pursuit of his enemies.

entries.title: Psalms 29:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. The king is only God's agent. anger-- literally, "face," as appearing against them. as a fiery oven-- as in it.

entries.title: Psalms 29:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. fruit-- children (Psa 37:25; Ho 9:16).

entries.title: Psalms 29:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. This terrible overthrow, reaching to posterity, is due to their crimes (Ex 20:5, 6).

entries.title: Psalms 29:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. turn their back-- literally, "place them [as to the] shoulder." against the face of them-- The shooting against their faces would cause them to turn their backs in flight.

entries.title: Psalms 29:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. The glory of all is ascribable to God alone.

entries.title: Psalms 29:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 22 Psa 22:1-31. The obscure words Aijeleth Shahar in this title have various explanations. Most interpreters agree in translating them by "hind of the morning." But great difference exists as to the meaning of these words. By some they are supposed (compare Psa 9:1) to be the name of the tune to which the words of the Psalm were set; by others, the name of a musical instrument. Perhaps the best view is to regard the phrase as enigmatically expressive of the subject--the sufferer being likened to a hind pursued by hunters in the early morning (literally, "the dawn of day")--or that, while hind suggests the idea of a meek, innocent sufferer, the addition of morning denotes relief obtained. The feelings of a pious sufferer in sorrow and deliverance are vividly portrayed. He earnestly pleads for divine aid on the ground of his relation to God, whose past goodness to His people encourages hope, and then on account of the imminent danger by which he is threatened. The language of complaint is turned to that of rejoicing in the assured prospect of relief from suffering and triumph over his enemies. The use of the words of the first clause of Psa 22:1 by our Saviour on the cross, and the quotation of Psa 22:18 by John (Joh 19:24), and of Psa 22:22 by Paul (He 2:12), as fulfilled in His history, clearly intimate the prophetical and Messianic purport of the Psalm. The intensity of the grief, and the completeness and glory of the deliverance and triumph, alike appear to be unsuitable representations of the fortunes of any less personage. In a general and modified sense (see on Psa 16:1), the experience here detailed may be adapted to the case of all Christians suffering from spiritual foes, and delivered by divine aid, inasmuch as Christ in His human nature was their head and representative. 1. A summary of the complaint. Desertion by God, when overwhelmed by distress, is the climax of the sufferer's misery. words of my roaring-- shows that the complaint is expressed intelligently, though the term "roaring" is figurative, taken from the conduct of irrational creatures in pain.

entries.title: Psalms 29:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The long distress is evinced by-- am not silent-- literally, "not silence to me," either meaning, I continually cry; or, corresponding with "thou hearest not," or answerest not, it may mean, there is no rest or quiet to me.

entries.title: Psalms 30 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Still he not only refrains from charging God foolishly, but evinces his confidence in God by appealing to Him. thou art holy-- or possessed of all the attributes which encourage trust, and the right object of the praises of the Church: hence the sufferer need not despair.

entries.title: Psalms 30:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. Past experience of God's people is a ground of trust. The mention of "our fathers" does not destroy the applicability of the words as the language of our Saviour's human nature.

entries.title: Psalms 30:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. He who was despised and rejected of His own people, as a disgrace to the nation, might well use these words of deep abasement, which express not His real, but esteemed, value.

entries.title: Psalms 30:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. For the Jews used one of the gestures (Mt 27:39) here mentioned, when taunting Him on the cross, and (Mt 27:43) reproached Him almost in the very, language of this passage. shoot out-- or, "open." the lip-- (Compare Psa 35:21).

entries.title: Psalms 30:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. Though ironically spoken, the exhortation to trust was well founded on his previous experience of divine aid, the special illustration of which is drawn from the period of helpless infancy. didst make me hope-- literally, "made me secure."

entries.title: Psalms 30:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. From this statement of reasons for the appeal, he renews it, pleading his double extremity, the nearness of trouble, and the absence of a helper.

entries.title: Psalms 30:9–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-13. His enemies, with the vigor of bulls and rapacity of lions, surround him, eagerly seeking his ruin. The force of both figures is greater without the use of any particle denoting comparison.

entries.title: Psalms 30:11–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. Utter exhaustion and hopeless weakness, in these circumstances of pressing danger, are set forth by the most expressive figures; the solidity of the body is destroyed, and it becomes like water; the bones are parted; the heart, the very seat of vitality, melts like wax; all the juices of the system are dried up; the tongue can no longer perform its office, but lies parched and stiffened (compare Ge 49:4; 2Sa 14:14; Psa 58:8). In this, God is regarded as the ultimate source, and men as the instruments.

entries.title: Psalms 31 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. Evildoers are well described as dogs, which, in the East, herding together, wild and rapacious, are justly objects of great abhorrence. The last clause has been a subject of much discussion (involving questions as to the genuineness of the Hebrew word translated "pierce)" which cannot be made intelligible to the English reader. Though not quoted in the New Testament, the remarkable aptness of the description to the facts of the Saviour's history, together with difficulties attending any other mode of explaining the clause in the Hebrew, justify an adherence to the terms of our version and their obvious meaning.

entries.title: Psalms 31:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. His emaciated frame, itself an item of his misery, is rendered more so as the object of delighted contemplation to his enemies. The verbs, "look" and "stare," often occur as suggestive of feelings of satisfaction (compare Psa 27:13; 54:7; 118:7).

entries.title: Psalms 31:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. This literally fulfilled prediction closes the sad picture of the exposed and deserted sufferer.

entries.title: Psalms 31:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-20. He now turns with unabated desire and trust to God, who, in His strength and faithfulness, is contrasted with the urgent dangers described.

entries.title: Psalms 31:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. Deliverance pleaded in view of former help, when in the most imminent danger, from the most powerful enemy, represented by the unicorn or wild buffalo. the lion's mouth-- (Compare Psa 22:13). The lion often used as a figure representing violent enemies; the connecting of the mouth intimates their rapacity.

entries.title: Psalms 31:6–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22-24. He declares his purpose to celebrate God's gracious dealings and publish His manifested perfections ("name," Psa 5:11), &c., and forthwith he invites the pious (those who have a reverential fear of God) to unite in special praise for a deliverance, illustrating God's kind regard for the lowly, whom men neglect [Psa 22:24]. To hide the face (or eyes) expresses a studied neglect of one's cause, and refusal of aid or sympathy (compare Psa 30:7; Is 1:15).

entries.title: Psalms 31:9–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25-26. My praise shall be of thee-- or, perhaps better, "from thee," that is, God gives grace to praise Him. With offering praise, he further evinces his gratitude by promising the payment of his vows, in celebrating the usual festival, as provided in the law (De 12:18; 16:11), of which the pious or humble, and they that seek the Lord (His true worshippers) shall partake abundantly, and join him in praise [Psa 22:26]. In the enthusiasm produced by his lively feelings, he addresses such in words, assuring them of God's perpetual favor [Psa 22:26]. The dying of the heart denotes death (1Sa 25:37); so its living denotes life.

entries.title: Psalms 31:11–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27-31. His case illustrates God's righteous government. Beyond the existing time and people, others shall be brought to acknowledge and worship God; the fat ones, or the rich as well as the poor, the helpless who cannot keep themselves alive, shall together unite in celebrating God's delivering power, and transmit to unborn people the records of His grace.

entries.title: Psalms 31:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 23 Psa 23:1-6. Under a metaphor borrowed from scenes of pastoral life, with which David was familiar, he describes God's providential care in providing refreshment, guidance, protection, and abundance, and so affording grounds of confidence in His perpetual favor. 1. Christ's relation to His people is often represented by the figure of a shepherd (Joh 10:14; He 13:20; 1Pe 2:25; 5:4), and therefore the opinion that He is the Lord here so described, and in Ge 48:15; Psa 80:1; Is 40:11, is not without some good reason.

entries.title: Psalms 31:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. green pastures-- or, "pastures of tender grass," are mentioned, not in respect to food, but as places of cool and refreshing rest. the still waters-- are, literally, "waters of "stillness," whose quiet flow invites to repose. They are contrasted with boisterous streams on the one hand, and stagnant, offensive pools on the other.

entries.title: Psalms 31:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. To restore the soul is to revive or quicken it (Psa 19:7), or relieve it (La 1:11, 19). paths of righteousness-- those of safety, as directed by God, and pleasing to Him. for his name's sake-- or, regard for His perfections, pledged for His people's welfare.

entries.title: Psalms 31:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. In the darkest and most trying hour God is near. the valley of the shadow of death-- is a ravine overhung by high precipitous cliffs, filled with dense forests, and well calculated to inspire dread to the timid, and afford a covert to beasts of prey. While expressive of any great danger or cause of terror, it does not exclude the greatest of all, to which it is most popularly applied, and which its terms suggest. thy rod and thy staff-- are symbols of a shepherd's office. By them he guides his sheep.

entries.title: Psalms 31:21–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. Another figure expresses God's provided care. a table-- or, "food," anointing oil-- the symbol of gladness, and the overflowing cup-- which represents abundance--are prepared for the child of God, who may feast in spite of his enemies, confident that this favor will ever attend him. This beautiful Psalm most admirably sets before us, in its chief figure, that of a shepherd, the gentle, kind, and sure care extended to God's people, who, as a shepherd, both rules and feeds them. The closing verse shows that the blessings mentioned are spiritual.

entries.title: Psalms 31:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 24 Psa 24:1-10. God's supreme sovereignty requires a befitting holiness of life and heart in His worshippers; a sentiment sublimely illustrated by describing His entrance into the sanctuary, by the symbol of His worship--the ark, as requiring the most profound homage to the glory of His Majesty. 1. fulness-- everything. world-- the habitable globe, with they that dwell-- forming a parallel expression to the first clause.

entries.title: Psalms 32 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. Poetically represents the facts of Ge 1:9.

entries.title: Psalms 32:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. The form of a question gives vivacity. Hands, tongue, and heart are organs of action, speech, and feeling, which compose character. hill of the Lord-- (compare Psa 2:6, &c.). His Church--the true or invisible, as typified by the earthly sanctuary.

entries.title: Psalms 32:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. righteousness-- the rewards which God bestows on His people, or the grace to secure those rewards as well as the result.

entries.title: Psalms 32:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Jacob-- By "Jacob," we may understand God's people (compare Is 43:22; 44:2, &c.), corresponding to "the generation," as if he had said, "those who seek Thy face are Thy chosen people."

entries.title: Psalms 32:5–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-10. The entrance of the ark, with the attending procession, into the holy sanctuary is pictured to us. The repetition of the terms gives emphasis.

entries.title: Psalms 32:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 25 Psa 25:1-22. The general tone of this Psalm is that of prayer for help from enemies. Distress, however, exciting a sense of sin, humble confession, supplication for pardon, preservation from sin, and divine guidance, are prominent topics. 1. lift up my soul-- (Psa 24:4; 86:4), set my affections (compare Col 3:2).

entries.title: Psalms 32:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. not be ashamed-- by disappointment of hopes of relief.

entries.title: Psalms 33 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. The prayer generalized as to all who wait on God-- that is, who expect His favor. On the other hand, the disappointment of the perfidious, who, unprovoked, have done evil, is invoked (compare 2Sa 22:9).

entries.title: Psalms 33:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. On the ground of former favor, he invokes divine guidance, according to God's gracious ways of dealing and faithfulness.

entries.title: Psalms 33:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. Confessing past and present sins, he pleads for mercy, not on palliations of sin, but on God's well-known benevolence.

entries.title: Psalms 33:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. upright-- acting according to His promise. sinners-- the general term, limited by the meek-- who are penitent. the way-- and his way--God's way of providence.

entries.title: Psalms 33:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. paths-- similar sense--His modes of dealing (compare Psa 25:4). mercy and truth-- (Job 14:1-22), God's grace in promising and faithfulness in performing.

entries.title: Psalms 33:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. God's perfections of love, mercy, goodness, and truth are manifested (his name, compare Psa 9:10) in pardoning sin, and the greatness of sin renders pardon more needed.

entries.title: Psalms 33:9–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-13. What he asks for himself is the common lot of all the pious.

entries.title: Psalms 33:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. The reason of the blessing explained-- the pious enjoy communion with God (compare Pr 3:21, 12), and, of course, learn His gracious terms of pardon.

entries.title: Psalms 33:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. His trust in God is fixed. net-- is frequently used as a figure for dangers by enemies (Psa 9:15; 10:9).

entries.title: Psalms 33:13–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-19. A series of earnest appeals for aid because God had seemed to desert him (compare Psa 13:1; 17:13, &c.), his sins oppressed him, his enemies had enlarged his troubles and were multiplied, increasing in hate and violence (Psa 9:8; 18:48).

entries.title: Psalms 33:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. keep my soul-- (Psa 16:1). put my trust-- flee for refuge (Psa 2:12).

entries.title: Psalms 33:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. In conscious innocence of the faults charged by his enemies, he confidently commits his cause to God. Some refer-- integrity, &c.-- to God, meaning His covenant faithfulness. This sense, though good, is an unusual application of the terms.

entries.title: Psalms 33:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. Extend these blessings to all Thy people in all their distresses.

entries.title: Psalms 33:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 26 Psa 26:1-12. After appealing to God's judgment on his avowed integrity and innocence of the charges laid by his enemies, the Psalmist professes delight in God's worship, and prays for exemption from the fate of the wicked, expressing assurance of God's favor. 1. Judge-- decide on my case; the appeal of innocence. in mine integrity-- freedom from blemish (compare Psa 25:21). His confidence of perseverance results from trust in God's sustaining grace.

entries.title: Psalms 33:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. He asks the most careful scrutiny of his affections and thoughts (Psa 7:9), or motives.

entries.title: Psalms 34 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. As often, the ground of prayer for present help is former favor.

entries.title: Psalms 34:1–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-8. As exemplified by the fruits of divine grace, presented in his life, especially in his avoiding the wicked and his purposes of cleaving to God's worship.

entries.title: Psalms 34:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Gather not, &c.-- Bring me not to death. bloody men-- (compare Psa 5:6).

entries.title: Psalms 34:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Their whole conduct is that of violence and fraud.

entries.title: Psalms 34:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. But, &c.-- He contrasts his character and destiny with that of the wicked (compare Psa 26:1, 2).

entries.title: Psalms 34:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 27 Psa 27:1-14. With a general strain of confidence, hope, and joy, especially in God's worship, in the midst of dangers, the Psalmist introduces prayer for divine help and guidance. 1. light-- is a common figure for comfort. strength-- or, "stronghold"--affording security against all violence. The interrogations give greater vividness to the negation implied.

entries.title: Psalms 34:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. eat ... my flesh-- (Job 19:22; Psa 14:4). The allusion to wild beasts illustrates their rapacity. they stumbled-- "they" is emphatic; not I, but they were destroyed.

entries.title: Psalms 34:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. In the greatest dangers. in this-- that is, then, in such extremity.

entries.title: Psalms 34:14–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. The secret of his confidence is his delight in communion with God (Psa 16:11; 23:6), beholding the harmony of His perfections, and seeking His favor in His temple or palace; a term applicable to the tabernacle (compare Psa 5:7). There he is safe (Psa 31:21; 61:5). The figure is changed in the last clause, but the sentiment is the same.

entries.title: Psalms 34:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. head be lifted up-- I shall be placed beyond the reach of my enemies. Hence he avows his purpose of rendering joyful thank offerings.

entries.title: Psalms 34:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. Still pressing need extorts prayer for help. cry with my voice-- denotes earnestness. Other things equal, Christians in earnest pray audibly, even in secret.

entries.title: Psalms 34:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. The meaning is clear, though the construction in a literal translation is obscure. The English Version supplies the implied clause. To seek God's face is to seek His favor (Psa 105:4).

entries.title: Psalms 34:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Hide not, &c.-- (Psa 4:6; 22:24). Against rejection he pleads former mercy and love.

entries.title: Psalms 34:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. In the extremity of earthly destitution (Psa 31:11; 38:11), God provides (compare Mt 25:35).

entries.title: Psalms 34:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. thy way-- of providence. a plain path-- (Psa 26:12). enemies-- literally, "watchers for my fall" (Psa 5:8).

entries.title: Psalms 34:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. will-- literally, "soul," "desire" (Psa 35:25). enemies-- literally, "oppressors." Falsehood aids cruelty against him. breathe out-- as being filled with it (Ac 9:1).

entries.title: Psalms 35 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. The strong emotion is indicated by the incomplete sentence, for which the English Version supplies a proper clause; or, omitting that, and rendering, "yet I believed," &c., the contrast of his faith and his danger is expressed. to see-- is to experience (Psa 22:17).

entries.title: Psalms 35:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. Wait, &c.-- in confident expectation. The last clause is, literally, "and wait," &c., as if expecting new measures of help.

entries.title: Psalms 35:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 28 Psa 28:1-9. An earnest cry for divine aid against his enemies, as being also those of God, is followed by the Psalmist's praise in assurance of a favorable answer, and a prayer for all God's people. 1. my rock-- (Psa 18:2, 31). be not silent to me-- literally, "from me," deaf or inattentive. become like them, &c.-- share their fate. go down into the pit-- or, "grave" (Psa 30:3).

entries.title: Psalms 35:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. lift up my hands-- a gesture of prayer (Psa 63:4; 141:2). oracle-- place of speaking (Ex 25:22; Nu 7:89), where God answered His people (compare Psa 5:7).

entries.title: Psalms 35:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Draw me not away-- implies punishment as well as death (compare Psa 26:9). Hypocrisy is the special wickedness mentioned.

entries.title: Psalms 35:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. The imprecation is justified in Psa 28:5. The force of the passage is greatly enhanced by the accumulation of terms describing their sin. endeavours-- points out their deliberate sinfulness.

entries.title: Psalms 35:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. Disregard of God's judgments brings a righteous punishment. destroy ... build ... up-- The positive strengthened by the negative form.

entries.title: Psalms 35:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. supplications-- or, "cries for mercy."

entries.title: Psalms 35:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. The repetition of "heart" denotes his sincerity.

entries.title: Psalms 35:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. The distinction made between the people. their strength-- and the anointed--may indicate Absalom's rebellion as the occasion.

entries.title: Psalms 35:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. The special prayer for the people sustains this view. feed them-- as a shepherd (Psa 23:1, &c.).

entries.title: Psalms 35:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 29 Psa 29:1-11. Trust in God is encouraged by the celebration of His mighty power as illustrated in His dominion over the natural world, in some of its most terrible and wonderful exhibitions. 1. Give-- or, "ascribe" (De 32:3). mighty-- or, "sons of the mighty" (Psa 89:6). Heavenly beings, as angels.

entries.title: Psalms 35:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. name-- as (Psa 5:11; 8:1). beauty of holiness-- the loveliness of a spiritual worship, of which the perceptible beauty of the sanctuary worship was but a type.

entries.title: Psalms 35:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. The voice of the Lord-- audible exhibition of His power in the tempest, of which thunder is a specimen, but not the uniform or sole example. the waters-- the clouds or vapors (Psa 18:11; Jr 10:13).

entries.title: Psalms 35:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. powerful ... majesty-- literally, "in power, in majesty."

entries.title: Psalms 35:17–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. The tall and large cedars, especially of Lebanon, are shivered, utterly broken. The waving of the mountain forests before the wind is expressed by the figure of skipping or leaping.

entries.title: Psalms 35:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. divideth-- literally, "hews off." The lightning, like flakes and splinters hewed from stone or wood, flies through the air.

entries.title: Psalms 35:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. the wilderness-- especially Kadesh, south of Judea, is selected as another scene of this display of divine power, as a vast and desolate region impresses the mind, like mountains, with images of grandeur.

entries.title: Psalms 35:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Terror-stricken animals and denuded forests close the illustration. In view of this scene of awful sublimity, God's worshippers respond to the call of Psa 29:2, and speak or cry, "Glory!" By "temple," or "palace" (God's residence, Psa 5:7), may here be meant heaven, or the whole frame of nature, as the angels are called on for praise.

entries.title: Psalms 35:22–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. Over this terrible raging of the elements God is enthroned, directing and restraining by sovereign power; and hence the comfort of His people. "This awful God is ours, our Father and our Love."

entries.title: Psalms 35:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 30 Psa 30:1-12. Literally, "A Psalm-Song"--a composition to be sung with musical instruments, or without them--or, "Song of the dedication," &c. specifying the particular character of the Psalm. Some suppose that of David should be connected with the name of the composition, and not with "house"; and refer for the occasion to the selection of a site for the temple (1Ch 21:26-30; 22:1). But "house" is never used absolutely for the temple, and "dedication" does not well apply to such an occasion. Though the phrase in the Hebrew, "dedication of the house of David," is an unusual form, yet it is equally unusual to disconnect the name of the author and the composition. As a "dedication of David's house" (as provided, De 20:5), the scope of the Psalm well corresponds with the state of repose and meditation on his past trials suited to such an occasion (2Sa 5:11; 7:2). For beginning with a celebration of God's delivering favor, in which he invites others to join, he relates his prayer in distress, and God's gracious and prompt answer. 1. lifted me up-- as one is drawn from a well (Psa 40:2).

entries.title: Psalms 35:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. healed me-- Affliction is often described as disease (Psa 6:2; 41:4; 107:20), and so relief by healing.

entries.title: Psalms 35:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. The terms describe extreme danger. soul-- or, "myself." grave-- literally, "hell," as in Psa 16:10. hast kept me ... pit-- quickened or revived me from the state of dying (compare Psa 28:1).

entries.title: Psalms 35:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. remembrance-- the thing remembered or memorial. holiness-- as the sum of God's perfections (compare Psa 22:3), used as name (Ex 3:15; Psa 135:13).

entries.title: Psalms 36 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. Relatively, the longest experience of divine anger by the pious is momentary. These precious words have consoled millions.

entries.title: Psalms 36:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. What particular prosperity is meant we do not know; perhaps his accession to the throne. In his self-complacent elation he was checked by God's hiding His face (compare Psa 22:24; 27:9).

entries.title: Psalms 36:3–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-11. As in Psa 6:5; 88:10; Is 38:18, the appeal for mercy is based on the destruction of his agency in praising God here, which death would produce. The terms expressing relief are poetical, and not to be pressed, though "dancing" is the translation of a word which means a lute, whose cheerful notes are contrasted with mourning, or (Am 5:16) wailing.

entries.title: Psalms 36:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. Though "my" is supplied before "glory" it is better as in Psa 16:9, to receive it as used for tongue, the organ of praise. The ultimate end of God's mercies to us is our praise to Him.

entries.title: Psalms 36:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 31 Psa 31:1-24. The prayer of a believer in time of deep distress. In the first part, cries for help are mingled with expressions of confidence. Then the detail of griefs engrosses his attention, till, in the assurance of strong but submissive faith, he rises to the language of unmingled joyful trust and exhorts others to like love and confidence towards God. 1. Expresses the general tone of feeling of the Psalm.

entries.title: Psalms 36:10–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-4. He seeks help in God's righteous government (Psa 5:8), and begs for an attentive hearing, and speedy and effectual aid. With no other help and no claim of merit, he relies solely on God's regard to His own perfections for a safe guidance and release from the snares of his enemies. On the terms "rock," &c., (compare Psa 17:2; 18:2, 50; 20:6; 23:3; 25:21).

entries.title: Psalms 37 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. commit my spirit-- my life, or myself. Our Saviour used the words on the Cross [Lu 23:46], not as prophetical, but, as many pious men have done, as expressive of His unshaken confidence in God. The Psalmist rests on God's faithfulness to His promises to His people, and hence avows himself one of them, detesting all who revere objects of idolatry (compare De 32:21; 1Co 8:4).

entries.title: Psalms 37:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. hast known my soul, &c.-- had regard to me in trouble.

entries.title: Psalms 37:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. shut me up ... enemy-- abandon to (1Sa 23:11). large room-- place of safety (compare Psa 18:19).

entries.title: Psalms 37:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. mine eye, &c.-- denotes extreme weakness (compare Psa 6:7). grief-- mingled sorrow and indignation (Psa 6:7). soul and ... belly-- the whole person.

entries.title: Psalms 37:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. among-- or, literally, "from," or, "by" my enemies. The latter clauses describe the progress of his disgrace to the lowest degree, till,

entries.title: Psalms 37:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. he is forgotten as one dead, and contemned as a useless broken vessel.

entries.title: Psalms 37:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. For-- introduces further reasons for his prayer, the unjust, deliberate, and murderous purposes of his foes.

entries.title: Psalms 37:9–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-18. In his profession of trust he includes the terms of the prayer expressing it.

entries.title: Psalms 37:14–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-21. God displays openly His purposed goodness to His people.

entries.title: Psalms 37:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. For I said-- literally, "And I said," in an adversative sense. I, thus favored, was despondent. in my haste-- in my terror. cut off ... eyes-- from all the protection of Thy presence.

entries.title: Psalms 37:18–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23-24. the Lord ... proud doer-- literally, "the Lord is keeping faith," that is, with His people, and is repaying, &c. Then let none despair, but take courage; their hopes shall not be in vain.

entries.title: Psalms 37:21–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 32 Psa 32:1-11. Maschil--literally, "giving instruction."
