The Psalmist describes the blessings of His forgiveness, succeeding the pains of conviction, and deduces from his own experience instruction and exhortation to others. 1-2. (Compare Ro 4:6). forgiven-- literally, "taken away," opposed to retain (Joh 20:23). covered-- so that God no longer regards the sin (Psa 85:3).

entries.title: Psalms 37:23–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. A vivid description of felt, but unacknowledged, sin. When-- literally, "for," as in Psa 32:4.

entries.title: Psalms 37:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. A prompt fulfilment of the purposed confession is followed by a prompt forgiveness.

entries.title: Psalms 37:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. For this-- that is, my happy experience. godly-- pious in the sense of Psa 4:3. a time-- (Is 55:6); when God's Spirit inclines us to seek pardon, He is ready to forgive. floods, &c.-- denotes great danger (Psa 18:17; 66:12).

entries.title: Psalms 37:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. His experience illustrates the statement of Psa 32:6.

entries.title: Psalms 37:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Whether, as most likely, the language of David (compare Psa 51:13), or that of God, this is a promise of divine guidance. I will ... mine eye-- or, My eye shall be on thee, watching and directing thy way.

entries.title: Psalms 37:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. The latter clause, more literally, "in that they come not near thee"; that is, because they will not come, &c., unless forced by bit and bridle.

entries.title: Psalms 37:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. The sorrows of the impenitent contrasted with the peace and safety secured by God's mercy.

entries.title: Psalms 37:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. The righteous and upright, or those conforming to the divine teaching for securing the divine blessing, may well rejoice with shouting.

entries.title: Psalms 37:33–35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 33 Psa 33:1-22. A call to lively and joyous praise to God for His glorious attributes and works, as displayed in creation, and His general and special providence, in view of which, the Psalmist, for all the pious, professes trust and joy and invokes God's mercy. 1-3. The sentiment falls in with Psa 32:11 (compare 1Co 14:15). The instruments (Psa 92:3; 144:9) do not exclude the voice.

entries.title: Psalms 37:36–38:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-9. Reasons for praise: first, God's truth, faithfulness, and mercy, generally; then, His creative power which all must honor.

entries.title: Psalms 38:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. In God's providence He thwarts men's purposes and executes His own. heathen-- literally, "nations."

entries.title: Psalms 38:3–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-19. The inference from the foregoing in Psa 33:12 is illustrated by God's special providence, underlying which is His minute knowledge of all men.

entries.title: Psalms 38:11–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20-22. waiteth-- in earnest expectation.

entries.title: Psalms 38:15–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 34 Psa 34:1-22. On the title compare 1Sa 21:13. Abimelech was the general name of the sovereign (Ge 20:2). After celebrating God's gracious dealings with him, the Psalmist exhorts others to make trial of His providential care, instructing them how to secure it. He then contrasts God's care of His people and His punitive providence towards the wicked. 1-4. Even in distress, which excites supplication, there is always matter for praising and thanking God (compare Ep 5:20; Php 4:6).

entries.title: Psalms 38:19–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-7. God's favor to the pious generally, and to himself specially, is celebrated. looked-- with desire for help. lightened-- or, "brightened," expressing joy, opposed to the downcast features of those who are ashamed or disappointed (Psa 25:2, 3).

entries.title: Psalms 38:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. taste and see-- try and experience.

entries.title: Psalms 39 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. that fear him-- who are pious--fear and love (Pr 1:7; 9:10). saints-- consecrated to His service (Is 40:31).

entries.title: Psalms 39:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. not want any good-- "good" is emphatic; they may be afflicted (compare Psa 34:10); but this may be a good (2Co 4:17, 18; He 12:10, 11).

entries.title: Psalms 39:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. children-- subjects of instruction (Pr 1:8, 10).

entries.title: Psalms 39:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. What man-- Whoever desires the blessings of piety, let him attend.

entries.title: Psalms 39:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-14. Sins of thought included in those of speech (Lu 6:45), avoiding evil and doing good in our relations to men are based on a right relation to God.

entries.title: Psalms 39:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. eyes of the Lord are upon-- (Psa 32:8; 33:18).

entries.title: Psalms 39:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. face ... against-- opposed to them (Le 17:10; 20:3). cut off the remembrance-- utterly destroy (Psa 109:13).

entries.title: Psalms 39:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-18. Humble penitents are objects of God's special tender regard (Psa 51:19; Is 57:15).

entries.title: Psalms 39:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. bones-- framework of the body.

entries.title: Psalms 39:12–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21-22. Contrast in the destiny of righteous and wicked; the former shall be delivered and never come into condemnation (Joh 5:24; Ro 8:1); the latter are left under condemnation and desolate.

entries.title: Psalms 40:1–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 35 Psa 35:1-28. The Psalmist invokes God's aid, contrasting the hypocrisy, cunning, and malice of his enemies with his integrity and generosity. The imprecations of the first part including a brief notice of their conduct, the fuller exposition of their hypocrisy and malice in the second, and the earnest prayer for deliverance from their scornful triumph in the last, are each closed (Psa 35:9, 10, 18, 27, 28) with promises of praise for the desired relief, in which his friends will unite. The historical occasion is probably 1Sa 24:1-22. 1-3. God is invoked in the character of a warrior (Ex 15:3; De 32:41).

entries.title: Psalms 40:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. (Compare Psa 9:17). devise my hurt-- purpose for evil to me.

entries.title: Psalms 40:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. (Compare Psa 1:4)-- a terrible fate; driven by wind on a slippery path in darkness, and hotly pursued by supernatural violence (2Sa 24:16; Ac 12:23).

entries.title: Psalms 40:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. net in a pit-- or, "pit of their net"--or, "net-pit," as "holy hill" for "hill of holiness" (Psa 2:6); a figure from hunting (Psa 7:15). Their imprecations on impenitent rebels against God need no vindication; His justice and wrath are for such; His mercy for penitents. Compare Psa 7:16; 11:5, on the peculiar fate of the wicked here noticed.

entries.title: Psalms 40:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. All my bones-- every part. him that spoileth him-- (Compare Psa 10:2).

entries.title: Psalms 40:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. False witnesses-- literally, "Witnesses of injustice and cruelty" (compare Psa 11:5; 25:19).

entries.title: Psalms 40:12–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-14. Though they rendered evil for good, he showed a tender sympathy in their affliction. spoiling-- literally, "bereavement." The usual modes of showing grief are made, as figures, to express his sorrow.

entries.title: Psalms 40:15–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-16. On the contrary, they rejoiced in his affliction. Halting, or, "lameness," as in Psa 38:17 for any distress. abjects-- either as cripples (compare 2Sa 4:4), contemptible; or, degraded persons, such as had been beaten (compare Job 30:1-8). I knew it not-- either the persons, or, reasons of such conduct. tear me, and ceased not-- literally, "were not silent"--showing that the tearing meant slandering.

entries.title: Psalms 40:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. darling-- (Compare Psa 22:20, 21).

entries.title: Psalms 41 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. (Compare Psa 22:22).

entries.title: Psalms 41:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. enemies wrongfully-- by false and slanderous imputations. wink with the eye-- an insulting gesture (Pr 6:13). without a cause-- manifests more malice than having a wrong cause.

entries.title: Psalms 41:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. deceitful matters-- or, "words of deceit." quiet in the land-- the pious lovers of peace.

entries.title: Psalms 41:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. On the gesture compare Psa 22:7; and on the expressions of malicious triumph, compare Psa 10:13; 28:3.

entries.title: Psalms 41:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23-24. (Compare Psa 7:6; 26:1; 2Th 1:6). God's righteous government is the hope of the pious and terror of the wicked.

entries.title: Psalms 41:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. swallowed him up-- utterly destroyed him (Psa 21:9; La 2:16).

entries.title: Psalms 41:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. clothed-- covered wholly (Job 8:22).

entries.title: Psalms 41:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27. favour ... cause-- delight in it, as vindicated by Thee. Let the Lord, &c.-- Let Him be greatly praised for His care of the just.

entries.title: Psalms 41:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28. In this praise of God's equitable government (Psa 5:8) the writer promises ever to engage.

entries.title: Psalms 41:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 36 Psa 36:1-12. On servant of the Lord, see on Psa 18:1, title. The wickedness of man contrasted with the excellency of God's perfections and dispensations; and the benefit of the latter sought, and the evils of the former deprecated. 1. The general sense of this difficult verse is, "that the wicked have no fear of God." The first clause may be rendered, "Saith transgression in my heart, in respect to the wicked, there is no fear," &c., that is, such is my reflection on men's transgressions.

entries.title: Psalms 41:13–42:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-4. This reflection detailed. until his iniquity-- literally, "for finding his iniquity for hating"; that is, he persuades himself God will not so find it--"for hating" involving the idea of punishing. Hence his words of iniquity and deceit, and his bold rejection of all right principles of conduct. The climax is that he deliberately adopts and patronizes evil. The negative forms affirm more emphatically their contraries.

entries.title: Psalms 42:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. mercy ... and ... faithfulness-- as mercy and truth (Psa 25:10).

entries.title: Psalms 42:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. shadow of thy wings-- (Compare De 32:11; Psa 91:1).

entries.title: Psalms 42:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. fatness-- richness. thy house-- residence--for the privileges and blessings of communion with God (Psa 23:6; 27:4). river of thy pleasures-- plenteous supply; may allude to Eden.

entries.title: Psalms 42:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Light is an emblem of all blessings, given of God as a means to gain more.

entries.title: Psalms 42:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. that know thee-- right knowledge of God is the source of right affections and conduct.

entries.title: Psalms 42:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. foot of ... hand ... wicked-- all kinds of violent dealing.

entries.title: Psalms 42:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. There-- in the acting of violence, they are overthrown. A signal defeat.

entries.title: Psalms 42:11–43:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 37 Psa 37:1-40. A composed and uniform trust in God and a constant course of integrity are urged in view of the blessedness of the truly pious, contrasted in various aspects with the final ruin of the wicked. Thus the wisdom and justice of God's providence are vindicated, and its seeming inequalities, which excite the cavils of the wicked and the distrust of the pious, are explained. David's personal history abundantly illustrates the Psalm. 1-2. The general sentiment of the whole Psalm is expressed. The righteous need not be vexed by the prosperity of the wicked; for it is transient, and their destiny undesirable.

entries.title: Psalms 43:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Trust-- sure of safety. shalt thou dwell-- or, "dwell thou"; repose quietly. verily ... fed-- or, "feed on truth," God's promise (Psa 36:5; compare Ho 12:1).

entries.title: Psalms 43:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. desires-- (Psa 20:5; 21:2), what is lawful and right, really good (Psa 84:11).

entries.title: Psalms 43:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. Commit thy way-- (Pr 16:3). Works--what you have to do and cannot set forth as a burden. trust ... in him-- literally, "on Him." He will do what you cannot (compare Psa 22:8; 31:6). He will not suffer your character to remain under suspicion.

entries.title: Psalms 43:5–44:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. Rest in-- literally, "Be silent to the Lord." and wait-- Be submissive--avoid petulance and murmurings, anger and rash doing.

entries.title: Psalms 44:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Two reasons: The prosperity of the wicked is short; and the pious, by humble trust, will secure all covenant blessing, denoted here by "inherit the earth" (compare Psa 25:13).

entries.title: Psalms 44:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. shall not be-- literally, "is not"--is not to be found.

entries.title: Psalms 44:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. gnasheth ... teeth-- in beastly rage.

entries.title: Psalms 44:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. (Compare Psa 2:4). seeth-- knows certainly. his day-- of punishment, long delayed, shall yet come (He 10:37).

entries.title: Psalms 44:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. sword, and ... bow-- for any instruments of violence. slay-- literally, "slaughter" (1Sa 25:11). poor and needy-- God's people (Psa 10:17; 12:5). The punishment of the wicked as drawn on themselves--often mentioned (compare Psa 7:15, 16; 35:8).

entries.title: Psalms 44:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. riches-- literally, "noise and tumult," as incidental to much wealth (compare Psa 39:6). Thus the contrast with the "little" of one man is more vivid.

entries.title: Psalms 44:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. Even the members of the body needed to hold weapons are destroyed.

entries.title: Psalms 44:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18-19. God, who knows His people's changes, provides against evil and supplies all their need.

entries.title: Psalms 44:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. While the wicked, however mighty, are destroyed, and that utterly, as smoke which vanishes and leaves no trace.

entries.title: Psalms 44:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21-22. payeth not-- not able; having grown poor (compare De 15:7). Ability of the one and inability of the other do not exclude moral dispositions. God's blessing or cursing makes the difference.

entries.title: Psalms 44:15–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23-24. steps-- way, or, "course of life"; as ordered by God, failures will not be permanent.

entries.title: Psalms 44:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. his seed is blessed-- literally, "for a blessing" (Ge 12:2; Psa 21:6). This position is still true as the rule of God's economy (1Ti 4:8; 6:6).

entries.title: Psalms 44:19–45:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27-29. The exhortation is sustained by the assurance of God's essential rectitude in that providential government which provides perpetual blessings for the good, and perpetual misery for the wicked.

entries.title: Psalms 45:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 30-31. The righteous described as to the elements of character, thought, word, and action.

entries.title: Psalms 45:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 32-33. The devices of the wicked against the good fail because God acquits them.

entries.title: Psalms 45:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 34. On the contrary, the good are not only blessed, but made to see the ruin of their foes.

entries.title: Psalms 45:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 35-36. of which a picture is given, under the figure of a flourishing tree (compare Margin), which soon withers.

entries.title: Psalms 45:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 37. By "the end" is meant reward (Pr 23:18; 24:14), or expectation of success, as in Psa 37:38, which describes the end of the wicked in contrast, and that is cut off (compare Psa 73:17).

entries.title: Psalms 45:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 38. together-- at once; entirely (Psa 4:8).

entries.title: Psalms 45:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 39-40. strength-- (Psa 27:1; 28:8). trouble-- straits (Psa 9:9; 10:1). In trust and quietness is the salvation of the pious from all foes and all their devices.

entries.title: Psalms 45:13–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 38 Psa 38:1-22. To bring to remembrance, or, remind God of His mercy and himself of his sin. Appealing to God for relief from His heavy chastisement, the Psalmist avows his integrity before men, complains of the defection of friends and persecution of enemies, and in a submissive spirit, casting himself on God, with penitent confession he pleads God's covenant relation and his innocence of the charges of his enemies, and prays for divine comfort and help. 1-4. He deprecates deserved punishment, which is described (Psa 6:1), under the figure of bodily disease [Psa 38:3].

entries.title: Psalms 45:17–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-8. The loathsomeness, corruption, and wasting torture of severe physical disease set forth his mental anguish [Psa 38:6]. It is possible some bodily disease was connected. The loins are the seat of strength. His exhaustion left him only the power to groan [Psa 38:9].

entries.title: Psalms 45:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. That God can hear (Ro 8:26).

entries.title: Psalms 45:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. My heart panteth-- as if barely surviving. light ... from me-- utter exhaustion (Psa 6:7; 13:3).

entries.title: Psalms 45:23–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. Friends desert, but foes increase in malignity.

entries.title: Psalms 45:25–26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-14. He patiently submits, uttering no reproaches or replies (Joh 19:9) to their insulting speeches;

entries.title: Psalms 45:27–46:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-17. for he is confident the Lord-- literally, "Sovereign" (to whom he was a servant), would answer his prayer (Psa 3:4; 4:1), and not permit their triumph in his partial halting, of which he was in danger.

entries.title: Psalms 46:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. Consciousness of sin makes suffering pungent, and suffering, rightly received, leads to confession.

entries.title: Psalms 46:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-20. Still, while humbled before God, he is the victim of deadly enemies, full of malice and treachery. enemies are lively-- literally, "of life," who would take my life, that is, deadly.

entries.title: Psalms 46:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21-22. (Compare Psa 22:19; 35:3). All terms of frequent use. In this Psalm the language is generally susceptible of application to Christ as a sufferer, David, as such, typifying Him. This does not require us to apply the confessions of sin, but only the pains or penalties which He bore for us.

entries.title: Psalms 46:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 39 Psa 39:1-13. To Jeduthun (1Ch 16:41, 42), one of the chief singers. His name mentioned, perhaps, as a special honor. Under depressing views of his frailty and the prosperity of the wicked, the Psalmist, tempted to murmur, checks the expression of his feelings, till, led to regard his case aright, he prays for a proper view of his condition and for the divine compassion. 1. I said-- or, "resolved." will take heed-- watch. ways-- conduct, of which the use of the tongue is a part (Jas 1:26). bridle-- literally, "muzzle for my mouth" (compare De 25:4). while ... before me-- in beholding their prosperity (Psa 37:10, 36).

entries.title: Psalms 46:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. even from good-- (Ge 31:24), everything.

entries.title: Psalms 46:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. His emotions, as a smothered flame, burst forth.

entries.title: Psalms 46:10–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-7. Some take these words as those of fretting, but they are not essentially such. The tinge of discontent arises from the character of his suppressed emotions. But, addressing God, they are softened and subdued. make me to know mine end-- experimentally appreciate. how frail I am-- literally, "when I shall cease."

entries.title: Psalms 46:14–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-10. Patiently submissive, he prays for the removal of his chastisement, and that he may not be a reproach.

entries.title: Psalms 46:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. From his own case, he argues to that of all, that the destruction of man's enjoyments is ascribable to sin.

entries.title: Psalms 46:18–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-13. Consonant with the tenor of the Psalm, he prays for God's compassionate regard to him as a stranger here; and that, as such was the condition of his fathers, so, like them, he may be cheered instead of being bound under wrath and chastened in displeasure.

entries.title: Psalms 46:21–47:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 40 Psa 40:1-17. In this Psalm a celebration of God's deliverance is followed by a profession of devotion to His service. Then follows a prayer for relief from imminent dangers, involving the overthrow of enemies and the rejoicing of sympathizing friends. In He 10:5, &c., Paul quotes Psa 40:6-8 as the words of Christ, offering Himself as a better sacrifice. Some suppose Paul thus accommodated David's words to express Christ's sentiments. But the value of his quotation would be thus destroyed, as it would have no force in his argument, unless regarded by his readers as the original sense of the passage in the Old Testament. Others suppose the Psalm describes David's feelings in suffering and joy; but the language quoted by Paul, in the sense given by him, could not apply to David in any of his relations, for as a type the language is not adapted to describe any event or condition of David's career, and as an individual representing the pious generally, neither he nor they could properly use it (see on Psa 40:7, below). The Psalm must be taken then, as the sixteenth, to express the feelings of Christ's human nature. The difficulties pertinent to this view will be considered as they occur. 1-3. The figures for deep distress are illustrated in Jeremiah's history (Jr 38:6-12). Patience and trust manifested in distress, deliverance in answer to prayer, and the blessed effect of it in eliciting praise from God's true worshippers, teach us that Christ's suffering is our example, and His deliverance our encouragement (He 5:7, 8; 12:3; 1Pe 4:12-16). inclined-- (the ear, Psa 17:6), as if to catch the faintest sigh.

entries.title: Psalms 47:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Blessed-- (Psa 1:1; 2:12). respecteth-- literally, "turns towards," as an object of confidence. turn aside-- from true God and His law to falsehood in worship and conduct.

entries.title: Psalms 47:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. be reckoned up in order-- (compare Psa 5:3; 33:14; Is 44:7), too many to be set forth regularly. This is but one instance of many. The use of the plural accords with the union of Christ and His people. In suffering and triumph, they are one with Him.

entries.title: Psalms 47:3–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-8. In Paul's view this passage has more meaning than the mere expression of grateful devotion to God's service. He represents Christ as declaring that the sacrifices, whether vegetable or animal, general or special expiatory offerings, would not avail to meet the demands of God's law, and that He had come to render the required satisfaction, which he states was effected by "the offering of the body of Christ" [He 10:10], for that is the "will of God" which Christ came to fulfil or do, in order to effect man's redemption. We thus see that the contrast to the unsatisfactory character assigned the Old Testament offerings in Psa 40:6 is found in the compliance with God's law (compare Psa 40:7, 8). Of course, as Paul and other New Testament writers explain Christ's work, it consisted in more than being made under the law or obeying its precepts. It required an "obedience unto death" [Php 2:8], and that is the compliance here chiefly intended, and which makes the contrast with Psa 40:6 clear. mine ears hast thou opened-- Whether allusion is made to the custom of boring a servant's ear, in token of voluntary and perpetual enslavement (Ex 21:6), or that the opening of the ear, as in Is 48:8; 50:5 (though by a different word in Hebrew) denotes obedience by the common figure of hearing for obeying, it is evident that the clause is designed to express a devotion to God's will as avowed more fully in Psa 40:8, and already explained. Paul, however, uses the words, "a body hast thou prepared me" [He 10:5], which are found in the Septuagint in the place of the words, "mine ears hast thou opened." He does not lay any stress on this clause, and his argument is complete without it. It is, perhaps, to be regarded rather as an interpretation or free translation by the Septuagint, than either an addition or attempt at verbal translation. The Septuagint translators may have had reference to Christ's vicarious sufferings as taught in other Scriptures, as in Is 53:4-11; at all events, the sense is substantially the same, as a body was essential to the required obedience (compare Ro 7:4; 1Pe 2:24).

entries.title: Psalms 47:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. I have preached-- literally, "announced good tidings." Christ's prophetical office is taught. He "preached" the great truths of God's government of sinners.

entries.title: Psalms 47:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. may be rendered as an assertion, that God will not withhold (Psa 16:1).

entries.title: Psalms 47:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. evils-- inflicted by others. iniquities-- or penal afflictions, and sometimes calamities in the wide sense. This meaning of the word is very common (Psa 31:11; 38:4; compare Ge 4:13, Cain's punishment; Ge 19:15, that of Sodom; 1Sa 28:10, of the witch of En-dor; also 2Sa 16:12; Job 19:29; Is 5:18; 53:11). This meaning of the word is also favored by the clause, "taken hold of me," which follows, which can be said appropriately of sufferings, but not of sins (compare Job 27:20; Psa 69:24). Thus, the difficulties in referring this Psalm to Christ, arising from the usual reading of this verse, are removed. Of the terrible afflictions, or sufferings, alluded to and endured for us, compare Lu 22:39-44, and the narrative of the scenes of Calvary. my heart faileth me-- (Mt 26:38), "My soul is exceeding sorrowful, even unto death." cannot look up-- literally, "I cannot see," not denoting the depression of conscious guilt, as Lu 18:13, but exhaustion from suffering, as dimness of eyes (compare Psa 6:7; 13:3; 38:10). The whole context thus sustains the sense assigned to iniquities.

entries.title: Psalms 47:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. (Compare Psa 22:19).

entries.title: Psalms 47:11–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. The language is not necessarily imprecatory, but rather a confident expectation (Psa 5:11), though the former sense is not inconsistent with Christ's prayer for the forgiveness of His murderers, inasmuch as their confusion and shame might be the very means to prepare them for humbly seeking forgiveness (compare Ac 2:37).

entries.title: Psalms 47:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. (Compare Psa 35:27). love thy salvation-- delight in its bestowal on others as well as themselves.

entries.title: Psalms 47:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. A summary of his condition and hopes. thinketh upon-- or provides for me. "He was heard," "when he had offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears, unto Him that was able to save him from death" [He 5:7].

entries.title: Psalms 47:16–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 41 Psa 41:1-13. The Psalmist celebrates the blessedness of those who compassionate the poor, conduct strongly contrasted with the spite of his enemies and neglect of his friends in his calamity. He prays for God's mercy in view of his ill desert, and, in confidence of relief, and that God will vindicate his cause, he closes with a doxology. 1-3. God rewards kindness to the poor (Pr 19:17). From Psa 41:2, 11 it may be inferred that the Psalmist describes his own conduct. poor-- in person, position, and possessions.

entries.title: Psalms 47:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. I said-- I asked the mercy I show. heal my soul-- (Compare Psa 30:2). "Sin and suffering are united," is one of the great teachings of the Psalms.

entries.title: Psalms 47:20–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. A graphic picture of the conduct of a malignant enemy.

entries.title: Psalms 47:22–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. So of others, all act alike.

entries.title: Psalms 47:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. mine ... friend-- literally, "the man of my peace." eat ... bread-- who depended on me or was well treated by me. hath lifted up heel-- in scornful violence. As David and his fortunes typified Christ and His (compare Introduction), so these words expressed the treatment he received, and also that of his Son and Lord; hence, though not distinctly prophetical, our Saviour (Joh 13:18) applies them to Judas, "that the Scripture may be fulfilled." This last phrase has a wide use in the New Testament, and is not restricted to denote special prophecies.

entries.title: Psalms 47:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. A lawful punishment of criminals is not revenge, nor inconsistent with their final good (compare Psa 40:14, 15).

entries.title: Psalms 47:26–28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-13. favourest-- or tenderly lovest me (Ge 34:19), evinced by relief from his enemies, and, farther, God recognizes his innocence by upholding him.

entries.title: Psalms 47:30–31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 42 Psa 42:1-11. Maschil--(See on Psa 32:1, title). For, or of (see Introduction) the sons of Korah. The writer, perhaps one of this Levitical family of singers accompanying David in exile, mourns his absence from the sanctuary, a cause of grief aggravated by the taunts of enemies, and is comforted in hopes of relief. This course of thought is repeated with some variety of detail, but closing with the same refrain. 1-2. Compare (Psa 63:1). panteth-- desires in a state of exhaustion.

entries.title: Psalms 47:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Where is thy God?-- implying that He had forsaken him (compare 2Sa 16:7; Psa 3:2; 22:8).

entries.title: Psalms 47:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. The verbs are properly rendered as futures, "I will remember," &c.,-- that is, the recollection of this season of distress will give greater zest to the privileges of God's worship, when obtained.

entries.title: Psalms 47:34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. Hence he chides his despondent soul, assuring himself of a time of joy. help of his countenance-- or, "face" (compare Nu 6:25; Psa 4:6; 16:11).

entries.title: Psalms 47:35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Dejection again described. therefore-- that is, finding no comfort in myself, I turn to Thee, even in this distant "land of Jordan and the (mountains) Hermon, the country east of Jordan. hill Mizar-- as a name of a small hill contrasted with the mountains round about Jerusalem, perhaps denoted the contempt with which the place of exile was regarded.

entries.title: Psalms 48 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. The roar of successive billows, responding to that of floods of rain, represented the heavy waves of sorrow which overwhelmed him.

entries.title: Psalms 48:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Still he relies on as constant a flow of divine mercy which will elicit his praise and encourage his prayer to God.

entries.title: Psalms 48:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. in view of which [Psa 42:8], he dictates to himself a prayer based on his distress, aggravated as it was by the cruel taunts and infidel suggestions of his foes.

entries.title: Psalms 48:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. This brings on a renewed self-chiding, and excites hopes of relief. health-- or help. of my countenance-- (compare Psa 42:5) who cheers me, driving away clouds of sorrow from my face. my God-- It is He of whose existence and favor my foes would have me doubt.

entries.title: Psalms 48:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 43 Psa 43:1-5. Excepting the recurrence of the refrain, there is no good reason to suppose this a part of the preceding, though the scope is the same. It has always been placed separate. 1. Judge-- or, "vindicate" (Psa 10:18). plead, &c.-- (Psa 35:1). ungodly-- neither in character or condition objects of God's favor (compare Psa 4:3).

entries.title: Psalms 48:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. God of my strength-- by covenant relation my stronghold (Psa 18:1). cast me off-- in scorn. because-- or, "in," that is, in such circumstances of oppression.

entries.title: Psalms 48:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. light-- as in Psa 27:1. truth-- or, "faithfulness" (Psa 25:5), manifest it by fulfilling promises. Light and truth are personified as messengers who will bring him to the privileged place of worship. tabernacles-- plural, in allusion to the various courts.

entries.title: Psalms 48:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. the altar-- as the chief place of worship. The mention of the harp suggests the prominence of praise in his offering.

entries.title: Psalms 48:12–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 44 Psa 44:1-26. In a time of great national distress, probably in David's reign, the Psalmist recounts God's gracious dealings in former times, and the confidence they had learned to repose in Him. After a vivid picture of their calamities, he humbly expostulates against God's apparent forgetfulness, reminding Him of their faithfulness and mourning their heavy sorrows. 1-3. This period is that of the settlement of Canaan (Jos 24:12; Jud 6:3). have told-- or, "related" (compare Ex 10:2).

entries.title: Psalms 48:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Thou art my King-- literally, "he who is my King," sustaining the same covenant relation as to the "fathers."

entries.title: Psalms 48:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. The figure drawn from the habits of the ox.

entries.title: Psalms 48:17–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-8. God is not only our sole help, but only worthy of praise.

entries.title: Psalms 48:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. But-- contrasting, cast off as abhorrent (Psa 43:2). goest not forth-- literally, "will not go" (2Sa 5:23). In several consecutive verses the leading verb is future, and the following one past (in Hebrew), thus denoting the causes and effects. Thus (Psa 44:10-12), when defeated, spoiling follows; when delivered as sheep, dispersion follows, &c.

entries.title: Psalms 48:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. The Babylonian captivity not necessarily meant. There were others (compare 1Ki 8:46).

entries.title: Psalms 49:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 49:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. shame of ... face-- blushes in disgrace.

entries.title: Psalms 49:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. Its cause, the taunts and presence of malignant enemies (Psa 8:2).

entries.title: Psalms 49:5–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-19. They had not apostatized totally-- were still God's people.

entries.title: Psalms 49:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20-21. A solemn appeal to God to witness their constancy. stretched out ... hands-- gesture of worship (Ex 9:29; Psa 88:9).

entries.title: Psalms 49:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. Their protracted sufferings as God's people attests the constancy. Paul (Ro 8:36) uses this to describe Christian steadfastness in persecution.

entries.title: Psalms 49:11–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23-26. This style of addressing God, as indifferent, is frequent (Psa 3:7; 9:19; 13:1, &c.). However low their condition, God is appealed to, on the ground, and for the honor, of His mercy.

entries.title: Psalms 49:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 45 Psa 45:1-17. Shoshannim--literally, "Lilies," either descriptive of an instrument so shaped, or denoting some tune or air so called, after which the Psalm was to be sung (see on Psa 8:1, title). A song of loves, or, of beloved ones (plural and feminine)--a conjugal song. Maschil--(See on Psa 32:1, title, and Psa 42:1, title) denotes the didactic character of the Psalm; that it gives instruction, the song being of allegorical, and not literal, import. The union and glories of Christ and his Church are described. He is addressed as a king possessed of all essential graces, as a conqueror exalted on the throne of a righteous and eternal government, and as a bridegroom arrayed in nuptial splendor. The Church is portrayed in the purity and loveliness of a royally adorned and attended bride, invited to forsake her home and share the honors of her affianced lord. The picture of an Oriental wedding thus opened is filled up by representing the complimentary gifts of the wealthy with which the occasion is honored, the procession of the bride clothed in splendid raiment, attended by her virgin companions, and the entrance of the joyous throng into the palace of the king. A prediction of a numerous and distinguished progeny, instead of the complimentary wish for it usually expressed (compare Ge 24:60; Ru 4:11, 12), and an assurance of a perpetual fame, closes the Psalm. All ancient Jewish and Christian interpreters regarded this Psalm as an allegory of the purport above named. In the Song of Songs the allegory is carried out more fully. Hosea (Ho 1:1-3:5) treats the relation of God and His people under the same figure, and its use to set forth the relation of Christ and His Church runs through both parts of the Bible (compare Is 54:5; 62:4, 5; Mt 22:3; 25:1; Joh 3:29; Ep 5:25-32, &c.). Other methods of exposition have been suggested. Several Jewish monarchs, from Solomon to the wicked Ahab, and various foreign princes, have been named as the hero of the song. But to none of them can the terms here used be shown to apply, and it is hardly probable that any mere nuptial song, especially of a heathen king, would be permitted a place in the sacred songs of the Jews. The advocates for any other than the Messianic interpretation have generally silenced each other in succession, while the application of the most rigorous rules of a fair system of interpretation has but strengthened the evidences in its favor. The scope of the Psalm above given is easy and sustained by the explication of its details. The quotation of Psa 45:6, 7 by Paul (He 1:8, 9), as applicable to Christ, ought to be conclusive, and their special exposition shows the propriety of such an application. 1. An animated preface indicative of strong emotion. Literally, "My heart overflows: a good matter I speak; the things which I have made," &c. inditing-- literally, "boiling up," as a fountain overflows. my tongue is the pen-- a mere instrument of God's use. of a ready writer-- that is, it is fluent. The theme is inspiring and language flows fast.

entries.title: Psalms 49:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. To rich personal attractions is added grace of the lips, captivating powers of speech. This is given, and becomes a source of power and proves a blessing. Christ is a prophet (Lu 4:22).

entries.title: Psalms 49:18–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. The king is addressed as ready to go forth to battle. sword-- (Compare Re 1:16; 19:15). mighty-- (Compare Is 9:6). glory and ... majesty-- generally used as divine attributes (Psa 96:6; 104:1; 111:3), or as specially conferred on mortals (Psa 21:5), perhaps these typically.

entries.title: Psalms 49:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. The result. people-- Whole nations are subdued.

entries.title: Psalms 50 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. No lawful construction can be devised to change the sense here given and sustained by the ancient versions, and above all by Paul (He 1:8). Of the perpetuity of this government, compare 2Sa 7:13; Psa 10:16; 72:5; 89:4; 110:4; Is 9:7.

entries.title: Psalms 50:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. As in Psa 45:6 the divine nature is made prominent, here the moral qualities of the human are alleged as the reason or ground of the mediatorial exultation. Some render "O God, thy God," instead of God, thy God-- but the latter is sustained by the same form (Psa 50:7), and it was only of His human nature that the anointing could be predicated (compare Is 61:3). oil of gladness-- or token of gladness, as used in feasts and other times of solemn joy (compare 1Ki 1:39, 40). fellows-- other kings.

entries.title: Psalms 50:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. The king thus inaugurated is now presented as a bridegroom, who appears in garments richly perfumed, brought out from ivory palaces-- His royal residence; by which, as indications of the happy bridal occasion, He has been gladdened.

entries.title: Psalms 50:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. In completion of this picture of a marriage festival, female attendants or bridesmaids of the highest rank attend Him, while the queen, in rich apparel (Psa 45:13), stands ready for the nuptial procession.

entries.title: Psalms 50:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. She is invited to the union, for forming which she must leave her father's people. She representing, by the form of the allegory, the Church, this address is illustrated by all those scriptures, from Ge 12:1 on, which speak of the people of God as a chosen, separate, and peculiar people. The relation of subjection to her spouse at once accords with the law of marriage, as given in Ge 3:16; 18:12; Ep 5:22; 1Pe 3:5, 6, and the relation of the Church to Christ (Ep 5:24). The love of the husband is intimately connected with the entire devotion to which the bride is exhorted.

entries.title: Psalms 50:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. daughter of Tyre-- (Psa 9:14); denotes the people. Tyre, celebrated for its great wealth, is selected to represent the richest nations, an idea confirmed by the next clause. These gifts are brought as means to conciliate the royal parties, representing the admitted subjection of the offerers. This well sets forth the exalted position of the Church and her head, whose moral qualities receive the homage of the world. The contribution of material wealth to sustain the institutions of the Church may be included (compare "riches of the Gentiles," Psa 72:10; Is 60:5-10).

entries.title: Psalms 50:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. the king's daughter-- a term of dignity. It may also intimate, with some allusion to the teaching of the allegory, that the bride of Christ, the Church, is the daughter of the great king, God. within-- Not only is her outward raiment costly, but all her apparel is of the richest texture. wrought gold-- gold embroidery, or cloth in which gold is woven.

entries.title: Psalms 50:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. The progress of the procession is described; according to the usual custom the bride and attendants are conducted to the palace. Some for the words-- in raiment of needlework-- propose another rendering, "on variegated (or embroidered) cloths"--that is, in the manner of the East, richly wrought tapestry was spread on the ground, on which the bride walked. As the dress had been already mentioned, this seems to be a probable translation.

entries.title: Psalms 50:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. As earthly monarchs govern widely extended empires by viceroys, this glorious king is represented as supplying all the principalities of earth with princes of his own numerous progeny.

entries.title: Psalms 50:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. The glories of this empire shall be as wide as the world and lasting as eternity. therefore-- Because thus glorious, the praise shall be universal and perpetual. Some writers have taxed their ingenuity to find in the history and fortunes of Christ and His Church exact parallels for every part of this splendid allegory, not excepting its gorgeous Oriental imagery. Thus, by the dresses of the king and queen, are thought to be meant the eminent endowments and graces of Christ and His people. The attendant women, supposed (though inconsistently it might seem with the inspired character of the work) to be concubines, are thought to represent the Gentile churches, and the bride the Jewish, &c. But it is evident that we cannot pursue such a mode of interpretation. For, following the allegory, we must suspend to the distant future the results of a union whose consummation as a marriage is still distant (compare Re 21:9). In fact, the imagery here and elsewhere sets before us the Church in two aspects. As a body, it is yet incomplete, the whole is yet ungathered. As a moral institution, it is yet imperfect. In the final catastrophe it will be complete and perfect. Thus, as a bride adorned, &c., it will be united with its Lord. Thus the union of Christ and the Church triumphant is set forth. On the other hand, in regard to its component parts, the relation of Christ as head, as husband, &c., already exists, and as these parts form an institution in this world, it is by His union with it, and the gifts and graces with which He endows it, that a spiritual seed arises and spreads in the world. Hence we must fix our minds only on the one simple but grand truth, that Christ loves the Church, is head over all things for it, raises it in His exaltation to the highest moral dignity--a dignity of which every, even the meanest, sincere disciple will partake. As to the time, then, in which this allegorical prophecy is to fulfilled, it may be said that no periods of time are specially designated. The characteristics of the relation of Christ and His Church are indicated, and we may suppose that the whole process of His exaltation from the declaration of His Sonship, by His resurrection, to the grand catastrophe of the final judgment, with all the collateral blessings to the Church and the world, lay before the vision of the inspired prophet.

entries.title: Psalms 50:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 46 Psa 46:1-11. Upon Alamoth--most probably denotes the treble, or part sung by female voices, the word meaning "virgins"; and which was sung with some appropriately keyed instrument (compare 1Ch 15:19-21; see on Psa 6:1, title). The theme may be stated in Luther's well-known words, "A mighty fortress is our God." The great deliverance (2Ki 19:35; Is 37:36) may have occasioned its composition. 1. refuge-- literally, "a place of trust" (Psa 2:12). strength-- (Psa 18:2). present help-- literally, "a help He has been found exceedingly." trouble-- as in Psa 18:7.

entries.title: Psalms 50:14–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-3. The most violent civil commotions are illustrated by the greatest physical commotions.

entries.title: Psalms 50:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. God's favor is denoted by a river (compare Psa 36:8; Zec 14:8; Re 22:1). city of God, the holy place-- His earthly residence, Jerusalem and the temple (compare Psa 2:6; 3:4; 20:2; 48:2, &c.). God's favor, like a river whose waters are conducted in channels, is distributed to all parts of His Church. most High-- denoting His supremacy (Psa 17:2).

entries.title: Psalms 50:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. right early-- literally, "at the turn of morning," or change from night to day, a critical time (Psa 30:5; compare Is 37:36).

entries.title: Psalms 50:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. (Compare Psa 46:2). earth melted-- all powers dissolved by His mere word (Psa 75:3; Ho 2:22).

entries.title: Psalms 50:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. with us-- on our side; His presence is terror to our enemies, safety to us. refuge-- high place (Psa 9:9; compare also Psa 24:6, 10).

entries.title: Psalms 50:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. what desolations-- literally, "who hath put desolations," destroying our enemies.

entries.title: Psalms 50:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. The usual weapons of war (Psa 7:12), as well as those using them, are brought to an end.

entries.title: Psalms 50:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Be still, &c.-- literally, "Leave off to oppose Me and vex My people. I am over all for their safety." (Compare Is 2:11; Ep 1:22).

entries.title: Psalms 50:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 47 Psa 47:1-9. Praise is given to God for victory, perhaps that recorded (2Ch 20:20-30); and His dominions over all people, Jews and Gentiles, is asserted. 1. clap ... hands ... people-- literally, "peoples," or "nations" (compare De 32:43; Psa 18:49; 98:9).

entries.title: Psalms 50:26–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-3. His universal sovereignty now exists, and will be made known.

entries.title: Psalms 50:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. He shall ... inheritance-- the heathen to be possessed by His Church (Psa 2:8), as Canaan by the Jews. excellency of Jacob-- literally, "pride," or, that in which he glories (not necessarily, though often, in a bad sense), the privileges of the chosen people-- whom he loved-- His love being the sole cause of granting them.

entries.title: Psalms 50:29–31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-7. God, victorious over His enemies, reascends to heaven, amid the triumphant praises of His people, who celebrate His sovereign dominion. This sovereignty is what the Psalm teaches; hence he adds, sing ... praises with understanding-- literally, "sing and play an instructive (Psalm)." The whole typifies Christ's ascension (compare Psa 68:18).

entries.title: Psalms 50:32–33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. The instruction continued. throne of ... holiness-- or, "holy throne" (see on Psa 2:6; Psa 23:4).

entries.title: Psalms 50:35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 48 Psa 48:1-14. This is a spirited Psalm and song (compare Psa 30:1), having probably been suggested by the same occasion as the foregoing. It sets forth the privileges and blessings of God's spiritual dominion as the terror of the wicked and joy of the righteous. 1. to be praised-- always: it is an epithet, as in Psa 18:3. mountain of his holiness-- His Church (compare Is 2:2, 3; 25:6, 7, 10); the sanctuary was erected first on Mount Zion, then (as the temple) on Moriah; hence the figure.

entries.title: Psalms 50:36–37
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-3. situation-- literally, "elevation." joy of, &c.-- source of joy. sides of the north-- poetically for eminent, lofty, distinguished, as the ancients believed the north to be the highest part of the earth (compare Is 14:13).

entries.title: Psalms 50:38–40
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-6. For-- The reason is given. Though the kings (perhaps of Moab and Ammon, compare Psa 83:3-5) combined, a conviction of God's presence with His people, evinced by the unusual courage with which the prophets (compare 2Ch 20:12-20) had inspired them, seized on their minds, and smitten with sudden and intense alarm, they fled astonished.

entries.title: Psalms 50:41
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. ships of Tarshish-- as engaged in a distant and lucrative trade, the most valuable. The phrase may illustrate God's control over all material agencies, whether their literal destruction be meant or not.

entries.title: Psalms 50:42
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. This present experience assures of that perpetual care which God extends to His Church.

entries.title: Psalms 50:43
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. thought of-- literally, "compared," or considered, in respect of former dealings. in the ... temple-- in acts of solemn worship (compare 2Ch 20:28).

entries.title: Psalms 51 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. According ... praise-- that is, As Thy perfections manifested (compare Psa 8:1; 20:1-7), demand praise, it shall be given, everywhere. thy right hand, &c.-- Thy righteous government is displayed by Thy power.

entries.title: Psalms 51:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. the daughters, &c.-- the small towns, or the people, with the chief city, or rulers of the Church. judgments-- decisions and acts of right government.

entries.title: Psalms 51:2–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-14. The call to survey Zion, or the Church, as a fortified city, is designed to suggest "how well our God secures His fold." This security is perpetual, and its pledge is His guidance through this life.

entries.title: Psalms 51:6–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 49 Psa 49:1-20. This Psalm instructs and consoles. It teaches that earthly advantages are not reliable for permanent happiness, and that, however prosperous worldly men may be for a time, their ultimate destiny is ruin, while the pious are safe in God's care. 1-3. All are called to hear what interests all. world-- literally, "duration of life," the present time.

entries.title: Psalms 51:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. incline-- to hear attentively (Psa 17:6; 31:2). parable-- In Hebrew and Greek "parable" and "proverb" are translations of the same word. It denotes a comparison, or form of speech, which under one image includes many, and is expressive of a general truth capable of various illustrations. Hence it may be used for the illustration itself. For the former sense, "proverb" (that is, one word for several) is the usual English term, and for the latter, in which comparison is prominent, "parable" (that is, one thing laid by another). The distinction is not always observed, since here, and in Psa 78:2; "proverb" would better express the style of the composition (compare also Pr 26:7, 9; Ha 2:6; Joh 16:25, 29). Such forms of speech are often very figurative and also obscure (compare Mt 13:12-15). Hence the use of the parallel word-- dark saying-- or, "riddle" (compare Eze 17:2). open-- is to explain. upon the harp-- the accompaniment for a lyric.

entries.title: Psalms 51:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. iniquity-- or, "calamity" (Psa 40:12). of my heels-- literally "my supplanters" (Ge 27:36), or oppressors: "I am surrounded by the evils they inflict."

entries.title: Psalms 51:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. They are vainglorious.

entries.title: Psalms 52 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-9. yet unable to save themselves or others.

entries.title: Psalms 52:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. For he seeth-- that is, corruption; then follows the illustration. wise ... fool-- (Psa 14:1; Pr 1:32; 10:1). likewise-- alike altogether--(Psa 4:8)--die--all meet the same fate.

entries.title: Psalms 52:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. Still infatuated and flattered with hopes of perpetuity, they call their lands, or "celebrate their names on account of (their) lands."

entries.title: Psalms 52:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. Contrasted with this vanity is their frailty. However honored, man abideth not-- literally, "lodgeth not," remains not till morning, but suddenly perishes as (wild) beasts, whose lives are taken without warning.

entries.title: Psalms 52:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. Though their way is folly, others follow the same course of life.

entries.title: Psalms 52:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. Like sheep-- (compare Psa 49:12) unwittingly, they are laid-- or, "put," &c. death shall feed on-- or, better, "shall rule" them-- as a shepherd (compare "feed," Psa 28:9, Margin). have dominion over-- or, "subdue" them in the morning-- suddenly, or in their turn. their beauty-- literally, "form" or shape. shall consume-- literally, "is for the consumption," that is, of the grave. from their dwelling-- literally, "from their home (they go) to it," that is, the grave.

entries.title: Psalms 52:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. The pious, delivered from "the power of the grave." power-- literally, "the hand," of death, are taken under God's care.

entries.title: Psalms 52:9–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-19. applies this instruction. Be not anxious (Psa 37:1, &c.), since death cuts off the prosperous wicked whom you dread.

entries.title: Psalms 52:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. (Compare Psa 49:12). The folly is more distinctly expressed by "understandeth not," substituted for "abideth not."

entries.title: Psalms 53:1–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 50 Psa 50:1-23. In the grandeur and solemnity of a divine judgment, God is introduced as instructing men in the nature of true worship, exposing hypocrisy, warning the wicked, and encouraging the pious. 1-4. The description of this majestic appearance of God resembles that of His giving the law (compare Ex 19:16; 20:18; De 32:1).

entries.title: Psalms 53:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. my saints-- (Psa 4:3). made-- literally, "cut" a covenant, &c.-- alluding to the dividing of a victim of sacrifice, by which covenants were ratified, the parties passing between the divided portions (compare Ge 15:10, 18).

entries.title: Psalms 53:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. The inhabitants of heaven, who well know God's character, attest His righteousness as a judge.

entries.title: Psalms 53:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. I will testify-- that is, for failure to worship aught. thy God-- and so, by covenant as well as creation, entitled to a pure worship.

entries.title: Psalms 53:8–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-15. However scrupulous in external worship, it was offered as if they conferred an obligation in giving God His own, and with a degrading view of Him as needing it [Psa 50:9-13]. Reproving them for such foolish and blasphemous notions, He teaches them to offer, or literally, "sacrifice," thanksgiving, and pay, or perform, their vows-- that is, to bring, with the external symbolical service, the homage of the heart, and faith, penitence, and love. To this is added an invitation to seek, and a promise to afford, all needed help in trouble.

entries.title: Psalms 53:16–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-20. the wicked-- that is, the formalists, as now exposed, and who lead vicious lives (compare Ro 2:21, 23). They are unworthy to use even the words of God's law. Their hypocrisy and vice are exposed by illustrations from sins against the seventh, eighth, and ninth commandments.

entries.title: Psalms 53:21–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21-22. God, no longer (even in appearance) disregarding such, exposes their sins and threatens a terrible punishment.

entries.title: Psalms 53:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. offereth praise-- (Psa 50:14), so that the external worship is a true index of the heart. ordereth ... aright-- acts in a straight, right manner, opposed to turning aside (Psa 25:5). In such, pure worship and a pure life evince their true piety, and they will enjoy God's presence and favor.

entries.title: Psalms 53:25–28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 51 Psa 51:1-19. On the occasion, compare 2Sa 11:12. The Psalm illustrates true repentance, in which are comprised conviction, confession, sorrow, prayer for mercy, and purposes of amendment, and it is accompanied by a lively faith. 1-4. A plea for mercy is a confession of guilt. blot out-- as from a register. transgressions-- literally, "rebellions" (Psa 19:13; 32:1).

entries.title: Psalms 53:29–54:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. His guilt was aggravated by his essential, native sinfulness, which is as contrary to God's requisitions of inward purity as are outward sins to those for right conduct.

entries.title: Psalms 54:1–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-12. A series of prayers for forgiveness and purifying. Purge ... hyssop-- The use of this plant in the ritual (Ex 12:22; Nu 19:6, 18) suggests the idea of atonement as prominent here; "purge" refers to vicarious satisfaction (Nu 19:17-20).

entries.title: Psalms 54:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. Then-- Such will be the effect of this gracious work. ways-- of providence and human duty (Psa 18:21, 30; 32:8; Lu 22:32).

entries.title: Psalms 54:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. Deliver-- or, "Free me" (Psa 39:8) from the guilt of murder (2Sa 12:9, 10; Psa 5:6). righteousness-- as in Psa 7:17; 31:1.

entries.title: Psalms 54:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. open ... lips-- by removing my sense of guilt.

entries.title: Psalms 54:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. Praise is better than sacrifice (Psa 50:14), and implying faith, penitence, and love, glorifies God. In true penitents the joys of pardon mingle with sorrow for sin.

entries.title: Psalms 54:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. Do good, &c.-- Visit not my sin on Thy Church. build ... walls-- is to show favor; compare Psa 89:40, for opposite form and idea.

entries.title: Psalms 54:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. God reconciled, material sacrifices will be acceptable (Psa 4:5; compare Is 1:11-17).

entries.title: Psalms 54:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 52 Psa 52:1-9. Compare 1Sa 21:1-10; 22:1-10, for the history of the title. Psa 52:1 gives the theme; the boast of the wicked over the righteous is vain, for God constantly cares for His people. This is expanded by describing the malice and deceit, and then the ruin, of the wicked, and the happy state of the pious. 1. mighty man-- literally, "hero." Doeg may be thus addressed, ironically, in respect of his might in slander.

entries.title: Psalms 54:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. tongue-- for self. mischiefs-- evil to others (Psa 5:9; 38:12). working deceitfully-- (Psa 10:7), as a keen, smoothly moving razor, cutting quietly, but deeply.

entries.title: Psalms 54:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. all-devouring-- literally, "swallowing," which utterly destroy (compare Psa 21:9; 35:25).

entries.title: Psalms 54:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. likewise-- or, "so," "also," as you have done to others God will do to you (Psa 18:27). The following terms describe the most entire ruin.

entries.title: Psalms 54:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. shall ... fear-- regard with religious awe. laugh at him-- for his folly;

entries.title: Psalms 54:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. for trusting in riches and being strong in "wickedness." wickedness-- literally, "mischief" (Psa 52:2), instead of trusting in God. the man-- literally, "the mighty man," or "hero" (Psa 52:1).

entries.title: Psalms 54:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. The figure used is common (Psa 1:3; Jr 11:16). green-- fresh. house, &c.-- in communion with God (compare Psa 27:4, 5). for ever and ever-- qualifies "mercy."

entries.title: Psalms 54:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. hast done-- that is, what the context supplies, "preserved me" (compare Psa 22:31). wait ... name-- hope in Thy perfections, manifested for my good (Psa 5:11; 20:1). for it is good-- that is, Thy name, and the whole method or result of its manifestation (Psa 54:6; 69:16).

entries.title: Psalms 54:25–28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 53 Psa 53:1-6. Upon Mahalath--(See on Psa 88:1, title). Why this repetition of the fourteenth Psalm is given we do not know. 1-4. with few verbal changes, correspond with Psa 14:1-4.

entries.title: Psalms 54:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. Instead of assurances of God's presence with the pious, and a complaint of the wicked, Psa 14:5, 6 portrays the ruin of the latter, whose "bones" even "are scattered" (compare Psa 141:7), and who are put to shame as contemptuously rejected of God.

entries.title: Psalms 54:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 54 Psa 54:1-7. See on Psa 4:1, title; Psa 32:1, title; for the history, see 1Sa 23:19, 29; 26:1-25. After an earnest cry for help, the Psalmist promises praise in the assurance of a hearing. 1. by thy name-- (Psa 5:11), specially, power. judge me-- as in Psa 7:8; 26:1.

entries.title: Psalms 54:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. (Compare Psa 4:1; 5:1).

entries.title: Psalms 54:34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. strangers-- perhaps Ziphites. oppressors-- literally, "terrible ones" (Is 13:11; 25:3). Such were Saul and his army. not set ... them-- acted as atheists, without God's fear (compare Psa 16:8).

entries.title: Psalms 54:35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. (Compare Psa 30:10). with them-- on their side, and for me (compare Psa 46:11).

entries.title: Psalms 55 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. He shall ... evil-- or, "Evil shall return on" (Psa 7:16) my enemies or watchers, that is, to do me evil (Psa 6:7). in thy truth-- Thy verified promise.

entries.title: Psalms 55:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. I will freely, &c.-- or, present a freewill offering (Le 7:16; Nu 15:3).

entries.title: Psalms 55:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. mine eye ... desire-- (compare Psa 59:10; 112:8), expresses satisfaction in beholding the overthrow of his enemies as those of God, without implying any selfish or unholy feeling (compare Psa 52:6, 7).

entries.title: Psalms 55:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 55 Psa 55:1-23. In great terror on account of enemies, and grieved by the treachery of a friend, the Psalmist offers an earnest prayer for relief. He mingles confident assurances of divine favor to himself with invocations and predictions of God's avenging judgments on the wicked. The tone suits David's experience, both in the times of Saul and Absalom, though perhaps neither was exclusively before his mind. 1. hide not thyself, &c.-- (compare Psa 13:1; 27:9), withhold not help.

entries.title: Psalms 55:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The terms of the last clause express full indulgence of grief.

entries.title: Psalms 55:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. oppression-- literally, "persecution." they ... iniquity-- literally, "they make evil doings slide upon me."

entries.title: Psalms 55:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. express great alarm.

entries.title: Psalms 55:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. be at rest-- literally, "dwell," that is, permanently.

entries.title: Psalms 55:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. Even a wilderness is a safer place than exposure to such evils, terrible as storm and tempest.

entries.title: Psalms 56 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Destroy-- literally, "swallow" (Psa 21:9). divide their tongues-- or, "confound their speech," and hence their counsels (Ge 11:7). the city-- perhaps Jerusalem, the scene of anarchy.

entries.title: Psalms 56:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. which is described in detail (compare Psa 7:14-16).

entries.title: Psalms 56:3–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-14. This description of treachery does not deny, but aggravates, the injury from enemies.

entries.title: Psalms 56:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. Let death, &c.-- or, "Desolations are on them." let them go-- literally, "they will go." quick-- or, living in the midst of life, death will come (compare Nu 16:33). among them-- or, "within them," in their hearts (Psa 5:9; 49:11).

entries.title: Psalms 56:7–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-18. God answers his constant and repeated prayers.

entries.title: Psalms 56:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. God hears the wicked in wrath. abideth-- or, "sitteth." of old-- enthroned as a sovereign. Because ... no changes-- Prosperity hardens them (Psa 73:5).

entries.title: Psalms 56:11–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20-21. The treachery is aggravated by hypocrisy. The changes of number, Psa 55:15, 23, and here, enliven the picture, and imply that the chief traitor and his accomplices are in view together.

entries.title: Psalms 56:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
