entries.content: 22. thy burden-- literally, "gift," what is assigned you. he shall sustain-- literally, "supply food," and so all need (Psa 37:25; Mt 6:11). to be moved-- from the secure position of His favor (compare Psa 10:6).

entries.title: Psalms 56:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. bloody ... days-- (compare Psa 5:6; 51:14), deceit and murderous dispositions often united. The threat is directed specially (not as a general truth) against the wicked, then in the writer's view.

entries.title: Psalms 56:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 56 Psa 56:1-13. Upon Jonath-elem-rechokim--literally, "upon the dove of silence" of distant places; either denoting a melody (see on Psa 9:1) of that name, to which this Psalm was to be performed; or it is an enigmatical form of denoting the subject, as given in the history referred to (1Sa 21:11, &c.), David being regarded as an uncomplaining, meek dove, driven from his native home to wander in exile. Beset by domestic and foreign foes, David appeals confidently to God, recites his complaints, and closes with joyful and assured anticipations of God's continued help. 1-2. would swallow-- literally, "pants as a raging beast" (Ac 9:1).

entries.title: Psalms 56:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. in-- or literally, "unto." thee-- to whom he turns in trouble.

entries.title: Psalms 56:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. in God ... his word-- By His grace or aid (Psa 60:12; 108:13), or, "I will boast in God as to His word"; in either case His word is the special matter and cause of praise. flesh-- for mankind (Psa 65:2; Is 31:3), intimating frailty.

entries.title: Psalms 56:20–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. A vivid picture of the conduct of malicious enemies.

entries.title: Psalms 56:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. Shall they escape? &c.-- or better, "Their escape is by iniquity." cast ... people-- humble those who so proudly oppose Thy servant.

entries.title: Psalms 56:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. God is mindful of his exile and remembers his tears. The custom of bottling the tears of mourners as a memorial, which has existed in some Eastern nations, may explain the figure.

entries.title: Psalms 56:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. God is for me-- or, "on my side" (Psa 118:6; 124:1, 2); hence he is sure of the repulse of his foes.

entries.title: Psalms 56:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. I will render praises-- will pay what I have vowed.

entries.title: Psalms 56:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. The question implies an affirmative answer, drawn from past experience. falling-- as from a precipice. before God-- in His favor during life.

entries.title: Psalms 56:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 57 Psa 57:1-11. Altaschith--or, "Destroy not." This is perhaps an enigmatical allusion to the critical circumstances connected with the history, for which compare 1Sa 22:1; 26:1-3. In Moses' prayer (De 9:26) it is a prominent petition deprecating God's anger against the people. This explanation suits the fifty-eighth and fifty-ninth also. Asaph uses it for the seventy-fifth, in the scope of which there is allusion to some emergency. Michtam--(See on Psa 16:1, title). To an earnest cry for divine aid, the Psalmist adds, as often, the language of praise, in the assured hope of a favorable hearing. 1. my soul-- or self, or life, which is threatened. shadow of thy wings-- (Psa 17:8; 36:7). calamities-- literally, "mischiefs" (Psa 52:2; 55:10).

entries.title: Psalms 56:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. performeth-- or, completes what He has begun.

entries.title: Psalms 56:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. from ... swallow me up-- that pants in rage after me (Psa 56:2). mercy and ... truth-- (Psa 25:10; 36:5), as messengers (Psa 43:3) sent to deliver him.

entries.title: Psalms 56:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. The mingled figures of wild beasts (Psa 10:9; 17:12) and weapons of war (Psa 11:2) heighten the picture of danger. whose ... tongue-- or slanders.

entries.title: Psalms 56:34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. This doxology illustrates his view of the connection of his deliverance with God's glory.

entries.title: Psalms 56:35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 56:36
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. I will ... praise-- both with voice and instrument.

entries.title: Psalms 56:37
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Hence-- he addresses his glory, or tongue (Psa 16:9; 30:12), and his psaltery, or lute, and harp. I myself ... early-- literally, "I will awaken dawn," poetically expressing his zeal and diligence.

entries.title: Psalms 56:38–39
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. As His mercy and truth, so shall His praise, fill the universe.

entries.title: Psalms 56:42
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 58 Psa 58:1-11. David's critical condition in some period of the Sauline persecution probably occasioned this Psalm, in which the Psalmist teaches that the innate and actual sinfulness of men deserves, and shall receive, God's righteous vengeance, while the pious may be consoled by the evidence of His wise and holy government of men. 1. O congregation-- literally, "Oh, dumb"; the word used is never translated "congregation." "Are ye dumb? ye should speak righteousness," may be the translation. In any case, the writer remonstrates with them, perhaps a council, who were assembled to try his cause, and bound to give a right decision.

entries.title: Psalms 56:43
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. This they did not design; but weigh ... violence-- or give decisions of violence. Weigh is a figure to express the acts of judges. in the earth-- publicly.

entries.title: Psalms 57 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-5. describe the wicked generally, who sin naturally, easily, malignantly, and stubbornly.

entries.title: Psalms 57:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. He prays for their destruction, under the figure of ravenous beasts (Psa 3:7; 7:2).

entries.title: Psalms 57:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. which run continually-- literally, "they shall go to themselves," utterly depart, as rapid mountain torrents. he bendeth ... his arrows-- prepares it. The term for preparing a bow applied to arrows (Psa 64:3). let them ... pieces-- literally, "as if they cut themselves off"--that is, become blunted and of no avail.

entries.title: Psalms 57:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. Other figures of this utter ruin; the last denoting rapidity. In a shorter time than pots feel the heat of thorns on fire--

entries.title: Psalms 57:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. wash ... wicked-- denoting great slaughter. The joy of triumph over the destruction of the wicked is because they are God's enemies, and their overthrow shows that He reigneth (compare Psa 52:5-7; 54:7). In this assurance let heaven and earth rejoice (Psa 96:10; 97:1, &c.).

entries.title: Psalms 58:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 59 Psa 59:1-17. See on Psa 57:1, title, and for history, 1Sa 19:11, &c. The scope is very similar to that of the fifty-seventh: prayer in view of malicious and violent foes, and joy in prospect of relief. 1. defend me-- (Compare Margin). rise up ... me-- (Compare Psa 17:7).

entries.title: Psalms 58:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. (Compare Psa 5:5; 6:8).

entries.title: Psalms 58:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. prepare, &c.-- literally, "set themselves as in array." awake-- (Compare Psa 3:7; 7:6), appeals to God in His covenant relation to His people (Psa 9:18).

entries.title: Psalms 58:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. They are as ravening dogs seeking prey, and as such, belch out-- that is, slanders, their impudent barkings.

entries.title: Psalms 58:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 58:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. By judicious expositors, and on good grounds, this is better rendered, "O my strength, on Thee will I wait" (Psa 59:17). defence-- (Compare Psa 18:3).

entries.title: Psalms 58:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. prevent me-- (Psa 21:3). see my desire-- in their overthrow (Psa 54:7). enemies-- as in Psa 5:8.

entries.title: Psalms 58:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. Slay them not-- at once (Jud 2:21-23); but perpetuate their punishment (Ge 4:12; Nu 32:13), by scattering or making them wander, and humble them.

entries.title: Psalms 58:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. let them even be ... taken in their pride-- while evincing it--that is, to be punished for their lies, &c.

entries.title: Psalms 58:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. Though delayed for wise reasons, the utter destruction of the wicked must come at last, and God's presence and power in and for His Church will be known abroad (1Sa 17:46; Psa 46:10, 11).

entries.title: Psalms 58:14–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. Meanwhile let the rapacious dogs prowl, they cannot hurt the pious; yea, they shall wander famished and sleepless.

entries.title: Psalms 58:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-17. Contrast the lot of God's servant, who employs his time in God's praise. sing aloud ... in the morning-- when they retire famishing and disappointed, or it may denote delightful diligence in praise, as in Psa 30:5.

entries.title: Psalms 58:19–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 60 Psa 60:1-12. Shushan-eduth--Lily of testimony. The lily is an emblem of beauty (see on Psa 45:1, title). As a description of the Psalm, those terms combined may denote a beautiful poem, witnessing--that is, for God's faithfulness as evinced in the victories referred to in the history cited. Aram-naharaim--Syria of the two rivers, or Mesopotamia beyond the river (Euphrates) (2Sa 10:16). Aram-zobah--Syria of Zobah (2Sa 10:6), to whose king the king of the former was tributary. The war with Edom, by Joab and Abishai (2Ch 18:12, 25), occurred about the same time. Probably, while doubts and fears alternately prevailed respecting the issue of these wars, the writer composed this Psalm, in which he depicts, in the language of God's people, their sorrows under former disasters, offers prayer in present straits, and rejoices in confident hope of triumph by God's aid. 1-3. allude to disasters. cast ... off-- in scorn (Psa 43:2; 44:9). scattered-- broken our strength (compare 2Sa 5:20). Oh, turn thyself-- or, "restore to us" (prosperity). The figures of physical, denote great civil, commotions (Psa 46:2, 3).

entries.title: Psalms 58:22–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. Yet to God's banner they will rally, and pray that, led and sustained by His power (right hand, Psa 17:7; 20:6), they may be safe.

entries.title: Psalms 58:24–28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-10. God hath spoken in-- or, "by." his holiness-- (Psa 89:35; Am 4:2), on the pledge of His attributes (Psa 22:3; 30:4). Taking courage from God's promise to give them possession (Ex 23:31; De 11:24) (and perhaps renewed to him by special revelation), with triumphant joy he describes the conquest as already made. Shechem, and ... Succoth-- as widely separated points, and--

entries.title: Psalms 58:29–30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. Hence he closes with a prayer for success, and an assurance of a hearing.

entries.title: Psalms 59 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 61 Psa 61:1-8. Neginah--or, Neginoth (see on Psa 4:1, title). Separated from his usual spiritual privileges, perhaps by Absalom's rebellion, the Psalmist prays for divine aid, and, in view of past mercies, with great confidence of being heard. 1-3. From the end-- that is, places remote from the sanctuary (De 28:64).

entries.title: Psalms 59:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. I will abide-- So I desire to do (compare Psa 23:6). trust in the covert, &c.-- make my refuge, in the shadow (compare Psa 17:8; 36:7).

entries.title: Psalms 59:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. the heritage-- or, part in the spiritual blessings of Israel (Psa 21:2-4). vows-- implies prayers.

entries.title: Psalms 59:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. the king-- himself and his royal line ending in Christ. Mercy and truth personified, as in Psa 40:11; 57:3.

entries.title: Psalms 60 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Thus for new blessings will new vows of praise ever be paid.

entries.title: Psalms 60:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 62 Psa 62:1-12. To Jeduthun--(See on Psa 39:1, title). The general tone of this Psalm is expressive of confidence in God. Occasion is taken to remind the wicked of their sin, their ruin, and their meanness. 1. waiteth-- literally, "is silent," trusts submissively and confidently as a servant.

entries.title: Psalms 60:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The titles applied to God often occur (Psa 9:9; 18:2). be greatly moved-- (Psa 10:6). No injury shall be permanent, though devised by enemies.

entries.title: Psalms 60:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Their destruction will come; as a tottering wall they already are feeble and failing. bowing wall shall ye be-- better supply "are." Some propose to apply these phrases to describe the condition of "a man"--that is, the pious suffer: thus, "Will ye slay him," &c.; but the other is a good sense.

entries.title: Psalms 60:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. his excellency-- or, elevation to which God had raised him (Psa 4:2). This they try to do by lies and duplicity (Psa 5:9).

entries.title: Psalms 60:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. (Compare Psa 62:1, 2).

entries.title: Psalms 60:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. rock of my strength-- or strongest support (Psa 7:10; 61:3).

entries.title: Psalms 60:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. pour out your heart-- give full expression to feeling (1Sa 1:15; Job 30:16; Psa 42:4). ye people-- God's people.

entries.title: Psalms 60:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. No kind of men are reliable, compared with God (Is 2:22; Jr 17:5). altogether-- alike, one as the other (Psa 34:3).

entries.title: Psalms 60:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Not only are oppression and robbery, which are wicked means of wealth, no grounds of boasting; but even wealth, increasing lawfully, ought not to engross the heart.

entries.title: Psalms 60:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. once; twice-- (as in Job 33:14; 40:5), are used to give emphasis to the sentiment. God's power is tempered by His mercy, which it also sustains.

entries.title: Psalms 60:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. for thou renderest-- literally, "that Thou renderest," &c., connected with "I heard this," as the phrase--"that power," &c. [Psa 62:11]--teaching that by His power He can show both mercy and justice.

entries.title: Psalms 60:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 63 Psa 63:1-11. The historical occasion referred to by the title was probably during Absalom's rebellion (compare 2Sa 15:23, 28; 16:2). David expresses an earnest desire for God's favor, and a confident expectation of realizing it in his deliverance and the ruin of his enemies. 1. early ... seek thee-- earnestly (Is 26:9). The figurative terms-- dry and thirsty-- literally, "weary," denoting moral destitution, suited his outward circumstances. soul-- and--flesh--the whole man (Psa 16:9, 10).

entries.title: Psalms 60:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The special object of desire was God's perfections as displayed in his worship (Psa 27:4).

entries.title: Psalms 60:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Experiencing God's mercy, which exceeds all the blessings of life, his lips will be opened for his praise (Psa 51:15).

entries.title: Psalms 60:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Thus-- literally, "Truly." will I bless-- praise Thee (Psa 34:1). lift up my hands-- in worship (compare Psa 28:2). in thy name-- in praise of Thy perfections.

entries.title: Psalms 60:19–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-8. Full spiritual blessings satisfy his desires, and acts of praise fill his thoughts and time.

entries.title: Psalms 60:23–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. those ... to destroy it-- or literally, "to ruin," or, "for ruin"; that is, such as seek to injure me (are) for ruin, appointed to it (compare Psa 35:8). shall go ... earth-- into the grave, or, to death; as their bodies are represented as a portion for--

entries.title: Psalms 60:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. the king-- that is, David himself, and all who reverence God, "shall share a glorious part," while treacherous foes shall be for ever silenced (Psa 62:4).

entries.title: Psalms 60:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 64 Psa 64:1-10. A prayer for deliverance from cunning and malicious enemies, with a confident view of their overthrow, which will honor God and give joy to the righteous. 1. preserve ... fear-- as well as the danger producing it.

entries.title: Psalms 60:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. insurrection-- literally, "uproar," noisy assaults, as well as their secret counsels.

entries.title: Psalms 60:29–61:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. Similar figures for slander (Psa 57:4; 59:7). bend-- literally, "tread," or, "prepared." The allusion is to the mode of bending a bow by treading on it; here, and in Psa 58:7, transferred to arrows.

entries.title: Psalms 61:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. A sentiment here more fully presented, by depicting their deliberate malice.

entries.title: Psalms 61:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. This is further evinced by their diligent efforts and deeply laid schemes.

entries.title: Psalms 61:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. The contrast is heightened by representing God as using weapons like theirs.

entries.title: Psalms 61:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. their ... tongue to fall, &c.-- that is, the consequences of their slanders, &c. (compare Psa 10:2; 31:16). all that see ... away-- Their partners in evil shall be terrified.

entries.title: Psalms 61:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. Men, generally, will acknowledge God's work, and the righteous, rejoicing in it, shall be encouraged to trust Him (Psa 58:10).

entries.title: Psalms 61:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 65 Psa 65:1-13. This is a song of praise for God's spiritual blessings to His people and His kind providence over all the earth. 1. Praise waiteth for thee-- literally, "To Thee silence praise," or (compare Psa 62:1), To Thee silence is praise--that is, Praise is waiting as a servant; it is due to Thee. So the last clause expresses the duty of paying vows. These two parts of acceptable worship, mentioned in Psa 50:14, are rendered in Zion, where God chiefly displays His mercy and receives homage.

entries.title: Psalms 61:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. All are encouraged to pray by God's readiness to hear.

entries.title: Psalms 61:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. God's mercy alone delivers us from the burden of iniquities, by purging or expiating by an atonement the transgressions with which we are charged, and which are denoted by-- Iniquities-- or, literally, "Words of iniquities."

entries.title: Psalms 61:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. dwell in thy courts; ... [and] satisfied with the goodness ... temple-- denote communion with God (Psa 15:1; 23:6; compare Psa 5:7). This is a blessing for all God's people, as denoted by the change of number.

entries.title: Psalms 61:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. terrible things-- that is, by the manifestation of justice and wrath to enemies, accompanying that of mercy to His people (Psa 63:9-11; 64:7-9). the confidence-- object of it. of all ... earth-- the whole world; that is, deservedly such, whether men think so or not.

entries.title: Psalms 61:13–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-13. God's great power and goodness are the grounds of this confidence. These are illustrated in His control of the mightiest agencies of nature and nations affecting men with awe and dread (Psa 26:7; 98:1, &c.), and in His fertilizing showers, causing the earth to produce abundantly for man and beast.

entries.title: Psalms 62 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 66 Psa 66:1-20. The writer invites all men to unite in praise, cites some striking occasions for it, promises special acts of thanksgiving, and celebrates God's great mercy. 1. Make ... noise-- or, "Shout."

entries.title: Psalms 62:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. his name-- as in Psa 29:2. make his praise glorious-- literally, "place honor, His praise," or, "as to His praise"; that is, let His praise be such as will glorify Him, or, be honorable to Him.

entries.title: Psalms 62:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. A specimen of the praise. How terrible-- (Compare Psa 65:8). submit-- (Compare Margin), show a forced subjection (Psa 18:44), produced by terror.

entries.title: Psalms 62:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. The terrible works illustrated in Israel's history (Ex 14:21). By this example let rebels be admonished.

entries.title: Psalms 62:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. behold the nations-- watch their conduct.

entries.title: Psalms 62:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. Here is, perhaps, cited a case of recent deliverance.

entries.title: Psalms 62:9–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-12. Out of severe trials, God had brought them to safety (compare Is 48:10; 1Pe 1:7).

entries.title: Psalms 62:12–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-15. These full and varied offerings constitute the payment of vows (Le 22:18-23).

entries.title: Psalms 62:15–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-20. With these he unites his public thanks, inviting those who fear God (Psa 60:4; 61:5, His true worshippers) to hear. He vindicates his sincerity, inasmuch as God would not hear hypocrites, but had heard him.

entries.title: Psalms 63:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 67 Psa 67:1-7. A prayer that, by God's blessing on His people, His salvation and praise may be extended over the earth. 1. cause his face to shine-- show us favor (Nu 6:24, 25; Psa 31:16).

entries.title: Psalms 63:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. thy way-- of gracious dealing (Is 55:8), as explained by-- saving health-- or literally, "salvation."

entries.title: Psalms 63:3–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-5. Thanks will be rendered for the blessings of His wise and holy government (compare Is 2:3, 4; 11:4).

entries.title: Psalms 63:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. The blessings of a fruitful harvest are mentioned as types of greater and spiritual blessings, under which all nations shall fear and love God.

entries.title: Psalms 63:9–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 68 Psa 68:1-35. This is a Psalm-song (see on Psa 30:1, title), perhaps suggested by David's victories, which secured his throne and gave rest to the nation. In general terms, the judgment of God on the wicked, and the equity and goodness of His government to the pious, are celebrated. The sentiment is illustrated by examples of God's dealings, cited from the Jewish history and related in highly poetical terms. Hence the writer intimates an expectation of equal and even greater triumphs and summons all nations to unite in praises of the God of Israel. The Psalm is evidently typical of the relation which God, in the person of His Son, sustains to the Church (compare Psa 68:18). 1-3. Compare Nu 10:35; Psa 1:4; 22:14, on the figures here used. before him-- as in Psa 68:2, from His presence, as dreaded; but in Psa 68:3, in His presence, as under His protection (Psa 61:7).

entries.title: Psalms 63:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. extol him ... heavens-- literally, "cast up for Him who rideth in the deserts," or "wilderness" (compare Psa 68:7), alluding to the poetical representation of His leading His people in the wilderness as a conqueror, before whom a way is to be prepared, or "cast up" (compare Is 40:3; 62:10). by his name JAH-- or, "Jehovah," of which it is a contraction (Ex 15:3; Is 12:2) (Hebrew). name-- or, "perfections" (Psa 9:10; 20:1), which--

entries.title: Psalms 63:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. are illustrated by the protection to the helpless, vindication of the innocent, and punishment of rebels, ascribed to Him.

entries.title: Psalms 63:15–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. (Compare Ex 19:16-18). thou wentest-- in the pillar of fire. thou didst march-- literally, "in Thy tread," Thy majestic movement.

entries.title: Psalms 63:17–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. a plentiful rain-- a rain of gifts, as manna and quails.

entries.title: Psalms 63:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. gave the word-- that is, of triumph. company-- or, choir of females, celebrating victory (Ex 15:20).

entries.title: Psalms 63:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. Kings of armies-- that is, with their armies. she that ... at home-- Mostly women so remained, and the ease of victory appears in that such, without danger, quietly enjoyed the spoils.

entries.title: Psalms 63:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. Some translate this, "When ye shall lie between the borders, ye shall," &c., comparing the peaceful rest in the borders or limits of the promised land to the proverbial beauty of a gentle dove. Others understand by the word rendered "pots," the smoked sides of caves, in which the Israelites took refuge from enemies in the times of the judges; or, taking the whole figuratively, the rows of stones on which cooking vessels were hung; and thus that a contrast is drawn between their former low and afflicted state and their succeeding prosperity. In either case, a state of quiet and peace is described by a beautiful figure.

entries.title: Psalms 63:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. Their enemies dispersed, the contrast of their prosperity with their former distress is represented by that of the snow with the dark and somber shades of Salmon.

entries.title: Psalms 63:23–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-16. Mountains are often symbols of nations (Psa 46:2; 65:6). That of Bashan, northeast of Palestine, denotes a heathen nation, which is described as a "hill of God," or a great hill. Such are represented as envious of the hill (Zion) on which God resides;

entries.title: Psalms 63:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. and, to the assertion of God's purpose to make it His dwelling, is added evidence of His protecting care. He is described as in the midst of His heavenly armies-- thousands of angels-- literally, "thousands of repetitions," or, "thousands of thousands"--that is, of chariots. The word "angels" was perhaps introduced in our version, from De 33:2, and Ga 3:19. They are, of course, implied as conductors of the chariots. as ... Sinai, in the holy place-- that is, He has appeared in Zion as once in Sinai.

entries.title: Psalms 63:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. From the scene of conquest He ascends to His throne, leading-- captivity captive-- or, "many captives captive" (Jud 5:12). received gifts for men-- accepting their homage, even when forced, as that of rebels. that the Lord God might dwell-- or literally, "to dwell, O Lord God" (compare Psa 68:16)--that is, to make this hill, His people or Church, His dwelling. This Psalm typifies the conquests of the Church under her divine leader, Christ. He, indeed, "who was with the Church in the wilderness" (Ac 7:38) is the Lord, described in this ideal ascension. Hence Paul (Ep 4:8) applies this language to describe His real ascension, when, having conquered sin, death, and hell, the Lord of glory triumphantly entered heaven, attended by throngs of adoring angels, to sit on the throne and wield the scepter of an eternal dominion. The phrase "received gifts for (or literally, among) men" is by Paul, "gave gifts to men." Both describe the acts of a conqueror, who receives and distributes spoils. The Psalmist uses "receiving" as evincing the success, Paul "gave" as the act, of the conqueror, who, having subdued his enemies, proceeds to reward his friends. The special application of the passage by Paul was in proof of Christ's exaltation. What the Old Testament represents of His descending and ascending corresponds with His history. He who descended is the same who has ascended. As then ascension was an element of His triumph, so is it now; and He, who, in His humiliation, must be recognized as our vicarious sacrifice and the High Priest of our profession, must also be adored as Head of His Church and author of all her spiritual benefits.

entries.title: Psalms 63:27–29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-21. God daily and fully supplies us. The issues or escapes from death are under His control, who is the God that saves us, and destroys His and our enemies.

entries.title: Psalms 63:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. Former examples of God's deliverance are generalized: as He has done, so He will do. from Bashan-- the farthest region; and-- depths of the sea-- the severest afflictions. Out of all, God will bring them. The figures of Psa 68:23 denote the completeness of the conquest, not implying any savage cruelty (compare 2Ki 9:36; Is 63:1-6; Jr 15:3).

entries.title: Psalms 64:1–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24-27. The triumphal procession, after the deliverance, is depicted. They have seen-- impersonally, "There have been seen." the goings of my God-- as leading the procession; the ark, the symbol of His presence, being in front. The various bands of music (Psa 68:25) follow, and all who are--

entries.title: Psalms 64:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28-29. Thanks for the past, and confident prayer for the future victories of Zion are mingled in a song of praise.

entries.title: Psalms 64:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 30. The strongest nations are represented by the strongest beasts (compare Margin).

entries.title: Psalms 64:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 31. Princes-- or, literally, "fat ones," the most eminent from the most wealthy, and the most distant nation, represent the universal subjection. stretch out her hands-- or, "make to run her hands," denoting haste.

entries.title: Psalms 64:9–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 32-36. To Him who is presented as riding in triumph through His ancient heavens and proclaiming His presence-- to Him who, in nature, and still more in the wonders of His spiritual government, out of His holy place (Psa 43:3), is terrible, who rules His Church, and, by His Church, rules the world in righteousness--let all nations and kingdoms give honor and power and dominion evermore.

entries.title: Psalms 64:14–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 69 Psa 69:1-36. Upon Shoshannim--(See on Psa 45:1, title). Mingling the language of prayer and complaint, the sufferer, whose condition is here set forth, pleads for God's help as one suffering in His cause, implores the divine retribution on his malicious enemies, and, viewing his deliverance as sure, promises praise by himself, and others, to whom God will extend like blessings. This Psalm is referred to seven times in the New Testament as prophetical of Christ and the gospel times. Although the character in which the Psalmist appears to some in Psa 69:5 is that of a sinner, yet his condition as a sufferer innocent of alleged crimes sustains the typical character of the composition, and it may be therefore regarded throughout, as the twenty-second, as typically expressive of the feelings of our Saviour in the flesh. 1-2. (Compare Psa 40:2). come in unto my soul-- literally, "come even to my soul," endanger my life by drowning (Jon 2:5).

entries.title: Psalms 64:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. (Compare Psa 6:6). mine eyes fail-- in watching (Psa 119:82).

entries.title: Psalms 64:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. hate me, &c.-- (Compare Joh 15:25). On the number and power of his enemies (compare Psa 40:12). then I restored ... away-- that is, he suffered wrongfully under the imputation of robbery.

entries.title: Psalms 64:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. This may be regarded as an appeal, vindicating his innocence, as if he had said, "If sinful, thou knowest," &c. Though David's condition as a sufferer may typify Christ's, without requiring that a parallel be found in character.

entries.title: Psalms 64:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. for my sake-- literally, "in me," in my confusion and shame.

entries.title: Psalms 64:20–65:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-12. This plea contemplates his relation to God as a sufferer in His cause. Reproach, domestic estrangement (Mr 3:21; Joh 7:5), exhaustion in God's service (Joh 2:17), revilings and taunts of base men were the sufferings.

entries.title: Psalms 65:2–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-15. With increasing reliance on God, he prays for help, describing his distress in the figures of Psa 69:1, 2.

entries.title: Psalms 65:5–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-18. These earnest terms are often used, and the address to God, as indifferent or averse, is found in Psa 3:7; 22:24; 27:9, &c.

entries.title: Psalms 65:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-20. Calling God to witness his distress, he presents its aggravation produced by the want of sympathizing friends (compare Is 63:5; Mr 14:50).

entries.title: Psalms 65:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. Instead of such, his enemies increase his pain by giving him most distasteful food and drink. The Psalmist may have thus described by figure what Christ found in reality (compare Joh 19:29, 30).

entries.title: Psalms 65:11–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22-23. With unimportant verbal changes, this language is used by Paul to describe the rejection of the Jews who refused to receive the Saviour (Ro 11:9, 10). The purport of the figures used is that blessings shall become curses, the "table" of joy (as one of food) a "snare," their welfare-- literally, "peaceful condition," or security, a "trap." Darkened eyes and failing strength complete the picture of the ruin falling on them under the invoked retribution.

entries.title: Psalms 65:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24-25. An utter desolation awaits them. They will not only be driven from their homes, but their homes-- or, literally, "palaces," indicative of wealth--shall be desolate (compare Mt 23:38).

entries.title: Psalms 65:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. Though smitten of God (Is 53:4), men were not less guilty in persecuting the sufferer (Ac 2:23). talk to the grief-- in respect to, about it, implying derision and taunts. wounded-- or, literally, "mortally wounded."

entries.title: Psalms 65:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27-28. iniquity-- or, "punishment of iniquity" (Psa 40:12). come ... righteousness-- partake of its benefits.

entries.title: Psalms 65:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 29. poor and sorrowful-- the afflicted pious, often denoted by such terms (compare Psa 10:17; 12:5). set me ... high-- out of danger.

entries.title: Psalms 65:19–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 30-31. Spiritual are better than mere material offerings (Psa 40:6; 50:8); hence a promise of the former, and rather contemptuous terms are used of the latter.

entries.title: Psalms 65:21–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 32-33. Others shall rejoice. "Humble" and poor, as in Psa 69:29. your heart, &c.-- address to such (compare Psa 22:26).

entries.title: Psalms 65:23–66:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 34-36. The call on the universe for praise is well sustained by the prediction of the perpetual and extended blessings which shall come upon the covenant-people of God. Though, as usual, the imagery is taken from terms used of Palestine, the whole tenor of the context indicates that the spiritual privileges and blessings of the Church are meant.

entries.title: Psalms 66:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 70 Psa 70:1-5. This corresponds to Psa 40:13-17 with a very few variations, as "turn back" (Psa 70:3) for "desolate," and "make haste unto me" (Psa 70:5) for "thinketh upon me." It forms a suitable appendix to the preceding, and is called "a Psalm to bring to remembrance," as the thirty-eighth [see on Psa 38:1, title].

entries.title: Psalms 66:9–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 71 Psa 71:1-24. The Psalmist, probably in old age, appeals to God for help from his enemies, pleading his past favors, and stating his present need; and, in confidence of a hearing, he promises his grateful thanks and praise. 1-3. (Compare Psa 30:1-3).

entries.title: Psalms 66:12–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. cruel man-- corrupt and ill-natured--literally, "sour."

entries.title: Psalms 66:14–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-9. His history from early infancy illustrated God's care, and his wonderful deliverances were at once occasions of praise and ground of confidence for the future. my praise ... of thee-- literally, "in" or "by Thee" (Psa 22:25).

entries.title: Psalms 66:18–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. The craft and malicious taunts of his enemies now led him to call for aid (compare the terms used, 2Sa 17:12; Psa 3:2; 7:2).

entries.title: Psalms 66:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 66:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 66:22–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-16. The ruin of his enemies, as illustrating God's faithfulness, is his deliverance, and a reason for future confidence.

entries.title: Psalms 66:25–67:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-21. Past experience again encourages. taught me, &c.-- by providential dealings.

entries.title: Psalms 67:2–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22-24. To the occasion of praise he now adds the promise to render it. will ... praise-- literally, "will thank." even thy truth-- as to Thy truth or faithfulness.

entries.title: Psalms 67:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 72 Psa 72:1-19. For, or literally, "of Solomon." The closing verse rather relates to the second book of Psalms, of which this is the last, and was perhaps added by some collector, to intimate that the collection, to which, as chief author, David's name was appended, was closed. In this view, these may consistently be the productions of others included, as of Asaph, sons of Korah, and Solomon; and a few of David's may be placed in the latter series. The fact that here the usual mode of denoting authorship is used, is strongly conclusive that Solomon was the author, especially as no stronger objection appears than what has been now set aside. The Psalm, in highly wrought figurative style, describes the reign of a king as "righteous, universal, beneficent, and perpetual." By the older Jewish and most modern Christian interpreters, it has been referred to Christ, whose reign, present and prospective, alone corresponds with its statements. As the imagery of the second Psalm was drawn from the martial character of David's reign, that of this is from the peaceful and prosperous state of Solomon's. 1. Give the king, &c.-- a prayer which is equivalent to a prediction. judgments-- the acts, and (figuratively) the principles of a right government (Joh 5:22; 9:39). righteousness-- qualifications for conducting such a government. king's son-- same person as a king--a very proper title for Christ, as such in both natures. 2, &c. The effects of such a government by one thus endowed are detailed. thy people ... and thy poor-- or, "meek," the pious subjects of his government.

entries.title: Psalms 67:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. As mountains and hills are not usually productive, they are here selected to show the abundance of peace, being represented as bringing-- or, literally, "bearing" it as a produce. by righteousness-- that is, by means of his eminently just and good methods of ruling.

entries.title: Psalms 67:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. That peace, including prosperity, as an eminent characteristic of Christ's reign (Is 2:4; Is 9:6; 11:9), will be illustrated in the security provided for the helpless and needy, and the punishment inflicted on oppressors, whose power to injure or mar the peace of others will be destroyed (compare Is 65:25; Zec 9:10). children of the needy-- for the needy (compare sons of strangers, Psa 18:45 [Margin]).

entries.title: Psalms 67:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. as long as ... endure-- literally, "with the sun," coeval with its existence, and before, or, in presence of the moon, while it lasts (compare Ge 11:28, "before Terah," literally, "in presence of," while he lived).

entries.title: Psalms 67:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. A beautiful figure expresses the grateful nature of His influence; 7, and, carrying out the figure, the results are described in an abundant production. the righteous-- literally, "righteousness." flourish-- literally, "sprout," or, "spring forth."

entries.title: Psalms 67:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. The foreign nations mentioned (Psa 72:9, 10) could not be included in the limits, if designed to indicate the boundaries of Solomon's kingdom. The terms, though derived from those used (Ex 23:31; De 11:24) to denote the possessions of Israel, must have a wider sense. Thus, "ends of the earth" is never used of Palestine, but always of the world (compare Margin).

entries.title: Psalms 67:14–68:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-11. The extent of the conquests. They that dwell in the wilderness-- the wild, untutored tribes of deserts. bow ... dust-- in profound submission. The remotest and wealthiest nations shall acknowledge Him (compare Psa 45:12).

entries.title: Psalms 68:1–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-14. They are not the conquests of arms, but the influences of humane and peaceful principles (compare Is 9:7; 11:1-9; Zec 9:9, 10).

entries.title: Psalms 68:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. In his prolonged life he will continue to receive the honorable gifts of the rich, and the prayers of his people shall be made for him, and their praises given to him.

entries.title: Psalms 68:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. The spiritual blessings, as often in Scripture, are set forth by material, the abundance of which is described by a figure, in which a "handful" (or literally, "a piece," or small portion) of corn in the most unpropitious locality, shall produce a crop, waving in the wind in its luxuriant growth, like the forests of Lebanon. they of the city ... earth-- This clause denotes the rapid and abundant increase of population-- of-- or, "from" the city-- Jerusalem, the center and seat of the typical kingdom. flourish-- or, glitter as new grass--that is, bloom. This increase corresponds with the increased productiveness. So, as the gospel blessings are diffused, there shall arise increasing recipients of them, out of the Church in which Christ resides as head.

entries.title: Psalms 68:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. His name-- or, "glorious perfections." as long as the sun-- (Compare Psa 72:5). men shall be blessed-- (Ge 12:3; 18:18).

entries.title: Psalms 68:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18-19. These words close the Psalm in terms consistent with the style of the context, while Psa 72:20 is evidently, from its prosaic style, an addition for the purpose above explained [see on Psa 72:1].

entries.title: Psalms 68:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. ended-- literally, "finished," or completed; the word never denotes fulfilment, except in a very late usage, as in Ezr 1:1; Da 12:7.

entries.title: Psalms 68:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 73 Psa 73:1-28. Of Asaph--(see Introduction). God is good to His people. For although the prosperity of the wicked, and the afflictions of the righteous, tempted the Psalmist to misgivings of God's government, yet the sudden and fearful ruin of the ungodly, seen in the light of God's revelation, reassures his heart; and, chiding himself for his folly, he is led to confide renewedly in God, and celebrate His goodness and love. 1. The abrupt announcement of the theme indicates that it is the conclusion of a perplexing mental conflict, which is then detailed (compare Jr 12:1-4). Truly-- or, "Surely it is so." clean heart-- (Psa 18:26) describes the true Israel.

entries.title: Psalms 68:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The figures express his wavering faith, by terms denoting tottering and weakness (compare Psa 22:5; 62:3).

entries.title: Psalms 68:13–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-9. The prosperous wicked are insolently proud (compare Psa 5:5). They die, as well as live, free from perplexities: pride adorns them, and violence is their clothing; indeed they are inflated with unexpected success. With all this--

entries.title: Psalms 68:20–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-12. Hence God's people are confounded, turned hither (or back) and thither, perplexed with doubts of God's knowledge and care, and filled with sorrow.

entries.title: Psalms 68:23–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-14. The Psalmist, partaking of these troubles, is especially disturbed in view of his own case, that with all his diligent efforts for a holy life, he is still sorely tried.

entries.title: Psalms 68:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. Freed from idiomatic phrases, this verse expresses a supposition, as, "Had I thus spoken, I should," &c., intimating that he had kept his troubles to himself. generation of thy children-- Thy people (1Jo 3:1). offend-- literally, "deceive, mislead."

entries.title: Psalms 68:26–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-17. Still he-- thought-- literally, "studied," or, "pondered this riddle"; but in vain; it remained a toil (compare Margin), till he--

entries.title: Psalms 68:28–30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18-20. their end-- future (Psa 37:37, 38), which is dismal and terribly sudden (Pr 1:27; 29:1), aggravated and hastened by terror. As one despises an unsubstantial dream, so God, waking up to judgment (Psa 7:6; 44:23), despises their vain shadow of happiness (Psa 39:6; Is 29:7). They are thrown into ruins as a building falling to pieces (Psa 74:3).

entries.title: Psalms 69 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21-22. He confesses how-- foolish-- literally, "stupid," and ignorant-- literally, "not discerning," had been his course of thought.

entries.title: Psalms 69:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. Still he was with God, as a dependent beneficiary, and so kept from falling (Psa 73:2).

entries.title: Psalms 69:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. All doubts are silenced in confidence of divine guidance and future glory. receive me to glory-- literally, "take for (me) glory" (compare Psa 68:18; Ep 4:8).

entries.title: Psalms 69:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25-26. God is his only satisfying good.

entries.title: Psalms 69:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27-28. The lot of apostates, described by a figure of frequent use (Jr 3:1, 3; Eze 23:35), is contrasted with his, who finds happiness in nearness to God (Jas 4:8), and his delightful work the declaration of His praise.

entries.title: Psalms 69:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 74 Psa 74:1-23. If the historical allusions of Psa 74:6-8, &c., be referred, as is probable, to the period of the captivity, the author was probably a descendant and namesake of Asaph, David's contemporary and singer (compare 2Ch 35:15; Ezr 2:41). He complains of God's desertion of His Church, and appeals for aid, encouraging himself by recounting some of God's mighty deeds, and urges his prayer on the ground of God's covenant relation to His people, and the wickedness of His and their common enemy. 1. cast ... off-- with abhorrence (compare Psa 43:2; 44:9). There is no disavowal of guilt implied. The figure of fire to denote God's anger is often used; and here, and in De 29:20, by the word "smoke," suggests its continuance. sheep ... pasture-- (Compare Psa 80:1; 95:7).

entries.title: Psalms 69:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The terms to denote God's relation to His people increase in force: "congregation"-- "purchased"--"redeemed"--"Zion," His dwelling.

entries.title: Psalms 69:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Lift ... feet-- (Ge 29:1)--that is, Come (to behold) the desolations (Psa 73:19).

entries.title: Psalms 69:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. roar-- with bestial fury. congregations-- literally, "worshipping assemblies." ensigns-- literally, "signs"--substituted their idolatrous objects, or tokens of authority, for those articles of the temple which denoted God's presence.

entries.title: Psalms 69:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. Though some terms and clauses here are very obscure, the general sense is that the spoilers destroyed the beauties of the temple with the violence of woodmen. was famous-- literally, "was known."

entries.title: Psalms 69:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. defiled-- or, "profaned," as in Psa 89:39.

entries.title: Psalms 69:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. together-- at once, all alike. synagogues-- literally, "assemblies," for places of assembly, whether such as schools of the prophets (2Ki 4:23), or "synagogues" in the usual sense, there is much doubt.

entries.title: Psalms 69:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. signs-- of God's presence, as altar, ark, &c. (compare Psa 74:4; 2Ch 36:18, 19; Da 5:2). no more any prophet-- (Is 3:2; Jr 40:1; 43:6). how long-- this is to last. Jeremiah's prophecy (Jr 25:11), if published, may not have been generally known or understood. To the bulk of the people, during the captivity, the occasional and local prophetical services of Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Daniel would not make an exception to the clause, "there is no more any prophet."

entries.title: Psalms 69:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. (Compare Psa 31:1). how long ... reproach?-- us, as deserted of God. blaspheme thy name-- or, "perfections," as power, goodness, &c. (Psa 29:2).

entries.title: Psalms 69:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. Why cease to help us? (Compare Psa 3:7; 7:6; 60:5).

entries.title: Psalms 69:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. For-- literally, "And," in an adversative sense.

entries.title: Psalms 69:21–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-15. Examples of the "salvation wrought" are cited. divide the sea-- that is, Red Sea. brakest ... waters-- Pharaoh and his host (compare Is 51:9, 10; Eze 29:3, 4).

entries.title: Psalms 69:24–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-17. The fixed orders of nature and bounds of earth are of God.

entries.title: Psalms 69:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. (Compare Psa 74:10; De 32:6). The contrast is striking-- that such a God should be thus insulted!

entries.title: Psalms 69:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. multitude-- literally, "beast," their flock or company of men (Psa 68:10). turtledove-- that is, the meek and lonely Church. congregation-- literally, "the company," as above--thus the Church is represented as the spoiled and defeated remnant of an army, exposed to violence.

entries.title: Psalms 69:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. And the prevalence of injustice in heathen lands is a reason for invoking God's regard to His promise (compare Nu 14:21; Psa 7:16; 18:48).

entries.title: Psalms 70 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. oppressed-- broken (Psa 9:9). return-- from seeking God. ashamed-- (Psa 35:4).

entries.title: Psalms 70:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22-23. (Compare Psa 3:7; 7:6). God hears the wicked to their own ruin (Ge 4:10; 18:20).

entries.title: Psalms 70:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 75 Psa 75:1-10. Al-taschith--(See on Psa 57:1, title). In impending danger, the Psalmist, anticipating relief in view of God's righteous government, takes courage and renders praise. 1. God's name or perfections are set forth by His wondrous works.

entries.title: Psalms 70:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-3. These verses express the purpose of God to administer a just government, and in a time of anarchy that He sustains the nation. Some apply the words to the Psalmist. receive the congregation-- literally, "take a set time" (Psa 102:13; Ho 2:3), or an assembly at a set time--that is, for judging.

entries.title: Psalms 70:7–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-8. Here the writer speaks in view of God's declaration, warning the wicked. Lift ... up the horn-- to exalt power, here, of the wicked himself--that is, to be arrogant or self-elated.

entries.title: Psalms 70:12–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. Contrasted is the lot of the pious who will praise God, and, acting under His direction, will destroy the power of the wicked, and exalt that of the righteous.

entries.title: Psalms 70:15–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 76 Psa 76:1-12. On Neginoth--(See on Psa 4:1, title). This Psalm commemorates what the preceding anticipates: God's deliverance of His people by a signal interposition of power against their enemies. The occasion was probably the events narrated in 2Ki 19:35; Is 37:1-28. (Compare Psa 46:1-11). 1-2. These well-known terms denote God's people and Church and His intimate and glorious relations to them.

entries.title: Psalms 70:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. brake ... the arrows-- literally, "thunderbolts" (Psa 78:48), from their rapid flight or ignition (compare Psa 18:14; Ep 6:16). the battle-- for arms (Ho 2:18).

entries.title: Psalms 70:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Thou-- God. mountains of prey-- great victorious nations, as Assyria (Is 41:15; Eze 38:11, 12; Zec 4:7).

entries.title: Psalms 71 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. slept their sleep-- died (Psa 13:3). none ... found ... hands-- are powerless.

entries.title: Psalms 71:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. chariot and horse-- for those fighting on them (compare Psa 68:17).

entries.title: Psalms 71:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. may ... sight-- contend with Thee (De 9:4; Jos 7:12).

entries.title: Psalms 71:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. God's judgment on the wicked is His people's deliverance (Psa 9:12; 10:7).

entries.title: Psalms 71:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Man's wrath praises God by its futility before His power. restrain-- or, "gird"; that is, Thyself, as with a sword, with which to destroy, or as an ornament to Thy praise.

entries.title: Psalms 71:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. Invite homage to such a God (2Ch 32:23), who can stop the breath of kings and princes when He wills (Da 5:23).

entries.title: Psalms 72 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 77 Psa 77:1-20. To Jeduthun--(See on Psa 39:1, title). In a time of great affliction, when ready to despair, the Psalmist derives relief from calling to mind God's former and wonderful works of delivering power and grace. 1. expresses the purport of the Psalm.

entries.title: Psalms 72:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. his importunacy. my sore ran ... night-- literally, "my hand was spread," or, "stretched out" (compare Psa 44:20). ceased not-- literally, "grew not numb," or, "feeble" (Ge 45:26; Psa 38:8). my soul ... comforted-- (compare Ge 37:35; Jr 31:15).

entries.title: Psalms 72:2–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-9. His sad state contrasted with former joys. was troubled-- literally, "violently agitated," or disquieted (Psa 39:6; 41:5). my spirit was overwhelmed-- or, "fainted" (Psa 107:5; Jon 2:7).

entries.title: Psalms 72:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Omitting the supplied words, we may read, "This is my affliction-- the years of," &c., "years" being taken as parallel to affliction (compare Psa 90:15), as of God's ordering.

entries.title: Psalms 72:10–73:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. He finds relief in contrasting God's former deliverances. Shall we receive good at His hands, and not evil? Both are orderings of unerring mercy and unfailing love.

entries.title: Psalms 73:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. Thy way ... in the sanctuary-- God's ways of grace and providence (Psa 22:3; 67:2), ordered on holy principles, as developed in His worship; or implied in His perfections, if "holiness" be used for "sanctuary," as some prefer translating (compare Ex 15:11).

entries.title: Psalms 73:2–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-20. Illustrations of God's power in His special interventions for His people (Ex 14:1-31), and, in the more common, but sublime, control of nature (Psa 22:11-14; Ha 3:14) which may have attended those miraculous events (Ex 14:24).

entries.title: Psalms 73:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 78 Psa 78:1-72. This Psalm appears to have been occasioned by the removal of the sanctuary from Shiloh in the tribe of Ephraim to Zion in the tribe of Judah, and the coincident transfer of pre-eminence in Israel from the former to the latter tribe, as clearly evinced by David's settlement as the head of the Church and nation. Though this was the execution of God's purpose, the writer here shows that it also proceeded from the divine judgment on Ephraim, under whose leadership the people had manifested the same sinful and rebellious character which had distinguished their ancestors in Egypt. 1. my people ... my law-- the language of a religious teacher (Psa 78:2; La 3:14; Ro 2:16, 27; compare Psa 49:4). The history which follows was a "dark saying," or riddle, if left unexplained, and its right apprehension required wisdom and attention.

entries.title: Psalms 73:12–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-8. This history had been handed down (Ex 12:14; De 6:20) for God's honor, and that the principles of His law might be known and observed by posterity. This important sentiment is reiterated in (Psa 78:7, 8) negative form.

entries.title: Psalms 73:18–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-11. The privileges of the first-born which belonged to Joseph (1Ch 5:1, 2) were assigned to Ephraim by Jacob (Ge 48:1). The supremacy of the tribe thus intimated was recognized by its position (in the marching of the nation to Canaan) next to the ark (Nu 2:18-24), by the selection of the first permanent locality for the ark within its borders at Shiloh, and by the extensive and fertile province given for its possession. Traces of this prominence remained after the schism under Rehoboam, in the use, by later writers, of Ephraim for Israel (compare Ho 5:3-14; 11:3-12). Though a strong, well-armed tribe, and, from an early period, emulous and haughty (compare Jos 17:14; Jud 8:1-3; 2Sa 19:41), it appears, in this place, that it had rather led the rest in cowardice than courage; and had incurred God's displeasure, because, diffident of His promise, though often heretofore fulfilled, it had failed as a leader to carry out the terms of the covenant, by not driving out the heathen (Ex 23:24; De 31:16; 2Ki 17:15).

entries.title: Psalms 73:21–74:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-14. A record of God's dealings and the sins of the people is now made. The writer gives the history from the exode to the retreat from Kadesh; then contrasts their sins with their reasons for confidence, shown by a detail of God's dealings in Egypt, and presents a summary of the subsequent history to David's time. Zoan-- for Egypt, as its ancient capital (Nu 13:22; Is 19:11).

entries.title: Psalms 74:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-16. There were two similar miracles (Ex 17:6; Nu 20:11). great depths-- and--rivers--denote abundance.

entries.title: Psalms 74:3–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-20. yet more-- literally, "added to sin," instead of being led to repentance (Ro 2:4).

entries.title: Psalms 74:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. fire-- the effect of the "anger" (Nu 11:1).

entries.title: Psalms 74:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. (Compare He 8:8, 9).

entries.title: Psalms 74:9–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 75 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 30-31. not estranged ... lust-- or, "desire"--that is, were indulging it.

entries.title: Psalms 75:3–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 33-39. Though there were partial reformations after chastisement, and God, in pity, withdrew His hand for a time, yet their general conduct was rebellious, and He was thus provoked to waste and destroy them, by long and fruitless wandering in the desert.

entries.title: Psalms 75:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 40-41. There were ten temptations (Nu 14:22).

entries.title: Psalms 75:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 43. wrought-- set or held forth.

entries.title: Psalms 75:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 45. The dog-fly or the mosquito.

entries.title: Psalms 75:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 46. caterpillar-- the Hebrew name, from its voracity, and that of-- locust-- from its multitude.

entries.title: Psalms 75:17–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 47-48. The additional effects of the storm here mentioned (compare Ex 9:23-34) are consistent with Moses' account.

entries.title: Psalms 75:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 49. evil angels-- or, "angels of evil"--many were perhaps employed, and other evils inflicted.

entries.title: Psalms 75:20–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 50-51. made a way-- removed obstacles, gave it full scope.

entries.title: Psalms 75:22–76:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 52-54. made his ... forth-- or, brought them by periodical journeys (compare Ex 15:1).

entries.title: Psalms 76:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 55. by line-- or, the portion thus measured. divided them-- that is, the heathen, put for their possessions, so tents--that is, of the heathen (compare De 6:11).

entries.title: Psalms 76:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 56-57. a deceitful bow-- which turns back, and so fails to project the arrow (2Sa 1:22; Ho 7:16). They relapsed.

entries.title: Psalms 76:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 58. Idolatry resulted from sparing the heathen (compare Psa 78:9-11).

entries.title: Psalms 76:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 59-60. heard-- perceived (Ge 11:7). abhorred-- but not utterly.

entries.title: Psalms 76:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 61. his strength-- the ark, as symbolical of it (Psa 96:6).

entries.title: Psalms 76:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 62. gave-- or, "shut up." his people-- (Psa 78:48; 1Sa 4:10-17).

entries.title: Psalms 76:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 63. fire-- either figure of the slaughter (1Sa 4:10), or a literal burning by the heathen. given to marriage-- literally, "praised"--that is, as brides.

entries.title: Psalms 76:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 64. (Compare 1Sa 4:17); and there were, doubtless, others. made no lamentation-- either because stupefied by grief, or hindered by the enemy.

entries.title: Psalms 76:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 76:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 66. And he smote ... part-- or, "struck His enemies' back." The Philistines never regained their position after their defeats by David.

entries.title: Psalms 76:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 67-68. tabernacle of Joseph-- or, "home," or, "tribe," to which-- tribe of Ephraim-- is parallel (compare Re 7:8). Its pre-eminence was, like Saul's, only permitted. Judah had been the choice (Ge 49:10).

entries.title: Psalms 76:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 69. Exalted as-- high palaces-- or, "mountains," and abiding as--the earth.

entries.title: Psalms 76:16–77:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 70-72. God's sovereignty was illustrated in this choice. The contrast is striking-- humility and exaltation--and the correspondence is beautiful.

entries.title: Psalms 77:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 79 Psa 79:1-13. This Psalm, like the seventy-fourth, probably depicts the desolations of the Chaldeans (Jr 52:12-24). It comprises the usual complaint, prayer, and promised thanks for relief. 1. (Compare Psa 74:2-7).

entries.title: Psalms 77:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 77:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 77:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. How long-- (Psa 13:1). be angry-- (Psa 74:1-10). jealousy burn-- (De 29:20).

entries.title: Psalms 77:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. (Compare Jr 10:25). Though we deserve much, do not the heathen deserve more for their violence to us (Jr 51:3-5; Zec 1:14)? The singular denotes the chief power, and the use of the plural indicates the combined confederates. called upon-- or, "by" thy name-- proclaimed Thy attributes and professed allegiance (Is 12:4; Ac 2:21).

entries.title: Psalms 77:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. former iniquities-- literally, "iniquities of former times." prevent us-- literally, "meet us," as in Psa 21:3.

entries.title: Psalms 77:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. for ... glory of thy name [and for] thy name's sake-- both mean for illustrating Thy attributes, faithfulness, power, &c. purge ... sins-- literally, "provide atonement for us." Deliverance from sin and suffering, for their good and God's glory, often distinguish the prayers of Old Testament saints (compare Ep 1:7).

entries.title: Psalms 77:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. This ground of pleading often used (Ex 32:12; Nu 14:13-16). blood ... shed-- (Psa 79:3).

entries.title: Psalms 77:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. prisoner-- the whole captive people. power-- literally, "arm" (Psa 10:15).

entries.title: Psalms 77:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. into their bosom-- The lap or folds of the dress is used by Eastern people for receiving articles. The figure denotes retaliation (compare Is 65:6, 7). They reproached God as well as His people.

entries.title: Psalms 77:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. sheep ... pasture-- (Compare Psa 74:1; 78:70).

entries.title: Psalms 77:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 80 Psa 80:1-19. Shoshannim--"Lilies" (see on Psa 45:1, title). Eduth--Testimony, referring to the topic as a testimony of God to His people (compare Psa 19:7). This Psalm probably relates to the captivity of the ten tribes, as the former to that of Judah. Its complaint is aggravated by the contrast of former prosperity, and the prayer for relief occurs as a refrain through the Psalm. 1-2. Joseph-- for Ephraim (1Ch 7:20-29; Psa 78:67; Re 7:8), for Israel. Shepherd-- (Compare Ge 49:24). leadest, &c.-- (Psa 77:20). dwellest ... cherubim-- (Ex 25:20); the place of God's visible glory, whence He communed with the people (He 9:5). shine forth-- appear (Psa 50:2; 94:1).

entries.title: Psalms 77:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Turn us-- that is, from captivity. thy face to shine-- (Nu 6:25).

entries.title: Psalms 77:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. be angry-- (Compare Margin.)

entries.title: Psalms 77:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. bread of tears-- still an Eastern figure for affliction.

entries.title: Psalms 78 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. strife-- object or cause of (Is 9:11). On last clause compare Psa 79:4; Eze 36:4.

entries.title: Psalms 78:2–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-11. brought-- or, "plucked up," as by roots, to be replanted. a vine-- (Psa 78:47). The figure (Is 16:8) represents the flourishing state of Israel, as predicted (Ge 28:14), and verified (1Ki 4:20-25).

entries.title: Psalms 78:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 78:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. The boar-- may represent the ravaging Assyrian and the wild beast-- other heathen.

entries.title: Psalms 78:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. visit this vine-- favorably (Psa 8:4).

entries.title: Psalms 78:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
