entries.content: 16. it-- the "vine" or they-- the "people" are suffering from Thy displeasure.

entries.title: Psalms 78:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. thy hand ... upon-- that is, strengthen (Ezr 7:6; 8:22). man of ... hand-- may allude to Benjamin (Ge 35:18). The terms in the latter clause correspond with those of Psa 80:15, from "and the branch," &c., literally, and confirm the exposition given above.

entries.title: Psalms 78:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. We need quickening grace (Psa 71:20; 119:25) to persevere in Thy right worship (Ge 4:26; Ro 10:11).

entries.title: Psalms 78:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. (Compare Psa 80:3, "O God"; Psa 80:7, "O God of hosts").

entries.title: Psalms 78:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 81 Psa 81:1-16. Gittith--(See on Psa 8:1, title). A festal Psalm, probably for the passover (compare Mt 26:30), in which, after an exhortation to praise God, He is introduced, reminding Israel of their obligations, chiding their neglect, and depicting the happy results of obedience. 1. our strength-- (Psa 38:7).

entries.title: Psalms 78:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. unites the most joyful kinds of music, vocal and instrumental.

entries.title: Psalms 78:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. the new moon-- or the month. the time appointed-- (Compare Pr 7:20).

entries.title: Psalms 78:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. a testimony-- The feasts, especially the passover, attested God's relation to His people. Joseph-- for Israel (Psa 80:1). went out through-- or, "over," that is, Israel in the exodus. I heard-- change of person. The writer speaks for the nation. language-- literally, "lip" (Psa 14:1). An aggravation or element of their distress that their oppressors were foreigners (De 28:49).

entries.title: Psalms 79 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. God's language alludes to the burdensome slavery of the Israelites.

entries.title: Psalms 79:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. secret place-- the cloud from which He troubled the Egyptians (Ex 14:24). proved thee-- (Psa 7:10; 17:3)--tested their faith by the miracle.

entries.title: Psalms 79:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. (Compare Psa 50:7). The reproof follows to Psa 81:12. if thou wilt hearken-- He then propounds the terms of His covenant: they should worship Him alone, who (Psa 81:10) had delivered them, and would still confer all needed blessings.

entries.title: Psalms 79:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. They failed, and He gave them up to their own desires and hardness of heart (De 29:18; Pr 1:30; Ro 11:25).

entries.title: Psalms 79:7–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-16. Obedience would have secured all promised blessings and the subjection of foes. In this passage, "should have," "would have," &c., are better, "should" and "would" expressing God's intention at the time, that is, when they left Egypt.

entries.title: Psalms 79:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 82 Psa 82:1-8. Before the great Judge, the judges of the earth are rebuked, exhorted, and threatened. 1. congregation-- (Compare Ex 12:3; 16:1). of the mighty-- that is, of God, of His appointment. the gods-- or, "judges" (Ex 21:6; 22:9), God's representatives.

entries.title: Psalms 79:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. accept the persons-- literally, "lift up the faces," that is, from dejection, or admit to favor and communion, regardless of merit (Le 19:15; Pr 18:5).

entries.title: Psalms 79:14–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. So must good judges act (Psa 10:14; Job 29:12).

entries.title: Psalms 79:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. By the wilful ignorance and negligence of judges, anarchy ensues (Psa 11:3; 75:3). out of course-- (Compare Margin; Psa 9:6; 62:2).

entries.title: Psalms 79:17–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. Though God admitted their official dignity (Joh 10:34), He reminds them of their mortality.

entries.title: Psalms 79:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. As rightful sovereign of earth, God is invoked personally to correct the evils of His representatives.

entries.title: Psalms 79:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 83 Psa 83:1-18. Of Asaph--(See on Psa 74:1, title). The historical occasion is probably that of 2Ch 20:1, 2 (compare Psa 47:1-9; 48:1-14). After a general petition, the craft and rage of the combined enemies are described, God's former dealings recited, and a like summary and speedy destruction on them is invoked. 1. God addressed as indifferent (compare Psa 35:22; 39:12). be not still-- literally, "not quiet," as opposed to action.

entries.title: Psalms 79:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. thine enemies-- as well as ours (Psa 74:23; Is 37:23).

entries.title: Psalms 79:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. hidden ones-- whom God specially protects (Psa 27:5; 91:1).

entries.title: Psalms 79:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. from being a nation-- utter destruction (Is 7:8; 23:1). Israel-- here used for Judah, having been the common name.

entries.title: Psalms 79:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. they have consulted-- with heart, or cordially. together-- all alike.

entries.title: Psalms 80 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-8. tabernacles-- for people (Psa 78:67). they-- all these united with the children of Lot, or Ammonites and Moabites (compare 2Ch 20:1).

entries.title: Psalms 80:3–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-11. Compare the similar fate of these (2Ch 20:23) with that of the foes mentioned in Jud 7:22, here referred to. They destroyed one another (Jud 4:6-24; 7:25). Human remains form manure (compare 2Ki 9:37; Jr 9:22).

entries.title: Psalms 80:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. The language of the invaders. houses-- literally, "residences," enclosures, as for flocks (Psa 65:12). of God-- as the proprietors of the land (2Ch 20:11; Is 14:25).

entries.title: Psalms 80:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. like a wheel-- or, whirling of any light thing (Is 17:13), as stubble or chaff (Psa 1:4).

entries.title: Psalms 81 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. Pursue them to an utter destruction.

entries.title: Psalms 81:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. that they may seek-- or as Psa 83:18, supply "men," since Psa 83:17, 18 amplify the sentiment of Psa 83:16, expressing more fully the measure of destruction, and the lesson of God's being and perfections (compare 2Ch 20:29) taught to all men.

entries.title: Psalms 81:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 84 Psa 84:1-12. (See on Psa 8:1, title, and Psa 42:1, title). The writer describes the desirableness of God's worship and prays for a restoration to its privileges. 1. amiable-- not lovely, but beloved. tabernacles-- (Psa 43:3).

entries.title: Psalms 81:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. longeth-- most intensely (Ge 31:30; Psa 17:12). fainteth-- exhausted with desire. courts-- as tabernacles (Psa 84:1)--the whole building. crieth out-- literally, "sings for joy"; but here, and La 2:19, expresses an act of sorrow as the corresponding noun (Psa 17:1; 61:2). heart and ... flesh-- as in Psa 63:1.

entries.title: Psalms 81:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. thine altars-- that is, of burnt offering and incense, used for the whole tabernacle. Its structure afforded facilities for sparrows and swallows to indulge their known predilections for such places. Some understand the statement as to the birds as a comparison: "as they find homes, so do I desire thine altars," &c.

entries.title: Psalms 81:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. This view is favored by the language here, which, as in Psa 15:1; 23:6, recognizes the blessing of membership in God's family by terms denoting a dwelling in His house.

entries.title: Psalms 81:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. (Compare Psa 68:28). in whose heart ... the ways-- that is, who knows and loves the way to God's favor (Pr 16:17; Is 40:3, 4).

entries.title: Psalms 81:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. valley of Baca-- or, "weeping." Through such, by reason of their dry and barren condition, the worshippers often had to pass to Jerusalem. As they might become wells, or fountains, or pools, supplied by refreshing rain, so the grace of God, by the exercises of His worship, refreshes and revives the hearts of His people, so that for sorrows they have "rivers of delight" (Psa 36:8; 46:4).

entries.title: Psalms 81:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. The figure of the pilgrim is carried out. As such daily refit their bodily strength till they reach Jerusalem, so the spiritual worshipper is daily supplied with spiritual strength by God's grace till he appears before God in heaven. appeareth ... God-- the terms of the requisition for the attendance on the feasts (compare De 16:16),

entries.title: Psalms 81:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. God is addressed as a shield (compare Psa 84:11). thine anointed-- David (1Sa 16:12).

entries.title: Psalms 81:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. I had ... doorkeeper-- literally, "I choose to sit on the threshold," the meanest place.

entries.title: Psalms 81:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. As a sun God enlightens (Psa 27:1); as a shield He protects. grace-- God's favor, its fruit-- glory-- the honor He bestows. uprightly-- (Psa 15:2; 18:23).

entries.title: Psalms 81:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 85 Psa 85:1-13. On the ground of former mercies, the Psalmist prays for renewed blessings, and, confidently expecting them, rejoices. 1. captivity-- not necessarily the Babylonian, but any great evil (Psa 14:7).

entries.title: Psalms 81:20–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-3. (Compare Psa 32:1-5).

entries.title: Psalms 81:22–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-7. having still occasion for the anger which is deprecated.

entries.title: Psalms 81:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. He is confident God will favor His penitent people (Psa 51:17; 80:18). saints-- as in Psa 4:3, the "godly."

entries.title: Psalms 81:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. They are here termed "them that fear him"; and grace produces glory (Psa 84:11).

entries.title: Psalms 81:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. God's promises of "mercy" will be verified by His "truth" (compare Psa 25:10; 40:10); and the "work of righteousness" in His holy government shall be "peace" (Is 32:17). There is an implied contrast with a dispensation under which God's truth sustains His threatened wrath, and His righteousness inflicts misery on the wicked.

entries.title: Psalms 81:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. Earth and heaven shall abound with the blessings of this government;

entries.title: Psalms 81:30–82:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-13. and, under this, the deserted land shall be productive, and men be "set," or guided in God's holy ways. Doubtless, in this description of God's returning favor, the writer had in view that more glorious period, when Christ shall establish His government on God's reconciled justice and abounding mercy.

entries.title: Psalms 82:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 86 Psa 86:1-17. This is a prayer in which the writer, with deep emotion, mingles petitions and praises, now urgent for help, and now elated with hope, in view of former mercies. The occurrence of many terms and phrases peculiar to David's Psalms clearly intimates its authorship. 1-2. poor and needy-- a suffering child of God, as in Psa 10:12, 17; 18:27. I am holy-- or, "godly," as in Psa 4:3; 85:8.

entries.title: Psalms 82:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. lift up my soul-- with strong desire (Psa 25:1).

entries.title: Psalms 82:6–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-7. unto all ... that call upon thee-- or, "worship Thee" (Psa 50:15; 91:15) however undeserving (Ex 34:6; Le 11:9-13).

entries.title: Psalms 82:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. neither ... works-- literally, "nothing like thy works," the "gods" have none at all.

entries.title: Psalms 82:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. The pious Jews believed that God's common relation to all would be ultimately acknowledged by all men (Psa 45:12-16; 47:9).

entries.title: Psalms 82:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. Teach-- Show, point out. the way-- of Providence. walk in thy truth-- according to its declarations. unite my heart-- fix all my affections (Psa 12:2; Jas 4:8). to fear thy name-- (compare Psa 86:12) to honor Thy perfections.

entries.title: Psalms 82:14–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-14. The reason: God had delivered him from death and the power of insolent, violent, and godless persecutors (Psa 54:3; Eze 8:12).

entries.title: Psalms 82:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. Contrasts God with his enemies (compare Psa 86:5).

entries.title: Psalms 82:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. son ... handmaid-- homeborn servant (compare Lu 15:17).

entries.title: Psalms 82:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. Show me-- literally, "Make with me a token," by Thy providential care. Thus in and by his prosperity his enemies would be confounded.

entries.title: Psalms 82:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 87 Psa 87:1-7. This triumphal song was probably occasioned by the same event as the forty-sixth [see on Psa 46:1, title]. The writer celebrates the glory of the Church, as the means of spiritual blessing to the nation. 1. His-- that is, God's foundation-- or, what He has founded, that is, Zion (Is 14:32). is in the holy mountains-- the location of Zion, in the wide sense, for the capital, or Jerusalem, being on several hills.

entries.title: Psalms 82:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. gates-- for the enclosures, or city to which they opened (Psa 9:14; 122:2; compare Psa 132:13, 14).

entries.title: Psalms 82:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. spoken of thee-- or, "in thee," that is, the city of God (Psa 46:4; 48:2).

entries.title: Psalms 82:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. This is what is spoken by God. to them ... me-- literally, "for My knowers," they are true worshippers (Psa 36:10; Is 19:21). These are mentioned as specimens. this-- that is, nation was born there-- Of each it is said, "This was born," or is a native of Zion, spiritually.

entries.title: Psalms 82:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. The writer resumes-- This and that man-- literally, "man and man," or many (Ge 14:10; Ex 8:10, 14), or all (Is 44:5; Ga 3:28). the highest ... her-- God is her protector.

entries.title: Psalms 82:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. The same idea is set forth under the figure of a register made by God (compare Is 4:3).

entries.title: Psalms 82:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. As in a great procession of those thus written up, or registered, seeking Zion (Is 2:3; Jr 50:5), "the singers" and "players," or pipers, shall precede. all my springs-- So each shall say, "All my sources of spiritual joy are in Thee" (Psa 46:4; 84:6).

entries.title: Psalms 82:28–29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 88 Psa 88:1-18. Upon Mahalath--either an instrument, as a lute, to be used as an accompaniment (Leannoth, "for singing") or, as others think, an enigmatic title (see on Psa 5:1, Psa 22:1, and Psa 45:1, titles), denoting the subject--that is, "sickness or disease, for humbling," the idea of spiritual maladies being often represented by disease (compare Psa 6:5, 6; 22:14, 15, &c.). On the other terms, see on Psa 42:1 and Psa 32:1. Heman and Ethan (see on Psa 89:1, title) were David's singers (1Ch 6:18, 33; 15:17), of the family of Kohath. If the persons alluded to (1Ki 4:31; 1Ch 2:6), they were probably adopted into the tribe of Judah. Though called a song, which usually implies joy (Psa 83:1), both the style and matter of the Psalm are very despondent; yet the appeals to God evince faith, and we may suppose that the word "song" might be extended to such compositions. 1-2. Compare on the terms used, Psa 22:2; 31:2.

entries.title: Psalms 82:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. grave-- literally, "hell" (Psa 16:10), death in wide sense.

entries.title: Psalms 82:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. go ... pit-- of destruction (Psa 28:1). as a man-- literally, "a stout man," whose strength is utterly gone.

entries.title: Psalms 82:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. Free ... dead-- Cut off from God's care, as are the slain, who, falling under His wrath, are left, no longer sustained by His hand.

entries.title: Psalms 82:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Similar figures for distress in Psa 63:9; 69:3.

entries.title: Psalms 82:34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. Compare Psa 38:2, on first, and Psa 42:7, on last clause.

entries.title: Psalms 82:35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Both cut off from sympathy and made hateful to friends (Psa 31:11).

entries.title: Psalms 83 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Mine eye mourneth-- literally, "decays," or fails, denoting exhaustion (Psa 6:7; 31:9). I ... called-- (Psa 86:5, 7). stretched out-- for help (Psa 44:20).

entries.title: Psalms 83:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. shall the dead-- the remains of ghosts. arise-- literally, "rise up," that is, as dead persons.

entries.title: Psalms 83:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. amplify the foregoing, the whole purport (as Psa 6:5) being to contrast death and life as seasons for praising God.

entries.title: Psalms 83:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. prevent-- meet--that is, he will diligently come before God for help (Psa 18:41).

entries.title: Psalms 83:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 83:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. from ... youth up-- all my life.

entries.title: Psalms 83:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-17. the extremes of anguish and despair are depicted.

entries.title: Psalms 83:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. into darkness-- Better omit "into"--"mine acquaintances (are) darkness," the gloom of death, &c. (Job 17:13, 14).

entries.title: Psalms 83:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 89 Psa 89:1-52. Of Ethan--(See on Psa 88:1, title). This Psalm was composed during some season of great national distress, perhaps Absalom's rebellion. It contrasts the promised prosperity and perpetuity of David's throne (with reference to the great promise of 2Sa 7:12-17), with a time when God appeared to have forgotten His covenant. The picture thus drawn may typify the promises and the adversities of Christ's kingdom, and the terms of confiding appeal to God provided appropriate prayers for the divine aid and promised blessing. 1. mercies-- those promised (Is 55:3; Ac 13:34), and-- faithfulness-- that is, in fulfilling them.

entries.title: Psalms 83:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. I have said-- expressed, as well as felt, my convictions (2Co 4:13).

entries.title: Psalms 83:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. The object of this faith expressed in God's words (2Sa 7:11-16). with-- or literally, "to" my chosen-- as the covenant is in the form of a promise.

entries.title: Psalms 83:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. This is worthy of our belief, for His faithfulness (is praised) by the congregation of saints or holy ones; that is, angels (compare De 33:2; Da 8:13). sons of the mighty-- (compare Psa 29:1). So is He to be admired on earth.

entries.title: Psalms 83:18–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-14. To illustrate His power and faithfulness examples are cited from history. His control of the sea (the most mighty and unstable object in nature), and of Egypt (Psa 87:4), the first great foe of Israel (subjected to utter helplessness from pride and insolence), are specimens. At the same time, the whole frame of nature founded and sustained by Him, Tabor and Hermon for "east and west," and "north and south," together representing the whole world, declare the same truth as to His attributes.

entries.title: Psalms 83:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. His government of righteousness is served by "mercy" and "truth" as ministers (Psa 85:10-13). know the joyful sound-- understand and appreciate the spiritual blessings symbolized by the feasts to which the people were called by the trumpet (Le 25:9, &c.). walk ... countenance-- live in His favor (Psa 4:6; 44:3).

entries.title: Psalms 83:26–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-17. in-- or, "by" thy righteousness-- Thy faithful just rule. glory-- or, "beauty." of their strength-- They shall be adorned as well as protected. our horn-- exalt our power (Psa 75:10; Lu 1:69).

entries.title: Psalms 83:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. (Margin). Thus is introduced the promise to "our shield," "our king," David.

entries.title: Psalms 83:29–85:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-37. Then-- when the covenant was established, of whose execution the exalted views of God now given furnish assurance. thou ... to thy holy one-- or godly saint, object of favor (Psa 4:3). Nathan is meant (2Sa 7:17; 1Ch 17:3-15). laid help-- literally, "given help." David was chosen and then exalted.

entries.title: Psalms 85:4–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 38-52. present a striking contrast to these glowing promises, in mournful evidences of a loss of God's favor. cast off-- and rejected (compare Psa 15:4; 43:2; 44:9).

entries.title: Psalms 85:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 90 Psa 90:1-17. Contrasting man's frailty with God's eternity, the writer mourns over it as the punishment of sin, and prays for a return of the divine favor. A Prayer [mainly such] of Moses the man of God--(De 33:1; Jos 14:6); as such he wrote this (see on Psa 18:1, title, and Psa 36:1, title). 1. dwelling-place-- home (compare Eze 11:16), as a refuge (De 33:27).

entries.title: Psalms 85:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. brought forth [and] formed-- both express the idea of production by birth.

entries.title: Psalms 85:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. to destruction-- literally, "even to dust" (Ge 3:19), which is partly quoted in the last clause.

entries.title: Psalms 85:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Even were our days now a thousand years, as Adam's, our life would be but a moment in God's sight (2Pe 3:8). a watch-- or, third part of a night (compare Ex 14:24).

entries.title: Psalms 85:24–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. Life is like grass, which, though changing under the influence of the night's dew, and flourishing in the morning, is soon cut down and withereth (Psa 103:15; 1Pe 1:24).

entries.title: Psalms 85:26–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. For-- A reason, this is the infliction of God's wrath. troubled-- literally, "confounded by terror" (Psa 2:5). Death is by sin (Ro 5:12). Though "secret," the light of God's countenance, as a candle, will bring sin to view (Pr 20:27; 1Co 4:5).

entries.title: Psalms 85:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. are passed-- literally, "turn," as to depart (Jr 6:4). spend-- literally, "consume." as a tale-- literally, "a thought," or, "a sigh" (Eze 2:10).

entries.title: Psalms 85:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Moses' life was an exception (De 34:7). it is ... cut off-- or, "driven," as is said of the quails in using the same word (Nu 11:31). In view of this certain and speedy end, life is full of sorrow.

entries.title: Psalms 85:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. The whole verse may be read as a question implying the negative, "No one knows what Thy anger can do, and what Thy wrath is, estimated by a true piety."

entries.title: Psalms 85:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. This he prays we may know or understand, so as properly to number or appreciate the shortness of our days, that we may be wise.

entries.title: Psalms 85:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. (Compare Psa 13:2). let it repent-- a strong figure, as in Ex 32:12, imploring a change in His dealings.

entries.title: Psalms 85:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. early-- promptly.

entries.title: Psalms 85:34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. As have been our sorrows, so let our joys be great and long.

entries.title: Psalms 85:35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. thy work-- or, providential acts. thy glory-- (Psa 8:5; 45:3), the honor accruing from Thy work of mercy to us.

entries.title: Psalms 86 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. let the beauty-- or sum of His gracious acts, in their harmony, be illustrated in us, and favor our enterprise.

entries.title: Psalms 86:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 91 Psa 91:1-16. David is the most probable author; and the pestilence, mentioned in 2Sa 24:13-15, the most probable of any special occasion to which the Psalm may refer. The changes of person allowable in poetry are here frequently made. 1. dwelleth in the secret place-- (Psa 27:5; 31:20) denotes nearness to God. Such as do so abide or lodge secure from assaults, and can well use the terms of trust in Psa 91:2.

entries.title: Psalms 86:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. snares ... [and] ... noisome pestilence-- literally, "plagues of mischiefs" (Psa 5:9; 52:7), are expressive figures for various evils.

entries.title: Psalms 86:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. For the first figure compare De 32:11; Mt 23:37. buckler-- literally, "surrounding"--that is, a kind of shield covering all over.

entries.title: Psalms 86:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. terror-- or, what causes it (Pr 20:2). by night-- then aggravated. arrow-- that is, of enemies.

entries.title: Psalms 86:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. The security is more valuable, as being special, and, therefore, evidently of God; and while ten thousands of the wicked fall, the righteous are in such safety that they only see the calamity.

entries.title: Psalms 86:10–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-12. This exemption from evil is the result of trust in God, who employs angels as ministering spirits (He 1:14).

entries.title: Psalms 86:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. Even the fiercest, strongest, and most insidious animals may be trampled on with impunity.

entries.title: Psalms 86:15–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-16. God Himself speaks (compare Psa 46:10; 75:2, 3). All the terms to express safety and peace indicate the most undoubting confidence (compare Psa 18:2; 20:1; 22:5). set his love-- that of the most ardent kind.

entries.title: Psalms 86:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 92 Psa 92:1-15. A Psalm-song--(see on Psa 30:1, title). The theme: God should be praised for His righteous judgments on the wicked and His care and defense of His people. Such a topic, at all times proper, is specially so for the reflections of the Sabbath day. 1. sing ... name-- celebrate Thy perfections.

entries.title: Psalms 86:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. in the morning, ... every night-- diligently and constantly (Psa 42:8). loving kindness-- literally, "mercy." faithfulness-- in fulfilling promises (Psa 89:14).

entries.title: Psalms 86:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. In such a work all proper aid must be used. with a ... sound-- or, on Higgaion (see on Psa 9:16), perhaps an instrument of that name, from its sound resembling the muttered sound of meditation, as expressed also by the word. This is joined with the harp.

entries.title: Psalms 86:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. thy work-- that is, of providence (Psa 90:16, 17).

entries.title: Psalms 86:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. great ... works-- correspond to deep or vast thoughts (Psa 40:5; Ro 11:23).

entries.title: Psalms 86:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. A brutish man knoweth not-- that is, God's works, so the Psalmist describes himself (Psa 73:22) when amazed by the prosperity of the wicked, now understood and explained.

entries.title: Psalms 86:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. This he does in part, by contrasting their ruin with God's exaltation and eternity. most high-- as occupying the highest place in heaven (Psa 7:7; 18:16).

entries.title: Psalms 86:27–28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. A further contrast with the wicked, in the lot of the righteous, safety and triumph.

entries.title: Psalms 86:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. see ... [and] ... hear my desire-- or, literally, "look on" my enemies and hear of the wicked (compare Psa 27:11; 54:7)--that is, I shall be gratified by their fall.

entries.title: Psalms 86:30–87:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-14. The vigorous growth, longevity, utility, fragrance, and beauty of these noble trees, set forth the life, character, and destiny of the pious;

entries.title: Psalms 87:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. and they thus declare God's glory as their strong and righteous ruler.

entries.title: Psalms 87:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 93 Psa 93:1-5. This and the six following Psalms were applied by the Jews to the times of the Messiah. The theme is God's supremacy in creation and providence. 1. God is described as a King entering on His reign, and, for robes of royalty, investing Himself with the glorious attributes of His nature. The result of His thus reigning is the durability of the world.

entries.title: Psalms 87:5–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-4. His underived power exceeds the most sublime exhibitions of the most powerful objects in nature (Psa 89:9).

entries.title: Psalms 87:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. While His power inspires dread, His revealed will should secure our confidence (compare Psa 19:7; 25:10), and thus fear and love combined, producing all holy emotions, should distinguish the worship we offer in His house, both earthly and heavenly.

entries.title: Psalms 87:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 94 Psa 94:1-23. The writer, appealing to God in view of the oppression of enemies, rebukes them for their wickedness and folly, and encourages himself, in the confidence that God will punish evildoers, and favor His people. 1-2. God's revenge is His judicial infliction of righteous punishment. show thyself-- (Compare Margin).

entries.title: Psalms 87:12–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. In an earnest expostulation he expresses his desire that the insolent triumph of the wicked may be ended.

entries.title: Psalms 87:14–88:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. thy people [and] thine heritage-- are synonymous, the people being often called God's heritage. As justice to the weak is a sign of the best government, their oppression is a sign of the worst (De 10:18; Is 10:2).

entries.title: Psalms 88:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. Their cruelty is only exceeded by their wicked and absurd presumption (Psa 10:11; 59:7).

entries.title: Psalms 88:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. ye brutish-- (Compare Psa 73:22; 92:6).

entries.title: Psalms 88:3–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-11. The evidence of God's providential government is found in His creative power and omniscience, which also assure us that He can punish the wicked in regard to all their vain purposes.

entries.title: Psalms 88:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-13. On the other hand He favors though He chastens, the pious, and will teach and preserve them till the prosperous wicked are overthrown.

entries.title: Psalms 88:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. This results from His abiding love (De 32:15), which is further evinced by His restoring order in His government, whose right administration will be approved by the good.

entries.title: Psalms 88:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. These questions imply that none other than God will help (Psa 60:9),

entries.title: Psalms 88:11–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-19. a fact fully confirmed by his past experience. dwelt in silence-- as in the grave (Psa 31:17).

entries.title: Psalms 88:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. throne-- power, rulers. iniquity [and] mischief-- both denote evils done to others, as Psa 94:21 explains.

entries.title: Psalms 88:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22-23. Yet he is safe in God's care. defence-- (Psa 59:9). rock of ... refuge-- (Psa 9:9; 18:2).

entries.title: Psalms 88:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 95 Psa 95:1-11. David (He 4:7) exhorts men to praise God for His greatness, and warns them, in God's words, against neglecting His service. 1. The terms used to express the highest kind of joy. rock-- a firm basis, giving certainty of salvation (Psa 62:7).

entries.title: Psalms 88:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. come ... presence-- literally, "approach," or, meet Him (Psa 17:13).

entries.title: Psalms 88:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. above ... gods-- esteemed such by men, though really nothing (Jr 5:7; 10:10-15).

entries.title: Psalms 88:22–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. The terms used describe the world in its whole extent, subject to God.

entries.title: Psalms 88:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. come-- or, "enter," with solemn forms, as well as hearts.

entries.title: Psalms 88:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. This relation illustrates our entire dependence (compare Psa 23:3; 74:1). The last clause is united by Paul (He 3:7) to the following (compare Psa 81:8),

entries.title: Psalms 89 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-11. warning against neglect; and this is sustained by citing the melancholy fate of their rebellious ancestors, whose provoking insolence is described by quoting the language of God's complaint (Nu 14:11) of their conduct at Meribah and Massah, names given (Ex 17:7) to commemorate their strife and contention with Him (Psa 78:18, 41).

entries.title: Psalms 89:5–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 96 Psa 96:1-13. The substance of this Psalm, and portions of the ninety-seventh, ninety-eighth, and hundredth, are found in 1Ch 16:7-36, which was used by David's directions in the dedication of the tabernacle on Mount Zion. The dispensation of the Messiah was typified by that event, involving, as it did, a more permanent seat of worship, and the introduction of additional and more spiritual services. Hence the language of these Psalms may be regarded as having a higher import than that pertinent to the occasion on which it was thus publicly used. 1-3. All nations are invited to unite in this most joyful praise. new song-- literally, "fresh," or new mercies (Psa 33:3; 40:3).

entries.title: Psalms 89:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. For He is not a local God, but of universal agency, while idols are nothing.

entries.title: Psalms 90 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Honour and majesty-- are His attendants, declared in His mighty works, while power and grace are specially seen in His spiritual relations to His people.

entries.title: Psalms 90:1–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-9. Give-- or, "ascribe" (Psa 29:1) due honor to Him, by acts of appointed and solemn worship in His house.

entries.title: Psalms 90:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Let all know that the government of the world is ordered in justice, and they shall enjoy firm and lasting peace (compare Psa 72:3, 7; Is 9:6, 7).

entries.title: Psalms 90:5–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-13. For which reason the universe is invoked to unite in joy, and even inanimate nature (Ro 8:14-22) is poetically represented as capable of joining in the anthem of praise.

entries.title: Psalms 90:9–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 97 Psa 97:1-12. The writer celebrates the Lord's dominion over nations and nature, describes its effect on foes and friends, and exhorts and encourages the latter. 1-2. This dominion is a cause of joy, because, even though our minds are oppressed with terror before the throne of the King of kings (Ex 19:16; De 5:22), we know it is based on righteous principles and judgments which are according to truth.

entries.title: Psalms 90:11–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-5. The attending illustrations of God's awful justice on enemies (Psa 83:14) are seen in the disclosures of His almighty power on the elements of nature (compare Psa 46:2; 77:17; Ha 3:6, &c.).

entries.title: Psalms 90:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. heavens-- or, their inhabitants (Psa 50:6), as opposed to "nations" in the latter clause (compare Is 40:5; 66:18).

entries.title: Psalms 90:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. Idolaters are utterly put to shame, for if angels must worship Him, how much more those who worshipped them. all ye gods-- literally, "all ye angels" (Psa 8:5; 138:1; He 1:6; 2:7). Paul quotes, not as a prophecy, but as language used in regard to the Lord Jehovah, who in the Old Testament theophania is the second person of the Godhead.

entries.title: Psalms 90:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. The exaltation of Zion's king is joy to the righteous and sorrow to the wicked. daughters of Judah-- (Compare Psa 48:11).

entries.title: Psalms 90:18–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-12. Let gratitude for the blessings of providence and grace incite saints (Psa 4:3) to holy living. Spiritual blessings are in store, represented by light (Psa 27:1) and gladness.

entries.title: Psalms 90:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 98 Psa 98:1-9. In view of the wonders of grace and righteousness displayed in God's salvation, the whole creation is invited to unite in praise. 1. gotten ... victory-- literally, "made salvation," enabled Him to save His people. right hand, and ... arm-- denote power. holy arm-- or, "arm of holiness," the power of His united moral perfections (Psa 22:3; 32:11).

entries.title: Psalms 90:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. salvation-- the result of His righteousness (Psa 7:17; 31:1), and both are publicly displayed.

entries.title: Psalms 90:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. The union of mercy and truth (Psa 57:3; 85:10) secure the blessings of the promise (Ge 12:3; 18:18) to all the world (Is 52:10).

entries.title: Psalms 90:25–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-6. make a loud noise-- or, "burst forth" (Is 14:7; 44:23). before ... King-- hail Him as your sovereign; and while, with every aid to demonstrate zeal and joy, intelligent creatures are invited to praise, as in Psa 96:11-13, inanimate nature is also summoned to honor Him who triumphs and rules in righteousness and equity.

entries.title: Psalms 90:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 99 Psa 99:1-9. God's government is especially exercised in and for His Church, which should praise Him for His gracious dealings. 1. sitteth ... cherubim-- (compare 1Sa 4:4; Psa 80:1). tremble ... be moved-- inspired with fear by His judgments on the wicked.

entries.title: Psalms 91 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. great in Zion-- where He dwells (Psa 9:11).

entries.title: Psalms 91:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. thy ... name-- perfections of justice, power, &c. great and terrible name-- producing dread (De 10:17), and to be praised by those over whom He is exalted (Psa 97:9). it is holy-- or, "He is holy" (Psa 99:5, 9; Is 6:3).

entries.title: Psalms 91:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. To His wise and righteous government all nations should render honor. king's ... judgment-- His power is combined with justice. he is holy-- (compare Psa 22:3).

entries.title: Psalms 91:4–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-8. The experience of these servants of God is cited for encouragement. among ... priests, among ... upon the Lord [and] He spake ... pillar-- may be referred to all three (compare Ex 18:19; Le 8:15; De 5:5; 1Sa 9:13).

entries.title: Psalms 91:9–92:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 100 Psa 100:1-5. As closing this series (see on Psa 93:1), this Psalm is a general call on all the earth to render exalted praise to God, the creator, preserver, and benefactor of men. 1-2. With thankful praise, unite service as the subjects of a king (Psa 2:11, 12).

entries.title: Psalms 92:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. To the obligations of a creature and subject is added that of a beneficiary (Psa 95:7).

entries.title: Psalms 92:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Join joyfully in His public worship. The terms are, of course, figurative (compare Psa 84:2; 92:13; Is 66:23). Enter-- or, "Come with solemnity" (Psa 95:6).

entries.title: Psalms 92:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. The reason: God's eternal mercy and truth (Psa 25:8; 89:7).

entries.title: Psalms 92:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 101 Psa 101:1-8. In this Psalm the profession of the principles of his domestic and political government testifies, as well as actions in accordance with it, David's appreciation of God's mercy to him, and His judgment on his enemies: and thus he sings or celebrates God's dealings. 2. He avows his sincere purpose, by God's aid, to act uprightly (Ge 17:1; Psa 18:30).

entries.title: Psalms 92:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. set ... eyes-- as an example to be approved and followed. no wicked thing-- literally, "word," plan or purpose of Belial (Psa 41:8). work of ... aside-- apostates. not cleave to me-- I will not be implicated in it (compare Psa 1:1-3).

entries.title: Psalms 92:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. A froward heart-- or, "perverse heart" (Psa 18:26). Such a temper I will not indulge, nor even know evil or wickedness.

entries.title: Psalms 92:9–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. The slanderers and haughty persons, so mischievous in society, I will disown; but--

entries.title: Psalms 92:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. not dwell-- literally, "not sit," or tarry, or be established.

entries.title: Psalms 92:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. will early-- or, "diligently." city of the Lord-- or, "holy place" (Psa 48:2), where wicked men shall not be tolerated.

entries.title: Psalms 92:14–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 102 Psa 102:1-28. A Prayer of the afflicted, &c.--The general terms seem to denote the propriety of regarding the Psalm as suitably expressive of the anxieties of any one of David's descendants, piously concerned for the welfare of the Church. It was probably David's composition, and, though specially suggested by some peculiar trials, descriptive of future times. Overwhelmed--(compare Psa 61:2). Poureth out--pouring out the soul--(Psa 62:8). Complaint--(Psa 55:2). The tone of complaint predominates, though in view of God's promises and abiding faithfulness, it is sometimes exchanged for that of confidence and hope. 1-3. The terms used occur in Psa 4:1; 17:1, 6; 18:6; 31:2, 10; 37:20.

entries.title: Psalms 92:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. (Compare Psa 121:6). so that I forget-- or, "have forgotten," that is, in my distress (Psa 107:18), and hence strength fails.

entries.title: Psalms 92:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. voice ... groaning-- effect put for cause, my agony emaciates me.

entries.title: Psalms 92:19–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. The figures express extreme loneliness.

entries.title: Psalms 92:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. sworn against me-- or literally, "by me," wishing others as miserable as I am (Nu 5:21).

entries.title: Psalms 92:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. ashes-- a figure of grief, my bread; weeping or tears, my drink (Psa 80:5).

entries.title: Psalms 93 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. lifted ... cast me down-- or, "cast me away" as stubble by a whirlwind (Is 64:6).

entries.title: Psalms 93:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. shadow ... declineth-- soon to vanish in the darkness of night.

entries.title: Psalms 93:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. Contrast with man's frailty (compare Psa 90:1-7). thy remembrance-- that by which Thou art remembered, Thy promise.

entries.title: Psalms 93:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-14. Hence it is here adduced. for-- or, "when." the set time, &c.-- the time promised, the indication of which is the interest felt for Zion by the people of God.

entries.title: Psalms 93:5–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-17. God's favor to the Church will affect her persecutors with fear.

entries.title: Psalms 93:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. people ... created-- (compare Psa 22:31), an organized body, as a Church.

entries.title: Psalms 93:9–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-22. For-- or, "That," as introducing the statement of God's condescension. A summary of what shall be written. to loose ... appointed-- or, "deliver" them (Psa 79:11).

entries.title: Psalms 93:13–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23-28. The writer, speaking for the Church, finds encouragement in the midst of all his distresses. God's eternal existence is a pledge of faithfulness to His promises. in the way-- of providence. weakened-- literally, "afflicted," and made fearful of a premature end, a figure of the apprehensions of the Church, lest God might not perform His promise, drawn from those of a person in view of the dangers of early death (compare Psa 89:47). Paul (He 1:10) quotes Psa 102:26-28 as addressed to Christ in His divine nature. The scope of the Psalm, as already seen, so far from opposing, favors this view, especially by the sentiments of Psa 102:12-15 (compare Is 60:1). The association of the Messiah with a day of future glory to the Church was very intimate in the minds of Old Testament writers; and with correct views of His nature it is very consistent that He should be addressed as the Lord and Head of His Church, who would bring about that glorious future on which they ever dwelt with fond delightful anticipations.

entries.title: Psalms 94 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 103 Psa 103:1-22. A Psalm of joyous praise, in which the writer rises from a thankful acknowledgment of personal blessings to a lively celebration of God's gracious attributes, as not only intrinsically worthy of praise, but as specially suited to man's frailty. He concludes by invoking all creatures to unite in his song. 1. Bless, &c.-- when God is the object, praise. my soul-- myself (Psa 3:3; 25:1), with allusion to the act, as one of intelligence. all ... within me-- (De 6:5). his holy name-- (Psa 5:11), His complete moral perfections.

entries.title: Psalms 94:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. forget not all-- not any, none of His benefits.

entries.title: Psalms 94:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. diseases-- as penal inflictions (De 29:22; 2Ch 21:19).

entries.title: Psalms 94:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. redeemeth-- Cost is implied. destruction-- literally, "pit of corruption" (Psa 16:10). crowneth-- or, "adorneth" (Psa 65:11). tender mercies-- compassions (compare Psa 25:6; 40:11).

entries.title: Psalms 94:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. By God's provision, the saint retains a youthful vigor like the eagles (Psa 92:14; compare Is 40:31).

entries.title: Psalms 94:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Literally, "righteousness and judgments," denoting various acts of God's government.

entries.title: Psalms 94:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. ways-- of providence, &c., as usual (Psa 25:4; 67:2). acts-- literally, "wonders" (Psa 7:11; 78:17).

entries.title: Psalms 94:7–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-10. God's benevolence implies no merit. He shows it to sinners, who also are chastened for a time (Ex 34:6). keep (anger)-- in Le 19:18, bear a grudge (Jr 3:5, 12).

entries.title: Psalms 94:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. great-- efficient.

entries.title: Psalms 94:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. removed ... from us-- so as no longer to affect our relations to Him.

entries.title: Psalms 94:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. pitieth-- literally, "has compassion on."

entries.title: Psalms 94:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. he-- "who formed," Psa 94:9. knoweth our frame-- literally, "our form." we are dust-- made of and tending to it (Ge 2:7).

entries.title: Psalms 95 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-16. So short and frail is life that a breath may destroy it. it is gone-- literally, "it is not." know it no more-- no more recognize him (Psa 90:6; Is 40:6-8).

entries.title: Psalms 95:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-18. For similar contrast compare Psa 90:2-6; 102:27, 28.

entries.title: Psalms 95:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. God's firm and universal dominion is a pledge that He will keep His promises (Psa 11:4; 47:8).

entries.title: Psalms 95:5–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20-22. do his commandments ... word-- or, literally, "so as to hearken," &c., that is, their acts of obedience are prompt, so that they are ever ready to hear, and know, and follow implicitly His declared will (compare De 26:17; Lu 1:19).

entries.title: Psalms 95:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 104 Psa 104:1-35. The Psalmist celebrates God's glory in His works of creation and providence, teaching the dependence of all living creatures; and contrasting the happiness of those who praise Him with the awful end of the wicked. 1. God's essential glory, and also that displayed by His mighty works, afford ground for praise.

entries.title: Psalms 95:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. light-- is a figurative representation of the glory of the invisible God (Mt 17:2; 1Ti 6:16). Its use in this connection may refer to the first work of creation (Ge 1:3). stretchest out the heavens-- the visible heavens or sky which cover the earth as a curtain (Is 40:12).

entries.title: Psalms 95:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. in the waters-- or, it may be "with"; using this fluid for the beams, or frames, of His residence accords with the figure of clouds for chariots, and wind as a means of conveyance. walketh-- or, "moveth" (compare Psa 18:10, 11; Am 9:6).

entries.title: Psalms 95:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. This is quoted by Paul (He 1:7) to denote the subordinate position of angels; that is, they are only messengers as other and material agencies. spirits-- literally, "winds." flaming fire-- (Psa 105:32) being here so called.

entries.title: Psalms 95:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. The earth is firmly fixed by His power.

entries.title: Psalms 95:14–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-9. These verses rather describe the wonders of the flood than the creation (Ge 7:19, 20; 2Pe 3:5, 6). God's method of arresting the flood and making its waters subside is poetically called a "rebuke" (Psa 76:6; Is 50:2), and the process of the flood's subsiding by undulations among the hills and valleys is vividly described.

entries.title: Psalms 95:18–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-13. Once destructive, these waters are subjected to the service of God's creatures. In rain and dew from His chambers (compare Psa 104:3), and fountains and streams, they give drink to thirsting animals and fertilize the soil. Trees thus nourished supply homes to singing birds, and the earth teems with the productions of God's wise agencies,

entries.title: Psalms 95:22–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. so that men and beasts are abundantly provided with food. for the service-- literally, "for the culture," &c., by which he secures the results. oil ... shine-- literally, "makes his face to shine more than oil," that is, so cheers and invigorates him, that outwardly he appears better than if anointed. strengtheneth ... heart-- gives vigor to man (compare Jud 19:5).

entries.title: Psalms 95:24–96:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-19. God's care of even wild animals and uncultivated parts of the earth.

entries.title: Psalms 96:2–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20-23. He provides and adapts to man's wants the appointed times and seasons.

entries.title: Psalms 96:6–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24-26. From a view of the earth thus full of God's blessings, the writer passes to the sea, which, in its immensity, and as a scene and means of man's activity in commerce, and the home of countless multitudes of creatures, also displays divine power and beneficence. The mention of

entries.title: Psalms 96:9–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27-30. The entire dependence of this immense family on God is set forth. With Him, to kill or make alive is equally easy. To hide His face is to withdraw favor (Psa 13:1). By His spirit, or breath, or mere word, He gives life. It is His constant providence which repairs the wastes of time and disease.

entries.title: Psalms 96:13–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 31-34. While God could equally glorify His power in destruction, that He does it in preservation is of His rich goodness and mercy, so that we may well spend our lives in grateful praise, honoring to Him, and delightful to pious hearts (Psa 147:1).

entries.title: Psalms 96:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 35. Those who refuse such a protector and withhold such a service mar the beauty of His works, and must perish from His presence. Praise ye the Lord-- The Psalm closes with an invocation of praise, the translation of a Hebrew phrase, which is used as an English word, "Hallelujah," and may have served the purpose of a chorus, as often in our psalmody, or to give fuller expression to the writer's emotions. It is peculiar to Psalms composed after the captivity, as "Selah" is to those of an earlier date.

entries.title: Psalms 96:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 105 Psa 105:1-45. After an exhortation to praise God, addressed especially to the chosen people, the writer presents the special reason for praise, in a summary of their history from the calling of Abraham to their settlement in Canaan, and reminds them that their obedience was the end of all God's gracious dealings. 1. call ... name-- (Psa 79:6; Ro 10:13). Call on Him, according to His historically manifested glory. After the example of Abraham, who, as often as God acquired for Himself a name in guiding him, called in solemn worship upon the name of the Lord (Ge 12:8; 13:4). among the people-- or, "peoples" (Psa 18:49). deeds-- or, "wonders" (Psa 103:7).

entries.title: Psalms 96:21–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. Seeking God's favor is the only true mode of getting true happiness, and His strength [Psa 105:4] is the only true source of protection (compare Psa 32:11; 40:16). Glory ... name-- boast in His perfections. The world glories in its horses and chariots against the Church of God lying in the dust; but our hope is in the name, that is, the power and love of God to His people, manifested in past deliverances.

entries.title: Psalms 96:23–97:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. judgments ... mouth-- His judicial decisions for the good and against the wicked.

entries.title: Psalms 97:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. Rather, "He, Jehovah, is our God." His title, "Jehovah," implies that He, the unchangeable, self-existing Being, makes things to be, that is, fulfils His promises, and therefore will not forsake His people. Though specially of His people, He is God over all.

entries.title: Psalms 97:2–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-11. The covenant was often ratified. word-- answering to "covenant" [Psa 105:9] in the parallel clause, namely, the word of promise, which, according to Psa 105:10, He set forth for an inviolable law. commanded-- or, "ordained" (Psa 68:28). to a thousand generations-- perpetually. A verbal allusion to De 7:9 (compare Ex 20:6).

entries.title: Psalms 97:6–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-15. few ... in number-- alluding to Jacob's words (Ge 34:30), "I being few in number." yea, very few-- literally, "as a few," that is, like fewness itself (compare Is 1:9). strangers-- sojourners in the land of their future inheritance, as in a strange country (He 11:9).

entries.title: Psalms 97:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. God ordered the famine. God called for a famine-- as if it were a servant, ready to come at God's bidding. Compare the centurion's words, as to disease being God's servant (Mt 8:8, 9). upon the land-- namely, Canaan (Ge 41:54). staff of bread-- what supports life (Le 26:26; Psa 104:15; Is 3:1).

entries.title: Psalms 97:11–98:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-21. Joseph was sent of God (Ge 45:5).

entries.title: Psalms 98:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. To bind-- Not literally bind; but exercise over them absolute control, as the parallel in the second clause shows; also Ge 41:40, 44, in which not literal fettering, but commanding obedience, is spoken of. It refers to Psa 105:18. The soul that was once bound itself now binds others, even princes. The same moral binding is assigned to the saints (Psa 149:8). teach ... senators wisdom-- the ground of his exaltation by Pharaoh was his wisdom (Ge 41:39); namely, in state policy, and ordering well a kingdom.

entries.title: Psalms 98:3–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23-25. Israel ... and Jacob-- that is, Jacob himself is meant, as Psa 105:24 speaks of "his people." Still, he came with his whole house (Ge 46:6, 7). sojourned-- (Ge 47:4). land of Ham-- or, Egypt (Psa 78:51).

entries.title: Psalms 98:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. Moses ... chosen-- both what they were by divine choice (Psa 78:70).

entries.title: Psalms 98:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27. signs-- literally, "words of signs," or rather, as "words" in Hebrew means "things," "things of His signs," that is, His marvellous tokens of power (Psa 145:5, Margin). Compare the same Hebraism (Psa 65:3, Margin).

entries.title: Psalms 98:8–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28-36. The ninth plague is made prominent as peculiarly wonderful. they rebelled not-- Moses and Aaron promptly obeyed God (He 11:27); (compare Ex 7:1-11:10 and Psa 78:44-51, with which this summary substantially agrees). Or, rather, the "darkness" here is figurative (Jr 13:16), the literal plague of darkness (Ex 10:22, 23) being only alluded to as the symbol of God's wrath which overhung Egypt as a dark cloud during all the plagues. Hence, it is placed first, out of the historical order. Thus, "They rebelled not (that is, no longer) against His word," refers to the Egyptians. Whenever God sent a plague on them, they were ready to let Israel go, though refusing when the plague ceased. his word-- His command to let Israel go [Hengstenberg]. Of the ten plagues, only eight are mentioned, the fifth, the murrain of beasts, and the sixth, the boils, being omitted.

entries.title: Psalms 98:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 37. with silver and gold-- presented them by the Egyptians, as an acknowledgment due for their labors in their bondage (compare Ex 12:35). one feeble person-- or, "stumbler," unfit for the line of march. Compare "harnessed," that is, accoutred and marshalled as an army on march (Ex 13:18; Is 5:27).

entries.title: Psalms 98:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Psalms 98:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 39. covering-- in sense of protection (compare Ex 13:21; Nu 10:34). In the burning sands of the desert the cloud protected the congregation from the heat of the sun; an emblem of God's protecting favor of His people, as interpreted by Isaiah (Is 4:5, 6; compare Nu 9:16).

entries.title: Psalms 98:22–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 42-45. The reasons for these dealings: (1) God's faithfulness to His covenant, "His holy promise" of Canaan, is the fountain whence flowed so many acts of marvellous kindness to His people (compare Psa 105:8, 11). Ex 2:24 is the fundamental passage [Hengstenberg]. (2) That they might be obedient. The observance of God's commands by Abraham was the object of the covenant with him (Ge 18:19), as it was also the object of the covenant with Israel, that they might observe God's statutes. remembered ... and Abraham-- or, "remembered His holy word (that is, covenant confirmed) with Abraham."

entries.title: Psalms 99:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 106 Psa 106:1-48. This Psalm gives a detailed confession of the sins of Israel in all periods of their history, with special reference to the terms of the covenant as intimated (Psa 105:45). It is introduced by praise to God for the wonders of His mercy, and concluded by a supplication for His favor to His afflicted people, and a doxology. 1. Praise, &c.-- (See on Psa 104:35), begins and ends the Psalm, intimating the obligations of praise, however we sin and suffer 1Ch 16:34-36 is the source from which the beginning and end of this Psalm are derived.

entries.title: Psalms 99:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. His acts exceed our comprehension, as His praise our powers of expression (Ro 11:33). Their unutterable greatness is not to keep us back, but to urge us the more to try to praise Him as best we can (Psa 40:5; 71:15).

entries.title: Psalms 99:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. The blessing is limited to those whose principles and acts are right. How "blessed" Israel would be now, if he had "observed God's statutes" (Psa 105:45).

entries.title: Psalms 99:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. In view of the desert of sins to be confessed, the writer invokes God's covenant mercy to himself and the Church, in whose welfare he rejoices. The speaker, me, I, is not the Psalmist himself, but the people, the present generation (compare Psa 106:6). visit-- (Compare Psa 8:4).

entries.title: Psalms 99:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Compare 1Ki 8:47; Da 9:5, where the same three verbs occur in the same order and connection, the original of the two later passages being the first one, the prayer of Solomon in dedicating the temple. sinned ... fathers-- like them, and so partaking of their guilt. The terms denote a rising gradation of sinning (compare Psa 1:1). with our fathers-- we and they together forming one mass of corruption.

entries.title: Psalms 99:7–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-12. Special confession. Their rebellion at the sea (Ex 14:11) was because they had not remembered nor understood God's miracles on their behalf. That God saved them in their unbelief was of His mere mercy, and for His own glory. the sea ... the Red Sea-- the very words in which Moses' song celebrated the scene of Israel's deliverance (Ex 15:4). Israel began to rebel against God at the very moment and scene of its deliverance by God!

entries.title: Psalms 99:13–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-15. The faith induced by God's display of power in their behalf was short lived, and their new rebellion and temptation was visited by God with fresh punishment, inflicted by leaving them to the result of their own gratified appetites, and sending on them spiritual poverty (Nu 11:18). They soon forgat-- literally, "They hasted, they forgat" (compare Ex 32:8). "They have turned aside quickly (or, hastily) out of the way." The haste of our desires is such that we can scarcely allow God one day. Unless He immediately answers our call, instantly then arise impatience, and at length despair. his works-- (De 11:3, 4; Da 9:14). his counsel-- They waited not for the development of God's counsel, or plan for their deliverance, at His own time, and in His own way.

entries.title: Psalms 99:16–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-18. All the congregation took part with Dathan, Korah, &c., and their accomplices (Nu 16:41). Aaron the saint-- literally, "the holy one," as consecrated priest; not a moral attribute, but one designating his office as holy to the Lord. The rebellion was followed by a double punishment: (1) of the non-Levitical rebels, the Reubenites, Dathan and Abiram, &c. (De 11:6; Nu 26:10); these were swallowed up by the earth.

entries.title: Psalms 99:19–100:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-23. From indirect setting God at naught, they pass to direct. made-- though prohibited in Ex 20:4, 5 to make a likeness, even of the true God. calf-- called so in contempt. They would have made an ox or bull, but their idol turned out but a calf; an imitation of the divine symbols, the cherubim; or of the sacred bull of Egyptian idolatry. The idolatry was more sinful in view of their recent experience of God's power in Egypt and His wonders at Sinai (Ex 32:1-6). Though intending to worship Jehovah under the symbol of the calf, yet as this was incompatible with His nature (De 4:15-17), they in reality gave up Him, and so were given up by Him. Instead of the Lord of heaven, they had as their glory the image of an ox that does nothing but eat grass.

entries.title: Psalms 100:2–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24-27. The sin of refusing to invade Canaan, "the pleasant land" (Jr 3:19; Eze 20:6; Da 8:9), "the land of beauty," was punished by the destruction of that generation (Nu 14:28), and the threat of dispersion (De 4:25; 28:32) afterwards made to their posterity, and fulfilled in the great calamities now bewailed, may have also been then added. despised-- (Nu 14:31). believed not his word-- by which He promised He would give them the land; but rather the word of the faithless spies (compare Psa 78:22).

entries.title: Psalms 100:6–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28-30. sacrifices of the dead-- that is, of lifeless idols, contrasted with "the living God" (Jr 10:3-10; compare Psa 115:4-7; 1Co 12:2). On the words, joined themselves to Baal-peor-- see Nu 25:2, 3, 5. Baal-peor-- that is, the possessor of Peor, the mountain on which Chemosh, the idol of Moab, was worshipped, and at the foot of which Israel at the time lay encamped (Nu 23:28). The name never occurs except in connection with that locality and that circumstance.

entries.title: Psalms 100:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 31. counted ... righteousness-- "a just and rewardable action." for-- or, "unto," to the procuring of righteousness, as in Ro 4:2; 10:4. Here it was a particular act, not faith, nor its object Christ; and what was procured was not justifying righteousness, or what was to be rewarded with eternal life; for no one act of man's can be taken for complete obedience. But it was that which God approved and rewarded with a perpetual priesthood to him and his descendants (Nu 25:13; 1Ch 6:4, &c.).

entries.title: Psalms 100:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 32-33. (Compare Nu 20:3-12; De 1:37; 3:26). went ill with-- literally, "was bad for" Moses-- His conduct, though under great provocation, was punished by exclusion from Canaan.

entries.title: Psalms 100:12–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 34-39. They not only failed to expel the heathen, as God commanded-- (Ex 23:32, 33), literally, "said (they should)," but conformed to their idolatries [Psa 106:36], and thus became spiritual adulterers (Psa 73:27).

entries.title: Psalms 100:18–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 40-43. Those nations first seduced and then oppressed them (compare Jud 1:34; 2:14; 3:30). Their apostasies ungratefully repaid God's many mercies till He finally abandoned them to punishment (Le 26:39).

entries.title: Psalms 100:22–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 44-46. If, as is probable, this Psalm was written at the time of the captivity, the writer now intimates the tokens of God's returning favor.

entries.title: Psalms 101:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 107 Psa 107:1-43. Although the general theme of this Psalm may have been suggested by God's special favor to the Israelites in their restoration from captivity, it must be regarded as an instructive celebration of God's praise for His merciful providence to all men in their various emergencies. Of these several are given--captivity and bondage, wanderings by land and sea, and famine; some as evidences of God's displeasure, and all the deliverances as evidence of His goodness and mercy to them who humbly seek Him. 1-2. This call for thankful praise is the burden or chorus (compare Psa 107:8, 15, &c.).
